Tafseer: Alif Lam Mim: We have already provided a comprehensive discussion on the 'Muqatta'at' (isolated letters) in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah. There, we also presented the perspective of the teacher-imam, Maulana Farahi (RA). No new information has come to our attention in this regard that warrants mention here.

The Beautiful Names mentioned in this verse have already been researched. Some were explained in Surah Al-Fatihah and others in Surah Al-Baqarah.

Secrets and Requirements of the Attributes of Life (Hayat) and Subsistence (Qayyumiyyah): As hinted in the introduction, it should be noted that the reasoning in this Surah is largely based on Divine attributes. Among these, the attributes of Life and Subsistence are taken up first. We discussed the secrets and realities of these two attributes in the context of Ayat al-Kursi. Repeating them here would be lengthy. In this context, the reference to these attributes serves to establish the necessity of the Divine Book. The subsequent verses point toward this reality. The detail of this summary is that since Allah, besides Whom there is no deity, is a Living God, it is inevitable that He sees and hears everything; our prayers and pleas reach Him, and our deeds and actions are in His sight. This necessitates that He accepts our prayers according to His wisdom and that He will one day reward or punish our deeds. Furthermore, it follows that servants should adopt a way of life that is pleasing to Him. This requires that servants seek to know which actions God likes and which He dislikes, so they may adopt the path of His obedience and guidance to achieve felicity and benefit from the fountain of true life. The People of the Book were well-aware of the expression "the Lord God, the Living God." This expression is used abundantly in the scriptures of their prophets. Wherever Allah's power, knowledge, and pride (Ghayrah) are expressed, the term "Living Lord" is generally used. Although the Christians worship a crucified god according to their presumption, they too were not unacquainted with the term "Living God." Therefore, it self-evidently goes against them that they hold a concept of a Living God on one hand while believing in his crucifixion on the other. Similarly, the attribute "Qayyum" (the Sustainer/Subsisting) is mentioned repeatedly in the prophets' scriptures. Allah being 'Qayyum' means that the heavens and the earth and everything between them exist by Allah's command and power. This attribute is among Allah's self-evident attributes in which one must believe both rationally and according to the prophets' scriptures. The Christians also claim faith in these scriptures, yet they believe in the divinity of Jesus (PBUH). If they are asked: when you yourself admit that Jesus felt hunger and thirst, was in need of food and water, and was unable to sustain his own existence without these things, then how could he be God, whereas according to your own prophets' words, God must be 'Qayyum'? Or if asked: when it is proven from your own Gospels that Jesus wept when facing hardships and distress, his heart was constricted, and he cried out on the cross, then how could he be the one who upholds and sustains the heavens and the earth? In response to these questions, they have nothing but stubbornness and hair-splitting. The reference to the attribute 'Qayyum' here is a very clear proof that Allah's being 'Qayyum' necessitates that He grants us guidance. The detail is that the One God, our Lord, just as He is our Creator and Giver of life, is also—as stated in Ayat al-Kursi—the Sustainer of His creation, and for this, He has created all kinds of means and resources. Since He provided such arrangements for our physical livelihood, how is it possible that He would not provide for our social life that which ensures our stability and survival, especially since this is the original purpose of our creation? Consequently, this very thing became the source of establishing justice ('Adl and Qist) and the foundation for the revelation of Sharia and commands from Allah, because without it, human nature could not achieve the degree of evolution inherent in its existence. This 'Qayyumiyyah' also required that the Sustaining and Designing God monitor when servants, using their autonomy and rebellion, try to completely erase His system of justice. Thus, the history of the world bears witness that until the advent of the Seal of the Prophets, Muhammad (PBUH), Allah sent countless prophets and messengers to keep the world established on justice, truth, and His straight path. After completing the religion through the Seal of the Prophets and securing the Divine Book from all kinds of interference, He ensured for this purpose that in every era, a group—no matter how small—would continue to arise within this Ummah who would remain firm on truth and justice and call others to establish them. This reality is clarified in Hadiths, and subtle hints toward it appear in this Surah, to which we will draw attention in suitable places, God willing.

The Necessity of the Quran's Revelation: We explained the research of the meaning of "Haqq" (Truth) at the start of Surah Al-Baqarah. Here, among its various meanings, "Decisive Word" (Qawl-e-Faisal) is intended—meaning that which decides disputes and differences. The Quran states in various places that the People of the Book created differences in the Book given to them, causing the original truth to be lost. To clarify this lost truth, Allah revealed the Quran so that people could benefit from Allah's original religion and emerge from the maze of disputes onto the main highway of religion. The detailed discussion on "Musaddiqan lima bayna yadayhi" (confirming what preceded it) also passed in Surah Al-Baqarah. One well-known meaning of the Quran being a 'confirmer' is that it fundamentally confirms all the correct teachings of previous scriptures, only refuting what was added by those who made alterations. This harmony between the Quran and other heavenly scriptures is clear testimony that they are all rays of the same Sun of Truth and reflections of the same source of light. Its second meaning is that the attributes of the Quran and its bearer are mentioned in the prophecies of previous scriptures; these prophecies were awaiting their referent, and they were confirmed by the revelation of the Quran and the advent of Muhammad (PBUH). This is a great testimony to the Quran being the truth, and simultaneously, it confirms those scriptures by showing that the prophecies they contained proved true. From this perspective, the Quran was most deserving of a welcome from those who claimed faith in previous scriptures, as the appearance of the Quran primarily elevated their own status; however, they denied it due to their stubbornness and obstinacy.

"And He revealed the Torah and the Gospel before as guidance for the people. And He revealed the Criterion (Furqan)..." This is the detail of the summary in the preceding segment, clarifying the necessity of the Quran's revelation. The meaning is that since Allah is the Ever-Living and Sustainer (Hayy and Qayyum), He revealed the Quran as a decisive word to guide our lives and keep us established on justice and equity. Before this, He revealed the Torah and the Gospel for people's guidance, but their followers created many differences in them through alteration and by forgetting parts of them, making it impossible to distinguish between truth and falsehood. This situation required that Allah reveal the Quran as a touchstone to distinguish between truth and falsehood. Thus, He revealed this Book. Now, those who deny this Book face a severe punishment from Allah, because such people are enemies of Allah's system of justice and equity, which is necessary for the welfare of His creation and for their success in both this world and the Hereafter. If Allah were to leave such people alone and not punish their enmity toward justice, it would mean He had left His world to destruction and had no interest in its survival—whereas His attribute, as mentioned later in this Surah, is "Standing firmly for justice" (Qa'iman bil-Qist). This 'Qawwamiyyah' (maintenance of justice) necessitates that He takes retribution from the enemies of equity and gives them their due punishment. He is 'Aziz' (Mighty), meaning Dominant and Powerful, not weak or helpless such that anyone could overpower Him; similarly, He is 'Dhu-ntiqam' (Possessor of Retribution), meaning He is proud (Ghayyur) regarding justice and equity, not indifferent or unfeeling such that He would accept their violation. It is the manifestation of these attributes that He has erased from the face of the earth those nations that destroyed the equity He established, after giving them respite to a certain limit; and whenever attempts were made to annihilate His laws and commands, He ensured they were renewed and refined. His Sunnah for the establishment and survival of justice and equity is expressed here as 'Intiqam' (Retribution).

Exegesis of Verses 5 and 6: Reasoning for the Establishment of Justice through Divine Attributes: The punishment mentioned in the previous verse for the opponents of the Divine Book—or in other words, the destroyers of justice and equity—is the proof for this. Let no one think that what happens in God's world remains hidden from Him. Nothing is hidden from Him, whether large or small, and whether it occurs on earth or in the heavens. God's knowledge encompasses everything and every place; and why shouldn't it, for He is the one who created everyone and He is the one who fashions forms within the wombs. Can the One who created and fashioned be unaware? "Does He not know, He who created?"

Following this, Tawheed and the attributes of 'Aziz' (Mighty) and 'Hakim' (Wise) are referenced. The purpose is that if a Being possessing all-encompassing knowledge did not ensure the establishment of justice and equity, it could only be because He either lacks honor and power, or He possesses them but has no concern for wisdom or purpose in His actions—as if it were merely a player's game where good or evil, oppression or justice, are irrelevant to Him. This thought is self-evidently void. Allah is both 'Aziz' and 'Hakim'; He possesses this power and His every action contains justice and wisdom, and simultaneously He is aware of everything. Therefore, how is it possible that He would not take retribution from those who deny Allah's Book, which He revealed to highlight anew the signs and landmarks of truth and justice in the world?

Summary of Verses 7 to 17: After clarifying the necessity of the Quran's revelation, it is now explained what kind of people are denying this Quran and what pretexts they are using for their denial. In this regard, it is stated that the path of denial has been adopted by those in whose hearts is deviation (Kaji) and in whose natures is a love for mischief (Fitna). Due to this taste, they have no attachment to the Quran's original teachings. They only look for things within it that they can use as a cover to create doubts and suspicions in people's hearts against the Quran, thereby avoiding the path of truth themselves and discouraging others as much as they can. Consequently, their entire interest lies not in the 'Muhkamat' (clear verses) but only in the 'Mutashabihat' (allegorical verses), because these relate to parables and similes of the afterlife. By hair-splitting and 'worm-eating' (excessive scrutiny) within them, they can derive some point that serves as a pretext for their own misguidance and can be used to plunge others into Fitna. Following this, mention is made of those who possess 'firmness in knowledge and reason' (Rasikhun fil-Ilm). Regarding them, it is stated that they value Allah's verses and benefit from His teachings and instructions. Like the 'Muhkamat', they also value the 'Mutashabihat' of the Quran; instead of making them a means for objection and mischief-seeking, they make them a means for increasing knowledge and believe in them with a sincere heart. If they find something beyond the grasp of their understanding, instead of pursuing its ultimate reality and essence and making it a Fitna, they refer it to Allah, as this attitude is the requirement of firmness in knowledge and faith. Furthermore, they possess firm conviction in the Hereafter. For this reason, to remain protected from the whisperings of devils, they constantly pray to Allah to keep them safe from falling into misguidance after having blessed them with the wealth of faith.

After this, an indication is made toward the causes of misguidance that led to the destruction of their previous co-religionists. Simultaneously, by referencing some recent events of Muslim success, the reality is clarified that if they do not take heed from these events, their end will be the same as those before them. Following this, the veils currently standing between them and the Quran are pointed out, along with the reality of their baselessness and transience: the things of this fleeting world that they are infatuated with, causing them to turn away from the Quran, are nothing more than a mirage. Instead of running after them, seek the blessings of eternal life and God's pleasure, the path to which opens through patience, truthfulness, obedience to God, spending in Allah's path, and seeking forgiveness. The Quran was revealed to call toward this very path. In this light, recite the following verses:

'Huwalladhi anzala 'alaykal-kitab' (It is He who has sent down to you the Book): This refers to the Mighty, Wise, Living, and Sustaining God mentioned above. The purpose here is to draw the addressee's attention to several things. First, to point to the great mercy manifested in the form of this Book. This is the manifestation of Allah's attributes of Life and Subsistence—that He revealed this Book to bless His servants with eternal life and establish them on the path of truth. The right of this great blessing is that servants recognize its value, believe in it, and through it attain eternal life and everlasting survival. Second, it contains an element of intimidation and threat for those who refute and belie it, because the God who revealed this Book is both Mighty ('Aziz) and Sustaining (Qayyum). His honor, wisdom, and 'Qayyumiyyah' necessitate that He punishes those who obstruct His law of truth and justice. Third, it points to the temperament of the Book itself: it is the speech of a Mighty and Wise God. Therefore, it is itself Mighty and Wise. Accordingly, the attribute 'Aziz' and 'Hakim' for the Quran appears in various places. From this, the point emerges that this Book is not for entanglement and hair-splitting, but for benefiting according to one's capacity and success. A person should take whatever benefit they can from the wisdoms of this infinite ocean, without expecting to encompass all its secrets and realities. Whatever points they understand, they should not make them a means for Fitna and doubts, but should refer them to Allah.

In this verse, a few words are used that hold the status of Quranic terminology. Since the exact meaning of the verse will not be clear until the meaning of these terms is well-clarified, we first explain them.

Meaning of 'Ayat Muhkamat': 'Ayat Muhkamat' refers to those verses of the Quran that consist of the absolute self-evident truths of the horizons and the souls, the recognized principles of good and evil, and the certain and definite matters of 'Ma'ruf' (the recognized good) and 'Munkar' (the recognized evil). Hearts accept them, and there is no condition for accepting them other than having a sound heart ('qalb-e-salim'). Every intellect testifies in their favor provided it is not covered by the veils of prejudice, emotions, and unnatural rationalizations. Every true religion is based on these 'Muhkamat'; therefore, they have been transmitted through 'Tawatur' (continuous transmission) from all heavenly religions and all prophets. Because their roots are extremely firm within human nature, the storms of doubts and suspicions fail to shake them; for this reason, the Quran described them as 'Muhkamat' (firm/clear).

Meaning of 'Umm al-Kitab': The 'Ayat Muhkamat' are defined as having the status of 'Umm al-Kitab' (the Mother/Foundation of the Book). This means that these 'Muhkamat' are the reference point and center for the rest of the Book; the entire discussion revolves around them, and all branches sprout from them. If any dispute or difference arises, its decision is made by testing it against their touchstone. Furthermore, they hold the rank that they are treated as principles from which issues are deduced, and those issues are relied upon just as the principles are relied upon.

Meaning of 'Ayat Mutashabihat': 'Mutashabihat' refers to those verses in which the Quran has described matters beyond the reach of our observations and information in a parabolic and symbolic manner. The fundamental reality to which these matters relate is itself clear and proven; the intellect can understand as much of it as is necessary for it to understand. However, since it relates to an unseen world, the Quran presents them in the style of parables and similes so that seekers of knowledge can benefit from them according to their capacity and refer their original form and reality to Divine knowledge. These matters relate to God's attributes and actions or the blessings and pains of the Hereafter. As much as is necessary for us to understand comes into our understanding, increasing our knowledge and certainty. But if we exceed our limit and try to grasp their original reality and form, this thing becomes a Fitna, and the result is only that a person tries to remove one thorn of doubt from their mind and ends up piercing themselves with countless thorns. So much so that in the quest for the unobtained, they waste their obtained wealth and belie extremely clear realities because their form and shape have not yet manifested before them. The Quran pointed to this: 'Rather, they have denied that which they encompass not in knowledge and whose interpretation has not yet come to them' (Yunus: 39).

Some Examples of 'Mutashabihat': We quote some examples of such 'Mutashabihat' from the Quran. In Surah Al-Muddaththir, the Quran presents the picture of the punishment of Hell in these words: 'I will drive him into Saqar. And what can make you know what is Saqar? It lets nothing remain and leaves nothing [unburned], Blackening the skins. Over it are nineteen [angels]' (Al-Muddaththir: 26-30). The punishment mentioned in this verse is a reality, and for one who believes in the law of retribution, there is no room for denial. As for its detail, since it relates to an unseen world, its original form cannot come into our grasp in any way. In such matters, the correct method is for a person to suffice with what is understood. What is not understood cannot be understood in this world; therefore, instead of pursuing it, one should refer it to God. People of sound nature do this, but those in whose hearts is deviation and in whose intellects is a twist, instead of adopting this method, pursue the reality of the 'Mutashabihat' and parables, causing themselves to fall into Fitnas and plunging many others like them into Fitnas. See, when the word 'nineteen' (tis'ata 'ashar) appeared in the aforementioned verse, the Quran described the reaction of the mischief-seekers: they pursued what it meant. If it meant angels, they raised the question: what is the secret in specifying the number nineteen? The Quran commented on their reaction in these words: 'And We have not made the keepers of the Fire except angels. And We have not made their number except as a trial for those who disbelieve - that those who were given the Scripture may be convinced and those who have believed may increase in faith and those who were given the Scripture and the believers may not doubt and that those in whose hearts is hypocrisy and the disbelievers may say, "What does Allah intend by this as an example?" Thus does Allah leave astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills. And none knows the soldiers of your Lord except Him. And mention of the Fire is not but a reminder to humanity' (Al-Muddaththir: 31). Similarly, in Surah Al-Baqarah, while mentioning the blessings of Paradise in a parabolic manner, it is stated that when the blessings of Paradise are presented before the people of Paradise, they will cry out in joy that these are the same blessings we were given a tour of in the Quran: 'They say, "This is what we were provided with before." And they are given things in resemblance [mutashabihan]' (Al-Baqarah: 25). Meaning: the mention of the blessings of Paradise that occurred in the Quran in the guise of parables and 'Mutashabihat' benefits the believers by allowing them to take a tour of Paradise while sitting in the world. But regarding the attitude of the mischief-makers and the seekers of misguidance toward these same parables and 'Mutashabihat', the Quran stated in these words: 'Indeed, Allah is not timid to present an example - that of a mosquito or what is smaller than it. And those who have believed know that it is the truth from their Lord. But as for those who disbelieve, they say, "What did Allah intend by this as an example?" He misleads many thereby and guides many thereby. And He misleads not except the defiantly disobedient' (Al-Baqarah: 26). From this detail, it is known that 'Ayat Mutashabihat' refers to those verses of the Quran in which either the description of some blessing or punishment of the Hereafter has occurred in a parabolic and symbolic style, or some point regarding God's attributes and actions is presented in a parabolic style—for example, God breathing His spirit into Adam or creating Jesus (PBUH) without a father, etc. From such verses, as mentioned above, the knowledge and faith of believers increase, but those with a love for Fitna in their natures create many Fitnas by hair-splitting within them.

Meaning of 'Ta'wil': The word 'Ta'wil' is also used in a specific sense in this verse. Here it refers to the ultimate reality and the actual form of the aforementioned type of described thing. The sense in which this word is used here is the same sense in which it is used in Surah Yusuf: 'He said, "O my father, this is the reality [ta'wil] of my vision of before. My Lord has made it a reality"' (Yusuf: 100).

Some Warnings regarding 'Muhkamat' and 'Mutashabihat': A few points are worth mentioning here as warnings. Through them, all confusions on this path will be removed, God willing. First, the style of speech in this verse is not one of 'Hasr' (exclusivity). Therefore, one should not presume that the verses of the Quran are divided into only two categories: 'Muhkamat' and 'Mutashabihat'. Here, only these two categories are mentioned as contrasting types, and the purpose of their mention is merely to highlight the difference in taste between the seekers of Fitna and the seekers of guidance: those with Fitna-seeking natures have all their interest in the 'Mutashabihat' so that food can be provided for their taste of mischief-seeking. In contrast, those who seek knowledge and gnosis, and in whom reality-perception is dominant, have their primary interest in the 'Muhkamat'. As for the 'Mutashabihat', they benefit from the part they understand, and they do not pursue the discovery of the form and shape of the part they do not understand, but refer it to God. Because their feet become very firm in knowledge due to the 'Muhkamat', such things do not unsettle them. There are other types of verses in the Quran besides these two, but since the purpose here was not to encompass all types of Quranic verses, their mention was not necessary. For example: the stories (Qisas) of the Quran, the parables (Amthal) of the Quran, allusions, and hints. these things are neither of the rank and status of 'Umm al-Kitab' nor is it correct to place them in the category of 'Mutashabihat' regarding whose 'Ta'wil' reflection is prohibited. Second, this reality must be remembered: the verses of the Quran being 'Muhkam' or 'Mutashabih' is absolutely not in terms of words, but only in terms of meaning. The Quran, in its words and its language, is entirely in 'Arabicun Mubin' (clear Arabic). The differences that have occurred in the interpretation of words have generally happened due to three causes: either there was a shortcoming in reflection and research, or the improper prejudice of some wrong creed was the cause, or ignorance of the Arabic language was the reason. Obviously, where a confusion has arisen due to such a cause, reflection and thought upon it must occur in the light of the well-known and recognized rules and regulations of the Arabic language; this is not among the things upon which reflection is prohibited. Third, whether they are 'Mutashabihat' or 'Muhkamat', both these categories are distinct and known in the Quran. It is not the case, as some theologians (Mutakallimun) have presumed, that these two are indistinguishable; nor is it the case that the indication of words toward their meanings is something ambiguous and doubtful. Those who have understood it thus have understood it completely wrongly. The first of these points is explicitly wrong, and the second is extremely vague, leading to despair regarding the Quran altogether—even though Allah Almighty revealed the Quran as Light and Proof. Regarding the matters belonging to the world of the unseen, God has given news to the extent of our need; only the part that has been veiled from us—its 'Ta'wil' (ultimate reality) is behind the veil of the hidden. Fourth, the Quran has intended a specific meaning for 'Muhkam' and 'Mutashabih' here, which is separate to an extent from their literal meaning. These words are used in other places in the Quran where their literal meanings are intended. For example, 'Muhkam' refers to speech that is comprehensive, clear, and concise. In this case, its opposite word is 'Mufassal' (detailed). For example: 'A Book whose verses are perfected [uhkimat], then explained in detail [fussilat] from one who is Wise and Acquainted' (Hud: 1). The Sunnah of Allah has been that in the beginning, He revealed His teachings and instructions in the form of 'Muhkam' (firm/concise) statements so they could be well-embedded in the mind and memory and feel light for both the heart and tongue; then later, Allah Almighty provided their detail through revelation or through the Sunnah of the Prophet (PBUH). Similarly, there is a general meaning of 'Mutashabih', which is: a thing that is similar, harmonious, and of the same color as another; in this sense, the entire Quran is 'Mutashabih'. Consequently, from this aspect, the Quran has been called 'Mutashabih': 'a Book [that is] consistent [mutashabihan] and repeated' (Az-Zumar: 23). Fifth, just as the Quran consists of both 'Muhkamat' and 'Mutashabihat' verses, the signs in the souls and the horizons also consist of both 'Muhkamat' and 'Mutashabihat'. Regarding them too, the attitude of the people of knowledge and the people of deviation (Zaygh) is the same as mentioned above: those with firmness in their mind and thought obtain tranquility and certainty from the 'Muhkamat', and instead of being trapped in doubts and suspicions from the 'Mutashabihat', they refer them to God's knowledge and wisdom and admit the shortcoming of their own knowledge. Conversely, those in whose hearts is deviation make these 'Mutashabihat' a means for their own and others' misguidance. We will discuss this issue in detail, God willing, in the exegesis of Surah Al-Kahf. The event of the Battle of Uhud, as we hinted in the introductory discussion, also holds the nature of a 'Mutashabih' event; consequently, the revelation of this great verse after that war was to lift the veil from a very great reality of this universe. We hinted above that just as the Battle of Badr was a 'Day of Criterion' (Yawm al-Furqan) between truth and falsehood—by which the hearts of the believers were satisfied with Allah's promises and which became a 'Muhkam' sign to complete Allah's proof over the people of Kufr—similarly, the status of the Battle of Uhud is that of a 'Mutashabih' sign. This is because in it, falsehood apparently gained dominance over the truth, causing the disbelievers to presume that success and failure in war relate only to strategies and means/resources, and that God has no role in it nor does it have any connection to truth and falsehood. Obviously, this was a severe type of misunderstanding, the removal of which was extremely necessary; thus, when the appropriate time for its removal arrived, Allah Almighty removed it in this Surah, and this is a great verse in that series.

The Reality of 'Zaygh': The word 'Zaygh' that appeared in this verse—its reality should also be briefly understood. 'Zaygh' originally means to lean, incline, and deviate. This word simultaneously carries two meanings: one, deviation (kaji), and second, falling (suqut); something standing, when it leans, becomes close to falling. This state is the opposite of that 'Rasukh' (firmness) described for the 'Rasikhun fil-Ilm' in this verse. This 'Zaygh' is a general disease of the people of misguidance, but the People of the Book have been afflicted with this disease with the greatest intensity. The history of the Jews bears witness that they were afflicted with this disease from the beginning. And this aspect of their 'Zaygh' is particularly grave: that they were afflicted with it in the presence of their Prophet. Consequently, this is why they were afflicted with God's wrath because of it. This is mentioned in the Quran. In Surah As-Saff, it is mentioned thus: 'And [remember] when Moses said to his people, "O my people, why do you harm me while you certainly know that I am the messenger of Allah to you?" And when they deviated [zagu], Allah caused their hearts to deviate [azaghallahu qulubahum]. And Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people' (As-Saff: 5). These are the same Jews who, in explaining the reality of 'Kalimatullah' and other such terms, created philosophical hair-splittings to make them a maze, opening paths of misguidance for the Christians and causing them to fall into the creed of the divinity of Jesus. Later, this misguidance of the Christians was further increased by the imitation of idol-worshipping nations, and then gradually they became so distant from the truth that their link with it was severed and they fell into explicit Kufr. Thus, the Quran made this clarification regarding them: 'They have certainly disbelieved who say, "Allah is the Messiah, the son of Mary"' (Al-Ma'idah: 72).

The Difference between the Misguidance of Jews and Christians: The difference in the nature of the misguidance of Jews and Christians is simply that the Jews' misguidance is primarily practical and the Christians' is creedal. Due to this difference, their attitude in opposing the truth was also different from each other. The Jews opposed the Quran despite knowing it to be the truth. The Christians, just as they had been misled by falling into the 'Mutashabihat' of the Torah and Gospel, similarly had all their interest in the Quran only in the 'Mutashabihat'. By hair-splitting within them, they created various Fitnas and thus arranged for their own misguidance and misled others. They neither took interest in the 'Muhkamat' of the Quran themselves nor allowed those over whom they had influence to take interest. In short, regarding the 'Zaygh' of the heart and sight and the following of 'Mutashabihat', both Jews and Christians were on the same level; this disease was common to them, but their 'tastes' were slightly different. The Jews were more inclined toward 'seeking Fitna' (ibtigha' al-fitna) and the Christians toward 'seeking interpretation' (ibtigha' al-ta'wil). Since these misguidances are common to all the misguided of the world, the Quran kept the style of expression general to allow for vastness in the discourse, without specifying Jews and Christians. But those with a taste for the Quran know that the hint is toward them. This same style is in Surah Al-Fatihah. In it too, the words 'maghdhubi 'alayhim' (those under wrath) and 'dhallin' (those astray), though general—and due to being general, they have acquired great vastness—nonetheless have a specific hint toward the Jews and Christians.

An Example of Misguidance from 'Mutashabihat': It will be useful to clarify with an example the type of misguidance the Christians fell into due to following 'Mutashabihat'. The Quran and the Gospel both agree that Jesus is 'Kalimatullah' (the Word of Allah). The meaning of 'Kalimatullah' is perfectly clear: it is an expression for 'Amr' (command) and 'Hukm' (decree). Since the birth of Jesus (PBUH) occurred contrary to the general code of nature, Allah Almighty described him as His 'Word'—meaning his birth occurred from Allah Almighty saying the word 'Kun' (Be); this was an expression of the reality that the original thing for the occurrence of anything is Allah Almighty's command. Means/causes are merely the veil of the manifest. This point is described with extreme clarity in the Quran and contains no twists or turns that could create confusion in a clear-minded person. The Quran stated in unambiguous words: 'Indeed, the example of Jesus to Allah is like that of Adam. He created him from dust; then He said to him, "Be," and he was' (Al Imran: 59). Meaning: He made Adam living and speaking through the word 'Kun'. This same thing is described elsewhere as 'breathing of the spirit' (nafkh-e-ruh). Exactly the same is the case of Jesus (PBUH). The form of the alteration the Christians made in this clear point was: when they encountered idol-worshipping nations and religious debates began with them, those nations started objecting to them: "You worship a crucified god; we are a thousand times superior to you because we worship heavenly deities." To escape this objection of their rivals, the Christians tried to mold their creed into the same mold as theirs. For this purpose, they claimed that the Messiah is the 'Son of God' (Ibn Allah), not a creation. In the decoration of this new creed of theirs, they took material on one hand from the thought and philosophy of the Greeks, Magians, and Hindus, and on the other hand obtained guidance from the scholasticism (Ilm al-Kalam) of the Jewish theologians who were the product of the Jews' final period and who not only believed in means and intermediaries between the Creator and the creation but gave them the status of independent beings and called them 'Kalimatullah'. The Christians adopted exactly this creed for Jesus (PBUH). For some time, the matter remained at this limit, but gradually misguidance gave birth to more misguidance, and they declared him the 'equal' (kufu) of God, from His very essence, and with Him from eternity. And then, to support this creed, they even introduced some passages into the beginning of the Gospel of John through the back door of alteration, so that a 'home-grown' proof could be provided for this creed imported from the outside.

There is a Stop (Waqf) at 'wa ma ya'lamu...': 'wa ma ya'lamu ta'wilahu illallah' (And no one knows its ultimate reality except Allah): From the details above, it has automatically become clear that there is a stop here. This is the position of the majority of the Ahl al-Sunnah and is reported from Ibn Abbas, Aisha, Ali, Hasan, Malik bin Anas, Kisa'i, and Farra'. However, the Shi'a and some theologians favor 'Wasl' (continuation) here. According to them, the 'Ta'wil' of the 'Mutashabihat' is known to the 'Rasikhun fil-Ilm' (those firm in knowledge) besides Allah. The reason for this, as far as the Shi'a are concerned, is that these people hold the creed regarding their Imams that they have knowledge of everything. As for the other people who favor this, the reason is that they take 'Ta'wil' to mean 'meaning' (ma'na), even though the context of the verse is against this. This has been clarified above. Although after this explanation of the words and components of the verse, the correct interpretation of the verse has come forward on its own, for further satisfaction in view of its importance, we present its clear meaning. The meaning is: the same God who is both Mighty and Wise, who is Living and Sustaining—He revealed the Torah and the Gospel; then when confusion was created in them, His wisdom and 'Qayyumiyyah' necessitated that He reveal this Quran so that truth and falsehood could be distinguished through it; so those who obstruct it should remember that the Mighty God will not leave the truth oppressed—He will certainly take retribution for it. Following this, He clarified that the original reason the People of the Book are opposing this 'Furqan' (Criterion) is not that there is something in the Book itself causing them alarm; rather, the original reason is that there is deviation in their own hearts. Due to this deviation, they have no interest in the 'Muhkamat' of this Book—which hold the status of the original Book and upon which all its teachings and its entire wisdom and philosophy are based. If they have interest, it is only in its 'Mutashabihat' verses in which something is told in a parabolic and symbolic style. Due to the corruption of their nature, they pursue these and try to discover their form and reality for the sake of creating disorder and Fitna, even though their original reality is known to no one except Allah Almighty. To the extent that their knowledge is necessary, God has opened it; one should suffice with that much and refer the matter of their original reality to Allah; they will be revealed on the Day they manifest. The method of those firm in knowledge regarding 'Mutashabihat' is this: they consider both 'Muhkamat' and 'Mutashabihat' as gifts from their Lord and believe in both equally. Due to the firmness of their knowledge, they are aware of the secret that the purpose of Allah's verses is to remind the servants, and since they possess reason, they take the benefit from them that should be taken; they do not waste their time in some unsuccessful and futile effort to arrange the causes of their own loss. The Sunnah of Allah Almighty is that only those benefit from His verses who possess reason and use that reason correctly.

Most of these contents are derived from the insights of the teacher-imam (RA); only the explanation of some points is from my side; therefore, attribute the correct points to Maulana, and if there is any flaw, the responsibility lies solely on my head.

Exegesis of Verses 8 and 9: There is no linguistic or grammatical difficulty in these two verses. The research of the word 'Zaygh' passed above. This is the prayer of the 'Rasikhun fil-Ilm' (those firm in knowledge), expressing that they are not so indifferent regarding their religion as to unnecessarily invite doubts and suspicions and put their faith and Islam at risk; rather, they constantly pray to their Lord for the safety of their faith—that their firm steps in religion do not falter, and when the onslaught of Fitnas occurs, the Bestowing God (Wahhab) sends them spiritual reinforcement from Himself to serve as a means for their steadfastness. The second verse expresses the certainty these 'Rasikhun' possess regarding the Hereafter. The context here indicates that this certainty is the original guardian of both the heart and the reason. Without it, nothing can stop the human mind and thought from aimless wandering. Such a person considers life an extremely easy game and is ready to gamble it on every move; but those in whom the certainty of the Hereafter is deeply embedded take every step with caution and place it very carefully. This caution always keeps them established on the straight path.

Exegesis of Verses 10 and 11: The Original Obstacle to the Path of Truth: 'Innal-ladhina kafaru' here refers, as passed in verse 4, to the deniers of the Quran. Regarding them, it was stated that this wealth and children—the love of which has today become an obstacle for them in accepting a truth clearer than the sun—will not be able to save them from God's grasp. For this reason, in their love, one should not forget that God whose rights are prior to all and whose grasp is also irresistible. Wealth and children are mentioned here as the original veil to accepting the truth. Their detailed mention is coming in verse 14. In reality, it is their love that becomes an obstacle for a human in accepting the truth, but the human avoids admitting the original reality and seeks some excuses for their interests that can veil their original disease. The Quran has exposed the original internal disease of these people: it is in reality the love of wealth and children that is stopping them from bowing before the truths presented by the Quran, but to hide this, they try to create some objections and doubts from within the 'Mutashabihat' so that their worldliness is not unveiled. It is a common weakness of man that he avoids a reality due to some weakness of his soul but makes a display that gives the addressee the impression that there are actually some reasons, causes, objections, and doubts for his avoidance. Following this, it was stated that their way is exactly the same way adopted before them by Pharaoh and his people and other nations who belied God's messengers. They too, deliberately and purely out of love for the world, considered God's signs and the signs He presented as some magic and sorcery; therefore, they were not accepting them. Finally, Allah Almighty seized them for their sins, and once He seized them, there was no one who could save them from God's grasp. The phrase 'Shadidul-'Iqab' (Severe in Retribution) contains two meanings. One, that the punishment a human receives from God is the reaction to the human's own deeds; second, that just as the results of God's physical laws are impartial and mandatory, similarly the results of God's moral laws are impartial and mandatory—when the stage for their manifestation comes, they will manifest with such impartiality and with such certainty and power that no one will be able to escape them nor will anyone be able to save [others] from them.

Threat of Punishment to the Deniers of the Quran: Now this is a clear threat to all deniers of the Quran: these conspiracies you are plotting against the Quran are not going to be successful; you will suffer defeat at the hands of the bearers of the Quran, and these means and resources of yours in which you take great pride, and this numerical majority in which you have great trust—these things will prove to be of no use at all. You will be overcome in the world and driven toward Hell in the Hereafter; and do not consider this Hell a light thing—it is a wretched resting place. This warning was necessary because a human, first of all, cannot correctly estimate something they haven't seen, and even if they do estimate it to an extent, they become negligent and indifferent toward respecting it due to their inherent heedlessness.

Invitation to take Heed from the Battle of Badr: The proof for the aforementioned claim is presented from an event that had not occurred long ago. The hint is toward the event of Badr. The meaning is: in the struggle currently taking place between the bearers of the Quran and its opponents, the defeat will ultimately be for the opponents. A sign of the truthfulness of this prophecy exists in the battle that has already occurred between the Quraysh and the Muslims. In that battle, one group rose to elevate the Word of Allah, and the other—that of the disbelievers—to elevate the word of Satan. Although the disbelievers numbered over a thousand and the Muslims were only three hundred and thirteen, when the time for the encounter came, the disbelievers saw the Muslims as twice their own number with their own eyes. This happened due to Allah Almighty's special support and help, and victory or defeat is not originally related to numerical majority or minority but only to Allah Almighty's support and help, and this support and help are obtained by those who rise to elevate His Word. Those who have eyes can see the map of the future in this event: on what decision this conflict of truth and falsehood is finally going to end. In the event of Badr, there was a sign of the dominance of truth for all those groups of disbelievers who were at the forefront of opposing the Quran and Islam at that time. At that time, three groups—Jews, Christians, and Quraysh—were directly opposing Islam; now see how Allah Almighty made the battle of Badr a sign to open the eyes of these three groups.

A Sign for the Jews: As far as the Jews are concerned, we mentioned in Surah Al-Baqarah, in the series of the war of Talut and Jalut, that this war was exactly a mirror of the Battle of Badr in terms of its purpose, its characteristics, its map, and the number of Talut's army. Just as the Muslims were expelled from their homes and deprived of their Qibla, the Children of Israel were also evicted from their homes and deprived of their Qibla (the Ark). Just as there were three hundred and thirteen men with the Holy Prophet (PBUH), there were exactly the same number with Talut, as known from the traditions of the Children of Israel. Just as the Prophet (PBUH) tested his army's morale before the battle, Talut also tested his companions' resolve and discipline in a specific way. Then, just as Allah Almighty blessed the Holy Prophet (PBUH) with His support and help in this war—such that the Muslims achieved victory in extremely unfavorable and adverse circumstances and the great leaders of the Quraysh were killed—similarly, Allah Almighty helped Talut and his companions, and the small number of his companions prevailed over the extremely large number of enemies, and the famous Philistine commander was laid low by Prophet David's sling. This amazing similarity between the two wars was perfectly clear to the Jews. They had seen the entire map of one in their scriptures and had seen the entire event of the other with their own eyes. For this reason, it was not difficult for them to estimate that the field of Badr was not a fight of weapons but a war of truth and falsehood, and not a conflict of humans and humans but a war of angels and devils. Consequently, as we will clarify in the Tafseer of Surah Al-Anfal, there is a hint in the Quran that this reality was fully manifest to the Jews, but despite this, they did not value this sign of the Prophet of the Final Age (PBUH) and continued to oppose Islam. Similarly, for the Christians too, there was a very great sign of the Holy Prophet's (PBUH) truthfulness in this war. In the Revelations of John, there is a vision that when the Promised Prophet (the Seal of the Prophets, PBUH) appears, he will perform Jihad with the power of truth and in his retinue will be an army of Cherubim. This prophecy manifested at Badr in such a way that people saw the angels fighting the disbelievers with their own eyes. If after this sign the Christians still remained stuck in the cycles of 'Mutashabihat' and were not blessed with the success of admitting the truth, what can it be called other than their wretchedness? For the Quraysh, this war was an irrefutable testimony to the truth of the Quran and Islam even according to their own demand. They themselves had very openly declared this war a touchstone for distinguishing between truth and falsehood. Their own announcement was that whoever wins in this war will be considered on the truth, and whoever is defeated, on falsehood. Abu Jahl had made this prayer right in the battlefield: "O Allah, whichever of the two parties has become the greatest severer of the ties of kinship, crush him tomorrow morning." Regarding this war, both the Quran and the Holy Prophet (PBUH) had made extremely unambiguous prophecies that were fulfilled letter by letter. The Holy Prophet (PBUH) had even determined the specific spots where the specific leaders of the Quraysh would be killed, and at the end of the war, people saw that every word of the Prophet proved true. Consequently, for these reasons, the Quran described the Battle of Badr as 'Furqan' (the Criterion), meaning it established such a distinction between truth and falsehood that the supporters of Islam obtained proof of being on the truth and Allah's proof was completed over its opponents.

A Style of Omission (Hadhf): In the verse, the quality of the group of believers is stated as "this [one] was fighting in the path of Allah," but regarding the disbelievers, there is no explicit statement as to whose path they were fighting in. The reason for this lack of explicit statement is that this well-known style of the Arabic language is used much in the Quran: out of two contrasting points, one contrast is omitted for the sake of brevity because the mentioned one itself guides toward the omitted one. Here, according to this style, if the full point were unfolded, it would be: 'fi'atun mu'minatun tuqatilu fi sabilillahi wa ukhra kafiratun tuqatilu fi sabil-it-taghut'. From the first part of the sentence, the word 'mu'minatun' (believing) was omitted, and from the second, 'fi sabil-it-taghut' (in the path of Taghut) was omitted, because the attribute 'kafiratun' (disbelieving) in the second part points to 'mu'minatun' in the first, and the reference to 'fi sabilillah' in the first points to the need for 'fi sabil-it-taghut' in the second. This style of omission is used much in the Holy Quran; if it is not clear, the original force of the speech is not understood. This is a branch of 'Ijaz' (brevity), and 'Ijaz' is the soul of eloquence.

Who saw whom as double? In 'yarawnahum mithlayhim', the recitation (qira'at) of Nafi' is 'tarawnahum' (you see them), but this recitation is in our view by way of exegesis. His meaning is that this verb is for the addressees, i.e., the disbelievers. Meaning: O disbelievers! Your state was that you saw the Muslims as twice your own number. Through this exegesis of his, he clarified the reality that the matter of seeing the opponent as double did not happen to the Muslims but happened to the disbelievers. This interpretation of Nafi' seems very correct because there is an explicit statement in the verse that Allah Almighty made this thing an 'Ayah' (sign) for the disbelievers, and He also specifically stated that they saw this sign with the eyes of the head. If the event were the opposite—the Muslims seeing the disbelievers as double—then what sign was there in that for the disbelievers, and why would this sign be mentioned addressing them?

Answer to a Question: A question might arise for some people here. It is that in Surah Al-Anfal, where the event of the Battle of Badr is described, it seems that the disbelievers were shown as few in the eyes of the Muslims, and the Muslims were shown as few in the eyes of the disbelievers. This seems to go against the aforementioned interpretation of the verse. But the answer to this question in our view is that this matter of showing as few and showing as many manifested in two different stages in two different forms. Before entering the battlefield, undoubtedly the situation remained such that the Muslims felt the number of disbelievers was ordinary, and the disbelievers felt the Muslims were in an extremely insignificant position. But after practically entering the battlefield and the actual start of the war, the situation suddenly changed. Now when the disbelievers cast a glance at the battlefield, they saw that the map was different; due to the participation of the angels, the Muslims' army gained such superiority that it began to appear double to the disbelievers' eyes. From the Quran's description, it is known that this manifestation in two different stages was a God-wrought matter, and the purpose was that Allah Almighty should clash truth and falsehood against each other and, by manifesting His unseen support and help for the aid of truth, complete His proof over the opponents of truth. Consequently, under this wisdom, He showed the disbelievers as few in the eyes of the Muslims and the Muslims as few in the eyes of the disbelievers in the initial stage so that neither party would fear clashing with the other. But when the two clashed and the battlefield became heated, Allah Almighty helped the Muslims through the angels, and the disbelievers became completely overawed upon seeing the map of the battlefield. We quote the relevant verses of Surah Al-Anfal here so that the difference between the two occasions and the wisdom and benefit of both come forward. It was stated: '[Remember] when you were on the near side of the valley, and they were on the farther side, and the caravan was lower [in position] than you. If you had made an appointment [to meet], you would have missed the appointment. But [it was] so that Allah might accomplish a matter already destined - that those who were to perish [in disbelief] might perish upon evidence and those who were to live [in faith] might live upon evidence. And indeed, Allah is Hearing and Knowing. [Remember, O Muhammad], when Allah showed them to you in your dream as few; and if He had shown them to you as many, you [believers] would have lost courage and would have disputed in the matter [of whether to fight], but Allah saved [you from that]. Indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts. And [remember] when He showed them to you, when you met, as few in your eyes, and He made you [appear] as few in their eyes so that Allah might accomplish a matter already destined. And to Allah are [all] matters returned' (Al-Anfal: 42-44). From this detail, it became clear that there is a difference of occasion and place between the verse under discussion and the verses of Al-Anfal. The occasion mentioned in Al-Anfal is, as we mentioned, before the start of the war. And in the verse under discussion, it is after the start of the war, when Divine support has manifested in the form of the angels' reinforcement. In this way, there is perfect harmony between these two verses. It should also be noted that in Al-Anfal too, there is a hint that after the war started, the disbelievers saw the map of the battlefield as something else, and this observation lowered their morale. The detail of these hints will come in its place. There we will also clarify that although the Jews were secretly involved in the conspiracy of bringing the Quraysh up against the Muslims, they too lost heart upon seeing the map of Badr. 'wallahu yu'ayyidu bi-nasrihi may-yasha'' (And Allah supports with His victory whom He wills) points to the fact that Allah Almighty can bless whomever He wills with His support and victory; no one can catch His hand. And for the one He blesses with His support and victory, the question of majority or minority does not arise. Allah Almighty, if He wills, can make a drop into a river and a grain of sand into a sun. How many weak and powerless groups has He granted victory over armies like clouds? The original source-control of victory and defeat is in His hand alone.

'inna fi dhalika la-'ibratal-li-ulil-abshar': Meaning of 'Ibrat': 'Ibrat' means to cross over from one reality to another reality. The greatest difference between a person of vision and a dullard is that one does not see beyond their nose, but for the other, a minor sign, a slight warning, and a cursory hint opens a whole volume of realities. If one door opens for them, they get the key to open other doors. The Quran calls such people "Ulu-l-Absar" (People of Vision) because in their eyes, along with sight, there is the light of insight which possesses the capability to observe the whole in the part and the Tigris in a drop.

The Reality of 'Tazyin' (Beautification): The word 'shahawat' (desires) here is in the sense of 'mushtahayat', meaning 'liked things.' Wealth, children, and spouses are among the things that are naturally liked by humans and they should be liked. This is because these things are among the requirements for their individual and species survival. But here, the mere desire for them is not under discussion; rather, their 'beautification' (tazyin) is mentioned. The meaning of 'tazyin' is that a thing becomes so embedded in the eyes that a person begins to see everything in its color as an effect. So much so that it becomes impossible for them to see anything apart from it. They designate it as the scale and touchstone for weighing and testing everything. The dominance of the desire for a thing to this degree is obviously against the intent of the Creator of nature. It is from this that those immoderations manifest in life which turn a human away from the straight path of nature and Sharia. This is a state of illness produced from a lack of insight and a lack of respect for Divine limits—or in other words, a lack of Taqwa—and the primary role in it is of the soul and Satan. The soul exceeds natural limits in its wants, then Satan places such a captivating plating over these wants that the person's gaze does not turn toward any other direction. For this reason, the Quran has attributed this 'tazyin' to devils in other places. 

Meaning of 'Qintar' and 'Muqantarah': 'Qintar' means a vast amount of wealth. The attribute 'Muqantarah' is used with it in the same way that constructions like 'laylun alyal' (a most dark night) or 'zhillun zhalil' (a most shady shade) are used in Arabic. 

Meaning of 'Musawwamah': From 'sawmah', meaning a mark. 'Musawwamah' means marked. Since noble and fine horses are generally marked, this word became well-known as an expression for nobility and excellence.

'Lin-nas' refers to a specific group: Although the word 'lin-nas' (for the people) in the verse is apparently general, it refers to a specific group. This is the same type of usage as when the word 'insan' (man) is used in the Quran, and the specific group whose circumstances are under discussion in that place is intended. here, the context proves that those people are intended who are devoid of insight and Taqwa; therefore, they are pouncing upon the liked things of the world and do not even lift an eye toward the high values to which the Quran is drawing attention. 

In the description of the soul's liked things, a specific order is to be kept in mind. First, family and children are mentioned because they hold the highest place in terms of love; the love for other things is originally subordinate to them—in fact, mostly for them. After that, wealth is mentioned, and within wealth, the mention of gold precedes other currencies due to its high value. Among equipment, horses are mentioned first because the Arabs gave the greatest importance to the horse from the viewpoints of adornment, pride, and defense. After that, livestock are mentioned because before the appearance of civilization, in the era of nomadism, livelihood depended mostly on them. Finally, fields and gardens are mentioned because their importance began after entering the era of civilization when humans adopted the residence of cities and villages. Within the short phrase 'dhalika mata'ul-hayatid-dunya' (that is the enjoyment of worldly life), a world of meanings is hidden. It contains a hint toward the transience of this world, toward the reality-lessness of these things, and toward the foolishness of being infatuated with the transient pleasures of this fleeting world in comparison to an eternal world. From the aspect of structure, this verse is effectively an explanation of the subject of verse 10. In it, the clarification was made that the original thing inciting people to oppose the Quran is indeed the love of this world and the greed for its liked things; but to keep this disease hidden, they create and spread various types of doubts, suspicions, and objections so as to create a justification for this avoidance of theirs.

Invitation to the Addressees to Change their Angle of Vision: This is an invitation to the addressees to change their angle of vision, because without changing it, the quest for Quranic values cannot be produced in the heart. According to the Quran, the human's original disease is their narrow-mindedness and low-spiritedness. They have considered this short life of the world as the total life. Due to which their entire running and striving remains limited to the liked and sought things of this world, even though the original life is the life of the Hereafter, which is eternal and never-fading. If a human adopts the path of Taqwa in the quest for that life—meaning they observe the limits of Halal and Haram God has appointed regarding this world—then they will receive those blessings in the eternal life of the Hereafter which they cannot even imagine today. Among the blessings, 'pure spouses' (azwajun mutahharah) are specifically mentioned. We discussed this term under verse 25 of Al-Baqarah. Here, its mention specifically among the blessings of Paradise occurred because it became clear in verse 14 above that at the top of the list of the world's liked things are family and children; therefore, in the blessings of Paradise too, their mention was made specifically. 'Ridwan' means God's pleasure and satisfaction, but in the Quran, this word is generally used as a comprehensive expression for the blessings of Paradise. When it is mentioned, it is as if every blessing is mentioned: both that for which a garment of expression exists and that which is superior to every presumption, thought, analogy, and delusion.

'Basirun bil-'ibad' contains both a threat and a consolation: 'wallahu basirun bil-'ibad' (And Allah is Seeing of [His] servants) can be used in both types of occasions: threat and consolation. Here, the context proves it is in the place of consolation. Meaning: those who adopt the path of Taqwa in this worldly life for the sake of the Hereafter—Allah Almighty is aware of all those sacrifices and hardships. He will give them the full reward for all those sacrifices; not a single sacrifice of theirs will go to waste.

'Alladhina yaquluna...' (Those who say...) is an appositive (badal) for 'lilladhinat-taqaw' (for those who fear Allah) in the previous verse. This detail points toward those people who, by accepting this call of the Quran, withdrew from the liked things that make one heedless of the Hereafter, and repenting from the self-forgetfulness of their previous life, entered into the pure life of faith and righteous action. Simultaneously, it contains a silent preaching for those who were hesitating to come onto this path and were creating various types of pretexts for this hesitation of theirs.

The Character of the Bearers of the Quran: 'As-sabirina...' (The patient...). This is a second appositive (badal). Through this, the moral attributes with which this pure-natured group is characterized have been clarified, and from this, it also becomes clear what components make up the character and conduct necessary for being a bearer of the Quran. This verse effectively presents the character and conduct that is the absolute opposite of what was highlighted in the verse 'zuyyina lin-nasi hubbush-shahawat' above. Here, only five attributes are described: patience (Sabr), truthfulness (Sidq), devoutness (Qunut), spending (Infaq), and seeking forgiveness (Istighfar). 

The reality of patience is resolve and steadfastness on the truth in all kinds of circumstances, mild and harsh. Poverty, illness, calamity, opposition, war—in short, whatever kind of circumstances a person has to face, they should endure them with resolve and courage, face them, try to fulfill their duty, and remain firm on the stance of truth to the extent of their possibility. They should save the heart from despair and anxiety, the tongue from complaining against destiny, and their neck from bowing before any falsehood. A large part of religion is established upon this patience. If a person does not possess this attribute, then any greed, any temptation, or any trial can turn them away from the truth and make them prostrate before falsehood. Whoever wants to walk on the path of truth and is desirous of remaining established upon it must first produce the attribute of patience within themselves. For facing resistances—and on this path there is a confrontation with resistances at every step—the servant has this as their primary weapon. From the viewpoint of the philosophy of religion, religion is half gratitude and half patience. But practical experience bears witness that if a person lacks patience, the right of gratitude too cannot be fulfilled. Since the address here is to those being invited to climb the greatest height of truth, the aspect of their patience was highlighted first in the character of the people presented as a model before them.

'Sidq' (truthfulness) in its original reality is for something to be perfectly in accord with the event/fact. Its soul is firmness and solidity; if the joints of a spear prove to be as strong in trial as they appear to the sight, such a spear will be called 'sadiqu-l-ka'ub' in Arabic. The tongue being in harmony with the heart, the action being in accord with the word, the outward and inward being of the same color, the creed and the deed being in tandem—these are among the manifestations of 'Sidq', and the entire outward and inward of human life is illuminated by them. If this is not there, then the entire meaning of a human is finished. This is the thing that grants a human those wings of flight by which they climb spiritual heights, and it also provides support to their patience. 

The original spirit of 'Qunut' is humility and self-abasement for Allah, Jalla Shânuhu. This thing is the natural fruit of the awareness of Allah Almighty's infinite blessings and the feeling of His endless greatness. it makes blessing a means for gratitude and calamity a means for patience, and keeps the servant attentive toward Allah Almighty in every state. Originally, it is the humility and modesty of the reason and heart, but just as the reflection of every state of the heart is manifest on the human's outward, similarly its reflection is also manifest in the human's appearance, gait, talk, and character. This is the opposite of that pride and arrogance which is the result of considering blessings as the fruit of one's own personal entitlement, and it is also contrary to that fickleness and impatience produced from a lack of patience and truthfulness. 

The meaning of 'Infaq' (spending) is clear. This is the opposite attribute of that love for the world's liked things mentioned in the previous verse. If the love for the world's liked things overcomes the heart such that it stops a human from the rights of God and the servants, then this is the thing the Quran described as 'zuyyina lin-nas'. The trait of 'Infaq' is testimony to the fact that in the eyes of the spender, the original value and worth is not of the worldly shards of glass but of the eternal life of the Hereafter and its never-fading blessings. In contrast, a person who avoids spending in the path of God proves by their action that in their sight, all value and worth is only for the transient pleasures of this fleeting world; the concept of the life of the Hereafter does not exist in their mind at all. 

'Istighfar' means humble supplication to Allah Almighty that He places a veil over His servant's shortcomings, sins, and crimes. This supplication is the result of that shame and fear produced in the servant's heart from the feeling of their Lord's infinite favors and rewards and the concept of His justice and retribution. The qualifier "at the time of dawn" (waqt-e-sahar) is attached to it, aiming to point toward the reality that this time is most suitable for the acceptance of seeking forgiveness, most protected from the pitfalls of hypocrisy (Riya), and most favorable for concentration and reflection upon Allah's verses. Clarification of this has occurred from various angles in both the Quran and Hadith, and it is a great favor of the Kind Lord that alongside the instruction for seeking forgiveness, He Himself gave the news of the most favorable time for its acceptance. If you cast a look of reflection on this segment, then while on one hand the qualities of the people who can be bearers of the Quran become clear, the point also emerged as to what the obstacles are that stand between these opponents of the Quran and the Quran. It was known that they lack that patience which could keep them firm against the resistances of the soul and Satan, that truthfulness which could create accord between their creed and action, word and deed, outward and inward, that devoutness (Qunut) which could bow both their neck and their heart before the greatest Right-holder, that longing for eternal life which could urge them to sacrifice the world for the Hereafter, and that awareness and feeling of the blessings and punishments of the Bestowing and Judging God which could raise them from the beds of heedlessness and stand them before their Lord for the dawn supplications. And simultaneously, the style of expression made a subtle hint toward the fact that today, the people who have accepted this Quran and are supporting Allah's Messenger in spreading it are characterized by these attributes, and it is only due to being characterized by these attributes that they have become capable of carrying this heavy burden.

Subject of Verses 18 to 22: The same subject as above is described from another aspect with another magnificent preamble. Its summary is that the testimony of Allah, His angels, and all bearers of true knowledge has been from the beginning that there is no deity except the One Allah, and He is the Establisher of justice and equity, and the Mighty and Wise. The religion He granted for the guidance of servants and to establish them on justice and equity is Islam; this is Allah's true religion; this is the religion He sent through His prophets and messengers. But the Jews and Christians, due to their mutual stubbornness, deliberately created differences in this religion and set up separate idols of Judaism and Christianity instead of Islam. Following this, addressing the Prophet (PBUH), it was stated: if the Jews and Christians want to argue with you regarding this true religion, then do not take their opposition into account at all, but clearly announce to both the People of the Book and the Quraysh: "I and my companions have adopted the path of Islam; now let whoever wishes persist in their misguidance." Your responsibility is only to deliver this message; after that, refer the matter to Allah. He is well-aware of everyone's circumstances; He will give to each what He finds them deserving of. Further on, through the medium of the Prophet (PBUH), a threat is given to the People of the Book: those who have been denying Allah's verses, who have shed the blood of Allah's prophets unjustly, and who have been in pursuit of harming and killing the reformers, renewers, and champions of justice and equity—now the time for their judgment has come; now they will not be able to escape God's grasp; their deeds will go to waste in both this world and the Hereafter, and no one will be able to be their supporter or helper. In this light, recite the following verses.

Three Aspects of the Testimony to Tawheed and Equity: In this verse, Allah Almighty has referenced His own testimony, that of His angels, and that of the people of knowledge regarding His Oneness and His being 'Standing firmly for justice' (Qa'iman bil-Qist). This testimony is from three different aspects. 

1. Testimony of the Horizons (Afaq): One is the testimony of the horizons. The detail of this summary is that the Creator of this universe has made it in such a way and is running its system in such a way that it provides clear testimony that He is One, with no partner. The Quran has described this testimony under the heading of the proof of Tawheed with such detail that there is no need to quote its evidences. Then, from this same system of the universe, the Quran has proven that in every corner of it, the Creator of this universe has placed a 'Mizan' (Balance/Scale); it is impossible for anything to move an inch away from its determined axis and orbit. This is testimony that its Creator and Fashioner likes justice and equity ('Adl and Qist), and does not want any of His creations to deviate even a hair's breadth from this justice and equity. Evidences of this reality are many in the Quran. For the sake of brevity, we quote only one verse as an example: 'The sun and the moon [move] by precise calculation, And the stars and trees prostrate. And the heaven He raised and imposed the balance [mizan]. That you not transgress within the balance. And establish weight in justice and do not make deficient the balance' (Ar-Rahman: 5-9). Meaning: this universe by its very existence is bearing witness that its Creator is a lover of justice and equity, and its sun and moon, trees and stones, heaven and earth are giving this lesson in the language of their state every moment: just as they do not transgress even a hair's breadth from the scale appointed by God—their every movement being measured by that scale—similarly, the human should also adopt a measured way of life in all corners of their life according to God's balance, and not deviate at all from His appointed limits. 

2. Testimony of History: Within this testimony of the horizons falls the history of nations. The Quran has also presented the history of nations to prove that this world is not a place of chaos but its Creator and Master is running it under a system of justice and equity. On its stage, He sends various nations one after another and tests them: do they use their choice and their powers within God's law of justice and equity, or do they adopt the path of rebellion and defiance? As long as a nation uses its capabilities while staying within God's limits, He makes it prosperous and nurtures it; when it deviates from this path and adopts the path of defiance, then after giving respite to a certain limit, He erases it and makes another nation its heir. The Quran has described this Sunnah with great clarity. 

3. Testimony of the Souls (Anfus): The second testimony is the testimony of the souls. Allah Almighty has made human nature such that it itself bears witness to Tawheed and, alongside that, to Allah Almighty's being 'Standing firmly for justice.' We are describing the arguments for this testimony in various places in this book, and we have also written two books specifically on this subject titled 'Haqiqat-e-Shirk' and 'Haqiqat-e-Tawheed.' Those seeking details should read them. It is this Tawheed-loving nature of humans due to which the Quran declared Tawheed as the 'religion of nature' (Din al-Fitrah): 'the fitrah of Allah upon which He has created mankind.' And it is this justice-loving nature on the basis of which the Quran asks the deniers of reward and punishment: 'Then will We treat the Muslims like the criminals? What is [the matter] with you? How do you judge?' (Al-Qalam: 35-36). 

4. Testimony of Revelation (Wahi): The third testimony is the testimony of revelation. Allah Almighty sent His countless prophets and messengers to inform servants of His likes and dislikes and His commands and prohibitions, and He gave testimony to His Oneness and His being 'Standing firmly for justice' to all of them, and these prophets and messengers delivered this testimony to their respective nations. Traces and signs of this testimony exist even today in the traditions of those nations and the teachings of their scriptures, but they ignored these traces and traditions and fell into such theories and creeds that are contrary to both Tawheed and God's being 'Standing firmly for justice.' However, due to this wrong way of those nations, He has not withdrawn from these high attributes of His; He remains characterized by them and will always remain so. Consequently, it is the demand of these very attributes that He revealed the Quran—as passed in the preamble above—as a 'Furqan' (Criterion) between truth and falsehood so that the straight path of truth and justice becomes clear to people again and no excuse remains for persisting in falsehood. 

From this detail, it became clear that the testimony to God's Oneness and His being 'Standing firmly for justice' is not being obtained from only one aspect but from three different aspects. The system of the universe He made and its history are giving its testimony, the nature He created is a witness to it, and His Prophets have always heralded this reality. This point is stated with extreme summary in this verse, but the details of this summary are spread throughout the thirty parts of the Quran. Alongside His own testimony, Allah Almighty also referenced the testimony of the angels here. This is the expression and statement of a factual matter. The angels are the ones who become the means for the implementation of God's wills in the universe and the intermediary for delivering His revelation to God's Prophets; therefore, they are the primary witnesses among His creation to God's Tawheed and His being 'Standing firmly for justice.' Their testimony—aside from being a factual matter—was also stated specifically from this aspect: that the ignorant, by declaring them God's partners and intermediaries for false intercession, negated both Tawheed and God's being 'Standing firmly for justice.' This is because when the concept is that intercession can make truth into falsehood and falsehood into truth, then where did God remain 'Standing firmly for justice'? To remove this misunderstanding regarding the angels, the Quran has also referenced their own acknowledgments in various places through their own tongues. For the sake of brevity, we suffice with quoting one example: 'And there is not among us any except that he has a known position. And indeed, we are those who stand in rows. And indeed, we are those who exalt [Allah]' (As-Saffat: 164-166). We have already provided a detailed discussion on the angels within the verse of faith in Surah Al-Baqarah. 

Testimony of the 'Ulul-'Ilm' (People of Knowledge): After the angels, the testimony of the 'Ulul-'Ilm' is mentioned. 'Al-'Ilm' (The Knowledge) is a Quranic terminology referring to that true knowledge which has reached the world through the prophets and messengers. we have discussed this in detail elsewhere. The bearers of this knowledge have given testimony to God's Tawheed and His being 'Standing firmly for justice' in every era. This points to the group of reformers and renewers (Muslihin and Mujaddidin) who have arisen in every era and who have struggled to purify God's religion from innovations and mixtures, establishing creeds on the foundation of pure Tawheed and laws, actions, and morals on the basis of truth and justice. These are the people pointed to in the next verse with the phrase 'yamuruna bil-qist' (those who command justice) and regarding whom it was stated that the People of the Book used to kill them. A nuance of the wisdom of religion should be noted: here, the people of knowledge are referenced alongside Allah and the angels, and justice and equity ('Adl and Qist) are referenced alongside Tawheed. From this, it was known what position the people of knowledge hold in the eyes of Allah Almighty and what rank and status justice and equity hold in the system of Divine Sharia. The bearers of true knowledge are those who have a relationship with the category of angels, and the rank of justice and equity is so high and exalted among Divine attributes that the first thing that can be mentioned after Tawheed is this.
