Then, the People of the Book are asked in a tone of wonder: if they are avoiding faith in the final Prophet and the responsibilities of the covenant they made, are they then seeking a religion other than Allah's religion? Allah's religion is Islam, and this very religion is the religion of this entire universe, because every single thing in this universe obeys Allah in its creative circle, willingly or by compulsion. Following this, the comprehensive declaration (Kalimah Jami'ah) of the Muslim Ummah is referenced: if these People of the Book do not want to be free from the shackles of their prejudices, then leave them to their state and announce that we believe in all the prophets, we make no distinction among them, and we are obedient to God alone and surrender ourselves to Him. Then, in the following verses, the evil end of these People of the Book is mentioned: how can these people—who adopted the path of Kufr after faith and belied the final Messenger after recognizing him—benefit from God's guidance? They are deserving of the curse of Allah, His angels, and all of creation. In this light, recite the following verses.

Exegesis of Verses 81-82: The Covenant regarding the Prophets from the Children of Israel: In "mithaq an-nabiyyin", the possessive relationship (idhafah) is not toward the subject (fa'il) but toward the object (maf'ul). It does not mean a covenant was taken *from* the prophets, but it means a covenant was taken from the Children of Israel *regarding* the prophets. This covenant, as mentioned in the verse, was for this: since the Children of Israel were made bearers and trustees of the Book and Wisdom, the natural demand of their position is that when prophets come—especially when the final Prophet comes—they should be the first to step forward to believe in him and help him. The mention of this covenant has occurred in various styles in the Quran. For example, in Surah Al-Ma'idah: 'And Allah had already taken a covenant from the Children of Israel, and We delegated from among them twelve leaders. And Allah said, "I am with you. If you should establish prayer and give zakah and believe in My messengers and support them and loan Allah a goodly loan, I will surely remove from you your misdeeds and admit you to gardens beneath which rivers flow. But whoever of you disbelieves after that has certainly strayed from the soundness of the way."' 

The Covenant regarding the Unlettered Prophet: In that verse, the word 'rusul' (messengers) is general, but in another verse, there is an explicit mention of the 'Nabi-e-Ummi' (the Unlettered Prophet, PBUH). Observe: '...and decree for us in this world [that which is] good and [also] in the Hereafter; indeed, we have turned back to You." [Allah] said, "My punishment - I afflict with it whom I will, but My mercy encompasses all things." So I will decree it [especially] for those who fear Me and give zakah and those who believe in Our verses - Those who follow the Messenger, the unlettered prophet, whom they find written in what they have of the Torah and the Gospel, who enjoins upon them what is right and forbids them what is wrong and makes lawful for them the good things and prohibits for them the evil and relieves them of their burden and the shackles which were upon them. So they who have believed in him, honored him, supported him and followed the light which was sent down with him - it is those who will be the successful' (Al-A'raf: 156-157). 

From these verses, it is clearly known that a covenant was taken from both Jews and Christians to believe in the Unlettered Prophet (PBUH) and to support and aid him, but they paid no heed to this covenant. Some traces of this covenant exist in the Torah and Gospel too, but they have now become very hazy due to the dust of alteration. They will be discussed at the suitable occasion. 

'rasulum-musaddiqul-lima ma'akum' refers to the Unlettered Prophet (PBUH). We explained 'musaddiqul-lima ma'akum' in Al-Baqarah. It has two aspects. One particularly important aspect is that through the advent of the Prophet (PBUH) and his attributes and feats, the referent of those prophecies manifested which existed in the Torah and Gospel and for whose manifestation the People of the Book were waiting—and they should have been waiting. This is because from the confirmation of these prophecies, their own status was primarily elevated. For this meaning of the word 'tasdiq' (confirmation), keep this verse of a Hamasa poet in view: 

'Fadat nafsi wa ma malakat yamini... fawarisun saddaqu fihim zhununi' 
(My soul and what my right hand possesses be sacrificed... for the horsemen who proved my suspicions about them true).

From this aspect, if the Jews and Christians had reflected, they would have seen that through the advent of the Prophet, they and their books were being confirmed; but it was their wretchedness that they belied the one who confirmed them, and they denied the one whose proof and whose testimony they had carried the heavy burden of for so long when he finally arrived. 

A form of taking the Covenant in the Children of Israel: 'qala a-aqrartum wa akhadhtum 'ala dhalikum isri' (He said, "Do you acknowledge and take upon that My commitment?") has a specific occasion and place which must be kept in mind to understand the force of this segment. In the Mosaic Sharia, it was the rule that when such instructions from Allah Almighty were revealed, Sayyidina Moses would not suffice with merely reciting them to his companions individually; rather, he would gather the entire community of the Children of Israel—or at least all their leaders—in the Tabernacle, the Ark would be in front, and after sermon and reminder, he would recite the command of the Lord God and then take an acknowledgment of its obedience from everyone. After everyone's acknowledgment, he would emphasize to the people to be witnesses to it, and would make God a witness over it. Finally, he would also inform them of the worldly and afterlife consequences and results after the disobedience of this command. In this way, every command and prohibition of Allah Almighty would acquire the status of a covenant and 'Mithaq' between Allah Almighty and the Children of Israel. Now, what a place of heed this is: the bearers of the Sharia for whose protection these efforts were made—they scattered the shreds of every single covenant of it. In this light, reflect on the words 'fa-man tawalla ba'da dhalik' (so whoever turns away after that), and the true weight of 'ba'da dhalik' (after that) will be felt: who could be a greater covenant-breaker than those who turn away after this? The word 'Fasiq' (disobedient/wicked) here is not in the general sense, but in the sense it was used regarding Iblis: 'fa-fasaqa 'an amri rabbihi' (so he slipped out/fled from his Lord's command); in this same sense, it was used here for the Children of Israel.

Islam is the Religion of the Entire Universe: Upon all this avoidance and flight of the People of the Book, it is now asked in a tone of wonder: what after all is the objective of this effort of flight from Islam and the Prophet of Islam? Are these People of the Book seeking a religion other than Allah's religion? Allah's religion has been Islam since eternity. This is the religion He gave to all prophets and messengers, and this is the religion of this entire universe. The sun, moon, clouds, wind, and heaven and earth are all followers of this religion. The reality of Islam is to surrender oneself to Allah. Who has the audacity to defy God's command and His law? Those who commit some defiance in their limited circle of choice (and this circle of choice too is established by God and under His will)—they too are helpless and prostrate under God's laws within the creative circle. Who has the strength to flee from the physical laws of life and death? Thus, nature and reason demand that the human also willingly obey the laws of that same Creator and Master in their limited circle of choice, whose laws they are obeying by compulsion in their creative circle. In this way, their life will become harmonious and of the same color as every atom of this entire universe. Perfect compatibility will be produced in their circle of choice and creative circle, and the human will dye themselves in the color of God like the angels and prophets by surrendering the freedom granted by God to God's Sharia. This is Islam. This is 'Sibghatullah' (the color of Allah). This is God's religion. This is the religion of Adam, this is the call of Noah, and this is the religion of Abraham; and this final Prophet (PBUH) has come with its call. Then, leaving this religion of nature and this religion of the universe, what religion are these People of the Book—being People of the Book—seeking?

'wa ilayhi yurja'un' (and to Him they will be returned) contains a hint toward the reality that just as there is no path of escape from God's law in this life, similarly the path is blocked ahead too. One who is released from it by death is not truly released, but they too go to God and surrender themselves to Him.

The Comprehensive Declaration of Islam: This verse has already passed in Surah Al-Baqarah with the exact same words. Its words and contents were discussed there; see verse 136 of Al-Baqarah. This is the announcement of the comprehensive declaration of Islam through the tongue of the Prophet (PBUH), and the context of the speech is: if these People of the Book seek a religion other than Islam, then leave them to their desire. Let Satan trip them in whichever valley he wishes. Do not waste your time behind them, but announce: we have believed in Allah and in this religion of His which is the religion of all prophets. We make no distinction among these prophets—to believe in some and deny others. We hold faith in all, and we are obedient to God alone and surrender ourselves to Him.

After clarifying the arguments in favor of Islam, it was now announced in clear words that those who seek or persist in a religion other than Islam—regardless of whether it is Judaism or Christianity or any other religion—it will not be accepted by Allah, and such people will be deprived and failed in the Hereafter.

Exegesis of Verses 86 to 89: Specific Meaning of the word "Hidayat": We discussed the word 'Hidayat' (guidance) in Al-Baqarah, noting that it has three stages. Its final stage is the guidance of the Hereafter. In this stage, guidance is toward the ultimate goal and purpose, and the servant benefits from the fruit of their efforts and is successful with the result of their life's struggle. The word 'Hidayat' is used in this sense too in various places in the Quran. I repeatedly feel that 'yahdi' (He guides) in this verse is in this sense. The late teacher takes the general meaning of guidance from it. According to him, the negation of guidance for the Children of Israel here is communal, not individual. The meaning is: how can the path of Islam open for a nation that has committed such severe crimes!

In 'shahidu', 'testimonies' refers to the testimony of the heart: that the hearts of these People of the Book admit that this Messenger is true. His signs that have manifested to them are so clear that their hearts bear witness to his truthfulness, but they belie it purely out of stubbornness, prejudice, and envy.

'wallahu la yahdil-qawma-zh-zhalimin'. This is the justification for the previous verse: how can Allah Almighty make those successful in the Hereafter who adopted Kufr after faith, who have received the clear signs of this Messenger's truthfulness yet are belying them, whose hearts bear witness that this Messenger is on the truth yet their tongues belie him? Such people are committing a very great oppression against their own nature, their own reason, and their own soul; and it is the Sunnah of Allah that Allah Almighty does not make those successful who lose their own landmarks of guidance with their own hands and trip themselves. The punishment for such people is that the curse of Allah, His angels, and all of creation be upon them. The qualifier 'ajma'in' (all together) with 'lin-nas' (for mankind) highlights the reality that on the Day of Resurrection, both the good and the bad will curse them. The reason for the curse of the good is clear; the bad will curse because they were misled due to them. Thus, it is clarified in the Quran that on the Day of Resurrection, the misleading leaders and their misled followers will curse each other. The followers will say: you ruined us; if you hadn't blocked our path, we would have been on guidance. The leaders will say: we made you just as we were; you yourself were wretched that you did not adopt the path of guidance.

In 'khalidina fiha', the referent of the pronoun is Hell. Although the mention of Hell is not present in the words, the preceding mention of the curse has provided such a clear context for it that the need for its mention in words no longer remained. Effectively, the curse became the substitute for the punishment itself. Examples of this style in language are many. We will discuss this style in the exegesis of Surah Al-Hadid. Regarding this punishment, it was stated that neither will there be any reduction in it at any stage, nor will they ever receive respite from it. After falling into it, all doors of hope will be closed for them, except for those who repent after these warnings, reform their circumstances, and openly declare the truths they have been concealing until now. Allah Almighty is Forgiving and Merciful.

Exegesis of Verses 90 to 91: Description of those whose Repentance is not Accepted: This is the description of those whose repentance will not be accepted. These are the people who, after committing all those crimes mentioned above, fell into Kufr after faith. Then, after this Kufr, they continued to pile layer upon layer of disbelief. When the final hour came, they uttered "repentance, repentance" with the tongue, without reforming their crimes, without expressing and admitting their concealments of truth before the Prophet and the believers, and without trying to wash away their sins through spending in Allah's path and the aid and support of the Prophet. Rather, as hinted in the Quran, they died in the false hope of 'sayughfaru lana' (it will be forgiven for us)—that Allah will forgive all our errors. The Quran clarified here that for those afflicted with this type of vain greed, neither is this repentance a true repentance, nor will Allah Almighty grant acceptance to the repentance of such people. 

Similar is the case of those who fell into Kufr after faith and died in that state of Kufr. It stated: if such people were to give even the earth full of gold as a ransom (fidyah) to save themselves from the Divine punishment, it would not be accepted. This style of expression was adopted merely to convey the impossibility of their salvation. Otherwise, in the Hereafter, no one will have anything to give as ransom, nor is the Hereafter a place for this type of transaction. 'wa ma lahum min-nasirin' (and they will have no helpers) negates the vain greed these people held regarding the intercession of their great ancestors. It stated: in the Hereafter, they will have no helper. 

This subject has already passed in Surah Al-Baqarah. We provided a detailed discussion on it there. We quote the verse here too so that some implications of the verse under discussion come to light. It was stated: 'Indeed, those who conceal what We sent down of clear proofs and guidance after We made it clear for the people in the Scripture - those are cursed by Allah and cursed by those who curse, Except for those who repent and correct themselves and make evident [what they concealed]. Those - I will accept their repentance, and I am the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful. Indeed, those who disbelieve and die while they are disbelievers - upon them will be the curse of Allah and of the angels and of the people, all together, Abiding eternally therein. The punishment will not be lightened for them, nor will they be reprieved' (Al-Baqarah: 159-162).

Subject of Verses 92-99: If the preceding subject is in mind, then there will be no difficulty in understanding the following sequence of discourse. Above, from verse 63, the debate began that the Jews' and Christians' claim of being upon the religion of Abraham is a completely baseless claim. Upon the religion of Abraham are this Prophet and his companions; but these Jews and Christians want to veil the original facts through various conspiracies and alterations and mislead the creation so that no sacrifice has to be given. They want to be considered worthy of the highest station of loyalty to God merely by performing a few false or true rituals of religiosity. 

Here, first of all, this misconception was removed: the station of loyalty to God cannot be attained merely through false ritualism and showy religiosity. This is because the original thing is that you spend in God's path from those things that are beloved to you. Until a human becomes accustomed to sacrificing their beloved things for God, the courage to fulfill the requirements of God's oaths and pledges is not produced within them. 

Accusation against Muslims of Opposing the Religion of Abraham: Following this, an objection of the Jews—which they raised to prove the Muslims were against the religion of Abraham—was answered incidentally. It was that some such things are permissible for the Muslims to eat and drink which, according to the Jews' presumption, were Haram in the Abrahamic Sharia. For example, regarding the camel, they claimed it has been Haram since the time of Prophet Abraham (PBUH), but for the Muslims, it is not only Halal and pure but is their most beloved wealth, and they consider its slaughter and sacrifice a very rewarding act. Through this propaganda, their objective, as we hinted above, was merely to make the masses believe that Muhammad (PBUH) and his companions have turned away from the religion of Abraham. The Quran gave the answer: this point is merely a fabrication of the Jews. The Torah itself is a witness that the prohibition of the camel relates to the Mosaic Sharia, not the Abrahamic Sharia. 

After answering this incidental objection, they were invited again to follow the Abrahamic religion, and an indication was made toward those prophecies that were entered in the Torah regarding the primacy of the Kaaba, its centrality, and its being a fountainhead of blessing and guidance, but which they tried to veil. Furthermore, attention was drawn toward those signs which clearly testify that Prophet Abraham (PBUH) made this very land his residence, declared it the 'Abode of Security', established it as the center of Hajj and worship, and for thousands of years his descendants have been the bearers of his name and traditions on this land. In the final two verses, the People of the Book were reproached: what a place of regret it is that you, who were appointed by God to point out the path, are spending your efforts to block people from it and to lose it. In this light, recite the following verses. It is stated:

The Condition for Loyalty to God is Spending Beloved Wealth: The research of the word 'Birr' passed in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah. The original spirit of this word is the fulfillment of covenants and the performance of rights and duties, whether these be the rights and duties of God or of His servants. The Children of Israel were absolute zeros in the matter of fulfilling covenants and performing rights, yet by merely following a few rituals in an outward manner, they thought that the rank and station they held in loyalty to God was something no one else had achieved or could achieve. Consequently, in this presumption, they considered themselves the sole monopolists of the inheritance of Prophet Abraham and all other prophets and were not ready to even think that anyone could be their rival in this field. The Quran here struck at this very false presumption of theirs: the station of loyalty to God is not attained merely through empty claims and the performance of a few rituals, but it requires sacrifice. Until you spend in the path of God from your beloved things, this claim of yours is entirely without proof. For testing love and loyalty, this touchstone is one that was sufficient to effectively expose the entire pretense of the Children of Israel, because they tried to maintain the 'cost-free' outward shows of religiosity to an extent, but where the matter of spending arose—and that too of beloved wealth—then their entire claim of love and passion would vanish. Even though regarding the Prophet Abraham whom they claimed to follow and whose inheritance and deputy-ship they sat as sole monopolists of, they knew that the station of loyalty to God he achieved was not achieved through mere verbal expenditure but through the sacrifice of his beloved only son. The Quran has criticized this ritualistic religiosity of the Jews in various places. For example: 'Righteousness [birr] is not that you turn your faces toward the east or the west, but [true] righteousness is [in] one who believes in Allah, the Last Day, the angels, the Book, and the prophets and gives wealth, in spite of love for it, to relatives, orphans, the needy, the traveler, those who ask [for help], and for freeing slaves; [and who] establishes prayer and gives zakah; [those who] fulfill their promise when they promise; and [those who] are patient in poverty and hardship and during battle. Those are the ones who have been true, and it is those who are the righteous' (Al-Baqarah: 177).

'wa ma tunfiqu min shay'in fa-innallaha bihi 'alim' (And whatever you spend of anything - indeed, Allah is Knowing of it) aims at its mandatory result. Meaning: since God is aware of every single penny you spend, be at peace; not a single bit is going to go to waste. If you spend one, you will receive from ten to seven hundred times, and God's favor is additional to that, which has no limit or end.

Answer to a Jewish Objection: This is an incidental answer to the objection of the Jews mentioned above. The Jews claimed that they alone were the original followers of the religion of Abraham, not the Muslims. Among the arguments they presented was that Muslims considered certain foods permissible that they believed were forbidden in the Abrahamic Sharia. They specifically alluded to camel meat, which was a beloved form of wealth for the Arabs but is forbidden in the Jewish Sharia (as stated in Leviticus). The Quran refuted this misconception at the appropriate time, stating that all pure things (Tayyibat) meant for consumption were originally permissible for the Children of Israel as well, including camel meat. However, before the Torah was revealed, Israel (Jacob) had forbidden certain things upon himself. Thus, the Torah itself testifies that the prohibition of the camel or other such things is not found in the Abrahamic era; if it exists, it is found in the Torah.

Three Types of Forbidden 'Tayyibat' among the Jews: The pure things forbidden in the Torah contrary to the Abrahamic religion are of three types. First, those created merely by the hair-splitting and legalistic rulings of Jewish jurists. They issued fatwas forbidding things, which were later incorporated into the Torah as part of the text, giving a jurist's opinion the status of Divine Scripture. Second, things that became forbidden due to the Jews' rebellion, stubbornness, and habit of excessive questioning. They raised so many questions in determining the specifics of a matter that the scope of permissibility became increasingly narrow, and perfectly pure things became forbidden for them. Third, things that were avoided by their ancestors. For example, Sayyidina Jacob (PBUH) avoided certain things out of caution or personal distaste. The Jews attributed the source of such prohibitions to Prophet Abraham, and they were included in the Torah's list of prohibitions. These are the very prohibitions described in the Quran as 'Isr and Aghlal' (burdens and shackles). The Jewish scriptures contained prophecies that the Prophet sent upon the religion of Abraham would make all pure things lawful for them and liberate them from these self-imposed chains. We will discuss this in detail in the exegesis of Surah Al-An'am; here, these brief indications suffice.

Meaning, those who persist in claiming that the things they have forbidden were also forbidden in the religion of Abraham by Allah—even after this clarification—are inventing lies against Allah. And who is more unjust than those who invent lies against Allah!

Say to them that what you say is merely a false fabrication against Allah, whereas what Allah has stated is the truth. Therefore, do not try to prove your innovations as the religion of Abraham; instead, follow the religion of Abraham which I am calling you toward. Abraham (PBUH) was entirely single-minded (Hanif) on the path of Islam; he did not carve out other paths from it, nor was he among the polytheists.

Research on 'Bakkah': 'Bakkah' refers to Makkah. This was its name in ancient scriptures. Lexically, it means 'city' (e.g., Baalbek, the city of Baal). One example of the alterations the Jews made through 'Lavy al-Lisan' (twisting the tongue/pronunciation) to hide the signs of the final advent was this word. They distorted 'Bakkah' to 'Baca' and, treating it as a verbal noun, translated it as 'weeping,' thus changing the 'Valley of Bakkah' into the 'Valley of Weeping' (see Psalm 84) to obscure the greatest sign that could guide people to the final Prophet. In this verse, the Quran mentions Makkah as 'Bakkah' to remind them of the ancient name found in the Torah and still present in some scriptures like the Psalms.

(Maulana Farahi writes in 'Mufradat al-Quran' regarding the word Makkah: 'People have differed on its derivation, but there is no doubt it is a modified form of Bakkah, as it appears in the Quran. This was the name of the valley when Prophet Ishmael was settled there. It means habitation, as evidenced by the word Baalbek. Since Prophet Abraham came from Babylon, he preferred a word from his own language for the name of Makkah.')

This verse points to the facts proving that the House of worship built by Prophet Abraham (PBUH) is this very Baitullah in Makkah. Abraham made this the center of his religion, and from this House, in accordance with his famous prayer ('Our Lord, send among them...'), the call of the Prophet was raised—the one born among the unlettered (Ummiyyin) whose call would benefit the entire world. This is hinted at in the words 'blessed and a guidance for the worlds' (mubarakan wa hudan lil-'alamin).

It requires no proof that the 'Bethel' (House of God) built by Abraham mentioned in the Torah can only refer to the Baitullah in Makkah, not Jerusalem. This is because the construction of the Temple in Jerusalem occurred hundreds of years after Abraham (PBUH) at the hands of David and Solomon (PBUT). This is clear evidence that this House [in Makkah] is the manifestation of the blessings of the Abrahamic religion, not Jerusalem.

Signs of the Kaaba being the House of Allah: 'In it are clear signs' (fihi ayatun bayyinat), meaning there are extremely clear proofs that this House was built by Prophet Abraham (PBUH). Although the Jews tried to erase these signs, the Torah still contains irrefutable evidence that exposes their alterations and reveals the truth. We discussed this extensively in the section on the Qibla in Surah Al-Baqarah. 

After mentioning the signs generally, the verse highlights three specific points: 1. It is the 'Maqam Ibrahim' (Standing Place of Abraham). 2. Whoever enters this sanctuary is safe. 3. Hajj to this House is obligatory for all who are able. 

We have discussed these in Surah Al-Baqarah, but we briefly reiterate the points proving this House is Abraham's construction and the center of his religion: 

Meaning of 'Maqam Ibrahim': As explained in Al-Baqarah, this refers to the fact that Abraham chose this location for his residence after migration; here, near Marwah, he sacrificed his only son; here he settled his family to serve the House and establish prayer; and here a nation related to him has lived for centuries. These facts are proven so decisively by arguments from the Torah that no fair-minded person can deny them.

Makkah is a City of Peace: 'Whoever enters it is safe' (wa man dakhalahu kana aminan) refers to the fact that this land and this House are the manifestation of the acceptance of Abraham's prayer for peace. His prayer is recorded in the Quran: 'My Lord, make this city secure and keep me and my sons away from worshipping idols' (Ibrahim: 35). It is the blessing of this prayer that established the tradition of the Sacred Months and made it a crime to harm even an animal in the vicinity of this House.

'Hajj to the House is a duty to Allah...' points to the fact that the acceptance of Abraham's prayer for the centrality of this land is evident in every corner. His prayer is mentioned: 'Our Lord, I have settled some of my descendants in an uncultivated valley near Your sacred House... so make hearts among the people incline toward them and provide for them from the fruits that they might be grateful' (Ibrahim: 37).

The Tradition of Hajj: Similarly, the instruction given to Abraham by Allah to announce the Hajj has remained a tradition from his era until today: 'And [remember] when We assigned for Abraham the site of the House, [saying], "Do not associate anything with Me and purify My House for those who perform Tawaf and those who stand [in prayer] and those who bow and prostrate." And proclaim to the people the Hajj; they will come to you on foot and on every lean camel; they will come from every distant pass' (Al-Hajj: 26-27).

Referencing these signs aims to prove to the People of the Book that the only House in the world that can be Abraham's construction and the center of his mission is the Baitullah in Makkah. Despite their alterations, the Jewish scriptures still contain clues to this reality. 

'And whoever disbelieves - then indeed, Allah is free from need of the worlds': Meaning, if the People of the Book persist in their stubbornness and claim that the religion of Abraham is what they follow and its center is Jerusalem, then they are deniers of Allah's signs. Once the proof is completed, Allah becomes indifferent to who chooses disbelief or faith. The Hadith stating that Allah does not care if a person who neglects Hajj despite being able dies as a Jew or a Christian is based on this final segment of the verse. In our view, the reason is that such a person's attitude reflects the indifference the Jews and Christians showed toward the Baitullah, resulting in their own loss.

Exegesis of Verses 98-99: This is a rebuke and reproach to the People of the Book: why do you deliberately deny Allah's signs regarding the religion of Abraham, the Baitullah, and the final Messenger, which are present in your own scriptures? And why are you filling people's minds with doubts? You must know that this mischief you commit against Allah and His signs is done in His presence, and He sees everything. You seek to create doubts in those who have found the path through Allah's grace, trying to turn them back so their path is lost, even though Allah had originally placed you on this path to guide others. You were meant to be 'Witnesses of Allah' (Shuhada' Allah), but you chose the profession of highwaymen and robbers. Remember, Allah is not unaware of what you do.

Emphasis on Muslims to Avoid the Wicked among the People of the Book: 'A group of those who were given the Scripture' refers to that specific group of the People of the Book whose opposition and whisperings were detailed above. As mentioned previously and as will follow, there was also a group of fair-minded people among them; therefore, the Quran consistently ensures that no injustice is done to them. Thus, in emphasizing that Muslims avoid the machinations of the People of the Book, it points specifically to the group from which protection is intended. This was a requirement of justice and was also wise for the purpose of the call (Da'wah).

The specific aspect of warning in this verse is that since they are People of the Book, a well-intentioned person might have a good opinion of them, thinking that religious people could not possibly intend misguidance. But the reality is that they are such staunch enemies of Islam that they will not stop until they turn any Muslim who listens to them back into a disbeliever.

Strategies for Protection from Fitnas: 'I'tisam' means to hold fast or grasp something firmly. 'I'tisam billah' means to remain firm upon Allah's commands, instructions, and His Book in all circumstances, whether favorable or unfavorable, and regardless of opposition. The verse implies that while disbelief and apostasy are always a sign of wretchedness, to choose that path today—while Allah's verses are being recited to you and the Messenger is among you—would be the height of wretchedness. It would mean stumbling in broad daylight, leaving no room for excuse. The verse then provides the strategy to avoid such a stumble: if you want your feet to remain firm on the straight path and your opponents to fail in tripping you, the method is to hold fast to Allah—meaning make His verses and instructions your protection and remain steadfast upon them despite all opposition and machinations.

The Reality of 'I'tisam billah': This clarifies that holding fast to Allah means fearing Him as He should be feared ('haqqa tuqatih'). Although this piety (Taqwa) is required only to the extent of one's ability—as clarified in 'fear Allah as much as you are able' (At-Taghabun: 16)—there is a 'right' to fearing Him. First, the rights Allah has over a servant are unlike any other's. Second, the limits He established and the punishments for breaking them are entirely for the servants' worldly and afterlife welfare; He gains nothing from their observance. Third, His eyes are monitoring everywhere, even the whispers of the heart. Fourth, no one else can save a person from His grasp, and He can punish in both worlds forever. A person cannot understand the true meaning of fearing God, let alone fulfill it, without considering all these aspects. Many who abandon God and His Sharia out of fear of humans do so because they fail to distinguish between human opposition and Divine wrath.

'And do not die except as Muslims' clarifies the subtle reality that this fear is not temporary but a lifelong commitment. One must live and die upon it. This struggle begins with maturity and ends with the final breath. If this continuity breaks even at the last stage, a lifetime of effort is wasted. The style also implies that this path is not smooth but full of ups and downs and Fitnas. One will face the ambushes of Satan and the corruption of opponents. Greed will tempt, and fear will threaten. The one who reaches the destination having protected their faith through all these stages, surrendering their soul to the Creator in that state, is the one who truly feared God as He should be feared and attained the station of 'I'tisam billah'.

Meaning of 'Hablullah': 'Habl' means rope. It evolved to mean connection and link because a rope is a means of connecting two things. A Hamasa poet says: 'But I maintained the connection (habl) with him due to my link with the connection of Abu Bayan.' It further evolved to mean a 'covenant' because just as a rope binds two things, a covenant binds two nations. The Quran uses it in this sense: '...except by a covenant [habl] from Allah and a covenant from the people.' In the verse under discussion, 'Habl' refers to the Quran, as it is the covenant between our Lord and us. Holding fast to God is not literal, as God is not a physical object; it means holding fast to the Book that is the intermediary between us and Him. This clarifies the phrase 'whoever holds fast to Allah' from the previous verse. Among the predecessors, Qatadah, Suddi, Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, and Dhahhak held this view. Ibn Jarir narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri: 'The Messenger of Allah (PBUH) said: The Book of Allah is the rope of Allah extended from the heaven to the earth.' It is the thing that joins servants to God. Those who interpret it as 'Allah's Covenant' also effectively mean the Quran. We will discuss this in Surah Al-Ma'idah.

The emphasis on 'all together' (jami'an) and the prohibition 'do not be divided' (la tafarraqu) shows that this is required collectively. This 'Rope of Allah' is what binds Muslims together; they must not let their unity be scattered. If this connection weakens or they seek other ropes and standards for truth and falsehood, they will become scattered like the Jews and Christians.

A Warning to Muslims: Following this is a reminder of the great favor bestowed upon the Arabs through this Book. Before its revelation, every Arab tribe was an enemy to the other, engaged in bloody wars. Their deities were different and their interests conflicted. But this 'Rope of Allah' threaded them together into a string of pearls, turning mortal enemies into brothers. The point is: if you wish to maintain this state, keep your attachment to this Rope. If this link weakens, the state of ignorance (Jahiliyyah) will return. You were on the brink of a pit of fire, and God saved you. Do not arrange to fall back into it.

Because this is a crucial point and the instructions here deeply affect the future of Muslims—where even a minor error could open doors to horrific Fitnas—Allah has stated His instructions with great clarity, as indicated by 'Thus Allah makes clear...'

The Fundamental Purpose of the Caliphate: This instructs the Ummah on the arrangement necessary to maintain 'I'tisam bi-Hablillah' (holding fast to the Rope of Allah). Muslims are told to appoint a group from among themselves to call to good, command 'Ma'ruf' (the recognized good), and forbid 'Munkar' (the recognized evil). 'Ma'ruf' and 'Munkar' refer to the recognized values and prohibitions of both Sharia and society. The use of 'command' (amr) and 'forbid' (nahy) implies that this task is not merely through preaching but through the exercise of authority and power, which is impossible unless this group holds political power on behalf of the Ummah. If only preaching were intended, 'calling to good' would have sufficed. In our view, this verse proves the obligation of establishing the Caliphate. Following this command, the first thing Muslims did after the Prophet's (PBUH) death was establish the Caliphate upon the Prophetic method. Its primary purpose was to ensure Muslims did not deviate from the goal of holding fast to Allah. The methods were three: calling to good, commanding Ma'ruf, and forbidding Munkar. From these three, all departments of the Rightly Guided Caliphate emerged to fulfill the nation's internal and external responsibilities.

'And those are the successful' (wa ula'ika humul-muflihun) does not refer only to that specific group but to the entire Ummah: the nation that makes this arrangement for holding fast to Allah is the one that will achieve success in both worlds.

This verse uses the fate of the Jews and Christians as a lesson for Muslims: despite clear warnings, they abandoned God's rope, and then whatever rope each person found, they considered it the 'Rope of Allah.' This resulted in irreparable divisions. It warns: do not ruin your world and your Hereafter like them. This is not the path to success but to a painful punishment.

Exegesis of Verses 106 to 109: Warnings to Muslims: Reflecting on these verses reveals several realities. First, after losing the 'I'tisam bi-Hablillah', the People of the Book fell into division and discord, which is effectively equivalent to returning to Kufr after faith. Second, for those whom Allah honors by placing His rope in their hands—if they choose to abandon it and place other nooses around their necks, they will face a 'blackening of faces' on Judgment Day equal to the 'brightening of faces' they were once offered. Radiant faces belong to those who hold onto the rope in all circumstances; they are truly worthy of Allah's mercy. Third, these warnings are 'in truth' (bil-Haqq), meaning they will certainly happen. Those who treat them as empty threats will arrange their own disgrace. Allah announces this beforehand because He does not punish without completing the proof. Fourth, all authority in the heavens and earth belongs to Allah alone. All matters return to Him, and His decision is final. Any hope placed in others is a delusion that will prove to be a mirage when the truth manifests. Note that these warnings are being related to the Muslims to save them from these dangers.

Subject of Verses 110 to 120: The preceding warnings made it clear that the People of the Book proved themselves unworthy of the position of leadership. Thus, they were dismissed, and this trust was handed to this Ummah. Simultaneously, Muslims are given the glad tiding that their opponents can no longer cause them significant harm; they will face defeat everywhere, and disgrace has been destined for them. Incidentally, the righteous minority among the People of the Book is praised. Then, the original disease of the People of the Book is pointed out—the veil that prevented them from accepting the truth—and it is clarified that after being deprived of this truth, their outward shows of religiosity are worthless. Finally, Muslims are warned to end all intimate friendships with them, as their hearts contain nothing but enmity and no spark of goodness remains. In this light, recite the following verses.

'Kana' here is 'Tammah' (complete), as in 'Kanallahu 'aliman hakima'. 'Khayra Ummah' (the best nation) points to the reality that you are now on the correct highway of religion. The People of the Book created twists and turns, losing the original religion. God has now raised you for the guidance of creation. This was described in Surah Al-Baqarah as 'Ummat-e-Wasat' (the Middle Nation). As we wrote there, because this Ummah is exactly at the point of balance and on the middle highway, it is the 'Best Nation.' In 'lin-nas' (for the people), a possessive noun is omitted: for the reform, guidance, and bearing witness of Allah's religion over the people, as stated in 'that you may be witnesses over the people.'

The Position of 'Best Nation' is Conditional upon Attributes: 'You enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong and believe in Allah' is the proof for this Ummah being the best. This points to the fact that you were not exalted to this position based on lineage or descent—as the People of the Book presumed about themselves—but the responsibility of commanding the good and forbidding the evil granted you this entitlement. This automatically clarifies that this position is conditional upon attributes and responsibilities. Allah has not bound it to any specific group such that it remains with them even if they, like the Jews and Christians, turn 'Ma'ruf' into 'Munkar' and vice-versa.

Faith is the Root of Every Good: 'You believe in Allah' points to the fact that the original foundation is faith in Allah. Whatever honor or excellence one obtains in God's eyes is based on this. Commanding the good and forbidding the evil is only authoritative when accompanied by faith in Allah. Sermons of God-worship and religiosity from pulpits and stages that come from hollow breasts are of the nature described by the Quran regarding Jewish scholars: 'Do you enjoin righteousness upon the people and forget yourselves?'

'Fisq' (wickedness/disobedience) here means exiting from faith and obedience. We have explained this elsewhere.

Announcement of this Ummah's Leadership: Structurally, this verse is the announcement of this Ummah's position of leadership. In the introduction to this Surah, regarding the prayer 'O Allah, Owner of Sovereignty,' we wrote that it contains the decision of the People of the Book's dismissal and the Muslim Ummah's appointment. After detailing the covenant-breaking of both Jews and Christians, it is announced that the rightful claimants to the position of 'Best Nation' are these believers, not the Jews and Christians. Regarding the People of the Book, it was stated that if they believed in the Quran and the Prophet, it would have been 'better for them'—the ambiguity in 'better' reflects a Divine wrath that words could not fully contain. Then, in a tone of sorrow, it was stated that despite being People of the Book, only a few among them believed; the majority turned out to be covenant-breakers and disobedient.

Meaning of 'Adha': It means hurt and annoyance. The meaning is that their root has been cut. They no longer have enough strength to cause you major harm. The most they can do is utter some taunts, insults, and slanders to vent their frustration. They lack the courage for more. And if they come out to fight you, they will show their backs and then be so disgraced that no help will reach them from any side. Subsequent events confirmed this prophecy letter by letter. This subject is stated further on: 'And you will surely hear from those who were given the Scripture before you and from those who associate others with Allah much abuse [adhan kathiran].'

The Strike of Disgrace: 'Disgrace has been struck upon them' (dhuribat 'alayhimu-dh-dhillah) means that just as wet mud is plastered onto a wall, disgrace has been plastered onto them. This also hints that because they chose the path of disgrace over honor, disgrace was fully imposed upon them. 'Wherever they are found' (ayna ma thuqifu) points to the encompassment and universality of this disgrace: wherever they are, disgrace rules over them. Even in their own center, they are disgraced. There is no region in the world where they possess honor or stand on their own strength. 'Except by a covenant [habl] from Allah and a covenant from the people' points to the fact that if they are established anywhere, it is not on the reliance of their own power but because either a treaty with the people of Allah has given them security or they have obtained some support from neighboring tribes. These supports are temporary and provisional. Consequently, the various treaties the Prophet (PBUH) initially made with their tribes were later terminated due to their covenant-breaking and mischief, and they were either killed or exiled for their crimes. The treaties they had with other tribes also became practically ineffective as those tribes gradually came under the influence of Islam. A tree with hollow roots cannot stand for long on external supports. In this era, the so-called state of Israel is also under this ruling. It stands not on its own strength but by 'habl minal-nas'—the support of America and England—and something that stands on another's support is effectively not standing at all.

Meaning of 'wa ba'u bi-ghadhabim-minallah': We explained this in Surah Al-Baqarah: it describes the wretchedness and misfortune of the Jews—that where they were meant to return with the wealth of honor in both worlds, they returned with God's wrath due to their low-spiritedness, resulting in disgrace being imposed upon them. Allah Almighty had commissioned them for the position of Imamate and Testimony. If they had fulfilled its responsibilities and remained firm on their covenant, their status in both worlds was very high; but they could not manage the responsibilities due to their worldliness and low-spiritedness, and thus deserved God's wrath. This reality should be kept in view: the higher the station, the harder the ascent, and consequently, the more dangerous the fall.

Meaning of 'Maskanah': 'And poverty [maskanah] was struck upon them.' 'Maskanah' refers to a lack of courage and low-spiritedness. The Quran has clarified the low-spiritedness of the People of the Book through very realistic parables. These parables show that worldliness so dominated the Jews that no courage for the quest of the Hereafter or for sacrifice and selflessness remained within them. They did not find the courage to sacrifice even the smallest 'cash' of their world for the greatest 'credit' of the Hereafter. Torah shows that Prophet Moses (PBUH) also repeatedly rebuked them for this cowardice. Later prophets also lamented it. The Quran mentions it in various places. The parable of the dog is a parable of their low-spiritedness, and if you reflect, you will realize it was this very low-spiritedness that made them deserve the imposition of disgrace.

'dhalika bi-annahum kanu yakfurun...': This describes the cause: why did they deserve the punishment of disgrace, wrath, and low-spiritedness? It stated: the cause is that they used to deny Allah's verses and kill the prophets unjustly. The means of elevation and height are Allah's verses. Those who deny them and make this denial their habit—if they do not deserve disgrace, what else do they deserve? The Quran presented this in the parable: 'And if We had willed, We could have elevated him thereby, but he adhered [instead] to the earth.' Similar is the case of killing prophets and those who command justice. These are the flower of humanity. It is through their support that humanity crosses the stages of felicity and perfection. If a group has been their killer, what else could they deserve from God except wrath and disgrace?

'dhalika bi-ma 'ashaw-wa kanu ya'tadun': This describes the reason for their denial of Allah's verses and killing of prophets: meaning, disobedience and transgression of Allah's limits were their habit. This thing incited them to Kufr and the killing of prophets. Finally, these crimes caused God's wrath upon them, and disgrace and low-spiritedness were plastered onto them. 

After clarifying the components, reflect on the structure: the proof for the point made in the previous verse is stated here—how can those people harm you who are under God's strike everywhere and now live only on your supports?

'Ummatun qa'imah': meaning that group which is established upon Allah's covenant and His Sharia. 'yatluna ayatillahi ana'al-layli wa hum yasjudun': meaning they recite the Divine Book during the hours of the night and perform prayers. Prayer and recitation at night are proof of their being established on the Divine covenant, because the restlessness for this sincere prayer and recitation can only be produced in those who possess a very deep sense of their great responsibility. Describing prayer through 'prostration' (sujud) is, first, because prostration is among the most important pillars of prayer; second, it is the greatest manifestation of awe and humility; and third, because the Jews, as we mentioned elsewhere, had removed prostration from their prayer.

Exegesis of Verses 114-115: The Believing Group among the People of the Book: This mentions that small group of the People of the Book who are exempt from the majority described in the previous verse. It stated: not all People of the Book are like those mentioned above; rather, among them is a group of people who are firm on their covenant, night-worshippers, believers in Allah and the Last Day, performers of the duty of commanding the good and forbidding the evil, and those who race toward good deeds. This group included those who had already openly believed in the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and those who, although they had not announced their Islam by the time of these verses' revelation, were true believers in their hearts and eventually accepted Islam. The Quran has counted these people among the righteous and pious. Whatever good they do, they will not be deprived of its reward. After entering Islam, they will also receive the full reward for the good deeds they performed before entering Islam. This group is mentioned at the end of this Surah: 'And indeed, among the People of the Book are those who believe in Allah and what was revealed to you and what was revealed to them, [being] humble to Allah. They do not exchange the verses of Allah for a small price. Those will have their reward with their Lord. Indeed, Allah is swift in account' (Al Imran: 199).

Kufr and Shirk Ruin All Good Deeds: This refers to those People of the Book who, contrary to the aforementioned group, persisted in their Kufr. Regarding them, it stated: the love of wealth and children that made them indifferent to God will not save them from God's grasp and Hell. These people will fall into Hell and will never be able to emerge from it.

Whatever they spend in this worldly life for the display of their formal religiosity—this spending too is of no use to them in the Hereafter. The parable of their spending is that of a crop hit by a freezing wind that destroys it. Good deeds performed alongside Kufr and Shirk go to waste. Kufr and Shirk are a cold fire that turns all labor into a heap of ash. In the previous verse, it was stated that those People of the Book who remained firm on their faith and believed in the Quran after its revelation—all their previous good deeds will also be granted acceptance. In this verse, it is clarified that those who neither remained firm on their covenant nor entered Islam—all their efforts will be ruined.

'wa ma zhalamahumullahu...' (And Allah did not wrong them...) points to the fact that this is not an injustice from God upon them, but an injustice they committed against themselves. A tree remains safe by its root remaining healthy; if the root is uprooted, then no matter how much effort one spends in watering its branches and leaves, it is all futile. If the person is to be blamed for the ruin of their labor, it is themselves, not nature and the law of nature.

'la tattakhidhu bitanah': 'Bitanah' refers to the lining of a quilt, etc. 'Bitanat al-rajul' refers to a person's family and their close confidants (those with whom one shares secrets due to trust in their affection). 'Khabal' means corruption and disorder. 'la ya'lunakum khabala' means they will spare no effort in creating corruption within you. 'Anat' means hardship, trouble, and pain. 'waddu ma 'anittum' means they desire for you to face stumbles and get trapped in troubles. 'qad badat-il-bagh-dha'u min afwahihim' refers to those anti-Islamic statements from which it was perfectly clear that the People of the Book were ready to tolerate anything but were not ready to tolerate Islam and Muslims at any price. Consequently, the Quran even quoted their statement that they openly prefer the polytheists over the Muslims, saying: 'Those [polytheists] are more rightly guided than the believers.'

In 'ha-antum ula'i', the address pronoun has come between 'ha' and 'ula'i'. 'Ha' is actually a particle of alerting; therefore, when emphasis is needed, the Arabs adopt this method. They also say 'ha-ana-dha'.

Allah's Complete Book is only the Quran: We discussed 'wa tu'minuna bil-kitabi kullihi' (and you believe in all of the Scripture) in detail in Surah Al-Baqarah. Originally, the relationship between previous scriptures and the Quran is that of a part to a whole. The People of the Book were given only a portion of the Divine Book. The giving of the complete Book was reserved for the final advent. Consequently, regarding the People of the Book, these words appear repeatedly: 'Have you not seen those who were given a portion [nasiban] of the Scripture...' (Al Imran: 23). Allah's complete Book is the Quran, just as His complete religion is Islam. For this reason, when a Muslim believes in the Quran, they believe in God's complete Book—both what was revealed before and what was revealed after. The Quran is the compendium of all.

'wa idha khalaw' (and when they are alone), meaning when they are in their own assemblies, just as it was stated in Surah Al-Baqarah in exactly this type of context: 'and when they are alone with their evil ones, they say, "Indeed, we are with you."' 

Instruction to Muslims to be Alert toward the People of the Book: In these verses, there is a warning to Muslims similar to the warning in verse 28. Although the address is general, the focus—as we hinted under verse 28—is on those Muslims who, either due to their simplicity, did not well-understand the tricks of the People of the Book, or due to their weakness, did not want to break their previous links with them, whereas at this stage, the relations of the People of the Book with any Muslim had not remained sincere but were solely for conspiratorial purposes. For this reason, the Quran warned in extremely clear words and a perfectly certain tone: O you who have believed! Do not make those other than yourselves your confidants; they will spare no effort in harming you. They do not desire for you to achieve success in your objectives; rather, they are desirous of hardships and troubles for you. Their enmity has already manifested from their talk, but what is in their hearts is even more hard and severe. It stated: We have clarified this point well. If you still do not understand, you will suffer the consequences.

Following this, a sense of honor was invoked: you extend the swings of love toward them, but they hold no love for you at all, even though you believe in the complete Book and all the prophets, and they are not ready to believe in your Book. When they meet you, they say to deceive you that we have believed, and when they are among their own, they grind their teeth and bite their fingers at you in rage.

In the middle of this, through the tongue of the Prophet (PBUH), those grinding their teeth were addressed: you may die in this very furnace of rage if you wish, but you cannot harm Islam in the slightest.

Patience and Taqwa: It stated that their state is such that if you achieve any success, it causes them great grief, and if any harm reaches you, they rejoice at it. But if you show steadfastness and avoid the things you have been given extremely clear instructions to avoid, their tricks will not be able to harm you at all. Allah Almighty is encompassing all their activities and all their tricks. For further clarification of this final segment, cast a glance at verses 125 and 186 of this Surah. Under 'in tashbiru wa tattaqu', a point from Ibn Jarir is worth remembering. He says that the Taqwa commanded here primarily includes acting upon the instructions of 'la tattakhidhu bitanatam-min dunikum'—meaning avoiding making disbelievers your confidants.

Subject of Verses 121 to 129: Further on is the commentary on the events and circumstances that occurred at the occasion of the Battle of Uhud, and this sequence of discourse will continue until the end of the Surah. We clarified in the introductory discussions of this Surah that the defeat caused by the mismanagement of a group of Muslims at Uhud affected every group related to this struggle between Islam and Kufr from some aspect. Among the Muslims, those who were weak became discouraged by this incident, and the hypocrites took advantage of this discouragement to start filling their hearts with various types of whispers against Islam, its future, and the Prophet (PBUH). The Jews also received a great boost from this incident; they became active anew in propaganda against Islam and conspiracies against the Prophet (PBUH). The wound the Quraysh received from the defeat at Badr was effectively healed by this event, and they began to hope again that Islam could be harmed.

This situation required that the events of Uhud be commented upon to remove all the misunderstandings the opponents were creating in minds at this occasion. Simultaneously, this was the best opportunity to seize upon the Muslims' weaknesses and errors and instruct them to avoid them in the future, so that this Ummah becomes purified from weaknesses and truly worthy of the responsibilities of the position to which it was exalted in the verse 'You were the best nation.' Consequently, when the discourse in this Surah reached the point that you alone will be dominant and victorious and no trick of your opponents will harm you provided you remain firm on patience and Taqwa, the best opportunity arrived to comment on the events of Uhud and show what flaws were still present in communal life from the aspect of patience and Taqwa that caused this fall, and what lessons of individual and collective reform and purification are obtained from it. In this light, recite the following verses.

'Bawwa'a, yubawwi'u' means to settle, place, station, or commission. 'Maqa'id' is the plural of 'maq'ad', which means a place to sit, but in broad usage, it can also mean a place of ambush, and if context exists—as it does here—it can refer to a battle position/post.

This verse is a preamble to the commentary on the events of the Battle of Uhud and their effects that follows. Since this war had occurred recently, it was before everyone; it was sufficient to point to its events without naming it. But since this commentary was also on some circumstances and effects whose certain corners related to the hidden conspiracies of certain parties or related to mental and spiritual concepts and impressions, Allah Almighty referenced His attributes of 'Sami'' and 'Alim' to warn everyone in the preamble itself: there is no room for anyone to question, refute, or debate this commentary, because this commentary is from the One who knows and hears everything. Whatever He has said is based on infallible hearing and knowledge.

'Fashal' means to lose heart and abandon courage. In war, the primary importance belongs to courage and resolve; weapons and other things hold secondary status. For this reason, the weakness of certain groups was addressed first.

A Mischief of the Hypocrites: According to the statements of commentators, the two groups pointed to in this verse are the Banu Salama of the Khazraj tribe and the Banu Haritha of the Aws tribe. Within these two groups, some cowardice was produced due to the mischief of the hypocrites, but they then recovered. The hypocrites in reality did not want to go out for this war. The Holy Prophet (PBUH) had an estimate of this weakness of theirs. Consequently, he desired that the correct situation come forward before going out. For this, as a test, he placed this question before the Muslims: should the Quraysh be faced from within Madinah or by going out? The response from the true and firm Muslims was obviously: by going out; thus, they gave this response with full zeal and passion. But the hypocrites tried to explain the expediencies of fighting while besieged in Madinah. When the Prophet estimated the situation and the hypocrites' weakness became clear to him, he did what was in his heart and what his loyal companions had expressed. When the hypocrites saw that their conspiracy had failed, they did go out with the Muslims, but after going out, their leader Ibn Ubayy misled them and, using the pretext that his advice was not valued, separated with a force of three hundred men on the way. This event naturally affected the morale of some Muslim groups. This is because the Muslims' number was only one thousand against three thousand disbelievers. The flight of three hundred men out of a thousand at the exact moment was obviously a major incident which weak temperaments would naturally be affected by. The Quran seized upon this weakness and stated: the Muslims who go out for Jihad in the path of Allah—Allah is their Helper and Designer, and the demand of faith is that Muslims place full trust in Allah's help and His design. When God is with you, what does it matter if some group of hypocrites and cowards abandons you?

After clarifying the demand of faith and trust (tawakkul), the event of Badr was also reminded: when, despite your numerical minority and material lack of resources, God helped you just yesterday and gave you a magnificent victory, why then do you despair of this God? He is even today your Supporter, Helper, Ally, and Designer.

Meaning of 'Adhillah': 'Adhillah' is the plural of 'dhalil'. 'Dhalil' is the opposite of 'aziz'. 'Aziz' means dominant, powerful, and beyond the reach of others; 'dhalil' means weak, powerless, and a 'soft morsel' for others. Moral baseness is not among the fundamental components of this word but among its distant corollaries. Consequently, this word is also used in good meanings, for example: 'adhillatin 'alal-mu'minin a'izzatin 'alal-kafirin' (humble toward the believers, powerful against the disbelievers). Meaning: if the disbelievers want to poke them and soften them for their interests, they are a rock, but for the Muslims, they are very soft-natured. They can be benefited from however one wishes. In the verse under discussion too, this word only expresses the Muslims' numerical and material weakness at that time; there is no trace of moral weakness or disgrace in it.

'fattaqullaha la'allakum tashkurun': 'Taqwa' here, as we hinted under verse 120, is in its broad meaning—meaning avoid adopting the path of cowardice and lack of resolve contrary to the demand of faith, trust, and accepting God as the Ally and Designer. This Taqwa is necessary for fulfilling the true right of God's gratitude. Those devoid of resolve and courage will be beaten by Satan at every step and will adopt the path of falsehood instead of truth. Such people will not be able to fulfill the right of God's gratitude.

Meaning of 'Musawwimin': 'Musawwimin' is from 'sawmah' or 'simah', meaning a mark or sign. 'Al-khayl al-musawwamah' refers to horses that are marked. The attribute 'Musawwimin' for the angels aims to express that Allah Almighty will send them with special arrangement for this mission, and they will be wearing their distinguishing marks and badges specifically for this war. This is a reference to what the Prophet (PBUH) said to restore the Muslims' morale at the time when Abdullah bin Ubayy returned with his three hundred companions and some Muslim groups, as mentioned in the previous verse, were affected by discouragement. You (PBUH) said: if three hundred men have separated, so what? Is it not enough for you that Allah Almighty provides you help through three thousand 'sent down' angels in place of three hundred weak and low-spirited men? "Sent down" (munzalin) aims to point toward the fact that they will be sent down from heaven as fresh reinforcement by Allah Almighty specifically for this special task.

'Bala in tashbiru wa tattaqu...' (Yes, if you remain patient and fear Allah...): This was brought based on reliance upon Allah Almighty's mercy and victory: that this deficiency of three hundred men would be fulfilled by three thousand angels. Allah Almighty confirmed this statement of His Prophet and added two thousand more angels to it by His favor, but with the condition: 'in tashbiru wa tattaqu'—if you remain steadfast and avoid disobeying the commands of God and the Messenger. The events of the Battle of Uhud bear witness that Allah Almighty fulfilled this promise of His. Consequently, the Muslims in their initial attack thoroughly put the disbelievers to the sword and defeated them. But after achieving the defeat, a group of them showed weakness and, contrary to the Prophet's explicit instruction, left an extremely important post out of greed for spoils. The result was that the achieved victory turned into defeat due to the weakness in patience and Taqwa. Further on in this Surah, this point is mentioned thus: 'And Allah had certainly fulfilled His promise to you when you were killing them by His permission until [the time] when you lost courage and fell to disputing about the order [of the Prophet] and disobeyed after He had shown you that which you love. Among you are some who desire this world, and among you are some who desire the Hereafter. Then He turned you back from them [in defeat] that He might test you. And He has already forgiven you, and Allah is the possessor of bounty for the believers' (Al Imran: 152).

In 'ja'alahu', the referent of the pronoun is that promise of victory mentioned in the previous verse. The meaning is: at this occasion, Allah Almighty specifically promised your help through the angels so that this might be a cause of glad tiding for you and the discouragement you felt from the attitude of the opponents and hypocrites might be removed. Even if this glad tiding had not been revealed, the believers should nonetheless hold the creed that victory and help are always in Allah's hand alone, and He is Mighty and Dominant, granting victory and dominance to whom He wills, and also Wise, so none of His actions is devoid of wisdom. We will discuss this verse further in Surah Al-Anfal.

'li-yaqta'a tarafam...' (That He might cut off a part...). This describes the purpose of the special arrangement for encouragement at this occasion: that Allah Almighty desired that in this war, either the power of the Quraysh be completely trampled and they return disgraced, or at least a part of their strength be broken.

Consolation to the Holy Prophet (PBUH): This verse holds the nature of a digression (Iltifat) toward the Holy Prophet (PBUH). The way the hypocrites acted regarding this war and the effect they had on other Muslims was hinted at above. Naturally, the Prophet (PBUH) would have felt grief at this. Upon this, Allah Almighty consoled you: you have no role in this matter, nor is there any responsibility upon you for it. You performed your duty as it should be performed. Now, if a group wrongs its own soul, why should you grieve for it? Refer their matter to Allah. He will grant them the success of repentance if He wills. They will repent and He will forgive them. And if they are not worthy of it, He will punish them.

All authority in the heavens and the earth is in Allah's hand alone. He will forgive whom He wills and punish whom He wills. Finally, by referencing His attributes of 'Ghafur' (Forgiving) and 'Rahim' (Merciful), He clarified that God is Forgiving and Merciful. For this reason, if He punishes someone, He will do so only when He finds them deserving of punishment.

Subject of Verses 130 to 143: In the following verses, first, in connection with this same Jihad mentioned, spending (Infaq) is urged; then, to remove the discouragement produced by the defeat at Uhud, some of its wisdoms and benefits are clarified so that the heat for spending and Jihad is produced anew within those Muslims in whom some dejection had arisen. Although the address is general, the context proves that the focus of the speech is specifically toward those Muslims from whom some weakness was issued during this war, or upon whose minds the result of the war had left a bad effect. Effectively, this war had brought to the surface the 'dirt and dross' of many temperaments that had been suppressed until now, and the time had come to wash it clean. Consequently, the following sequence of discourse is mostly of this nature. This is effectively a part of the chapter on purification and cleansing. 

The subject of spending began with the mention of the prohibition of usury because usury and spending hold the relationship of opposites. This style is used much in the Quran: when one thing is described, its opposite is generally mentioned with it. Thus, in Surah Al-Baqarah too, the prohibition of usury was mentioned alongside the mention of spending. The only difference is that in Al-Baqarah, the prohibition of usury is after the mention of spending, and in this Surah, it is before spending; both these styles have their separate benefits. But this place is not suitable for discussion on this issue. For the clarification of the structure of the discourse here, just remember that the point of stopping from usury before the command of spending is exactly like emphasizing the avoidance of lies before the instruction to speak the truth. Cast a glance at what we wrote in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah regarding the relationship between usury and spending at this occasion. In this light, recite the following verses.

The Purpose of the Qualifier 'adh'afam-mudha'afah' (doubled and multiplied): The research of the word 'Riba' and other important questions related to it passed in Surah Al-Baqarah. The qualifier 'adh'afam-mudha'afah' attached to it here does not mean that only 'interest upon interest' is prohibited in Islam; rather, this qualifier—as we clarified under 'la yas'alunan-nasa ilhafa' in Surah Al-Baqarah with several examples—is merely for painting the situation and expressing its repulsiveness. Just as in 'do not compel your slave-girls to prostitution if they desire chastity', the purpose of the condition 'if they desire chastity' is not that if they do not desire chastity, they can be compelled to the profession. Rather, the purpose is merely to paint the situation and express its loathsomeness. Exactly so is the qualifier 'adh'afam-mudha'afah' in the verse under discussion.

There is also a nuance of eloquence in highlighting this qualifier here. We have shown above that the original subject being described here is spending in the series of Jihad. Its preamble was the mention of the prohibition of usury because it is the opposite of spending. Spending is mentioned here in a way that Muslims are invited to 'musabaqat' (competition)—meaning to advance against each other, to take the lead, and to win the field. Effectively, if there is a field of competition and race for the believers, it is this. Consequently, the preamble to this subject is: 'And hasten [sari'u] to forgiveness from your Lord and a garden...' This subject necessitated that before inviting to this field of competition, people be turned away from that field in which Jewish moneylenders and financiers were spending their entire force to outdo each other until now, and in imitation of whom the Arabs were also getting a taste for it. Those with a taste for the Arabic language can estimate that the expression of this competition in the field of usury is being made through the words 'adh'afam-mudha'afah'. If these words were not there, this reality would not have fully come forward. The Quran has desired that people, instead of gathering heaps of filth 'doubled and multiplied' in this impure field, should gamble for that Paradise whose width is equal to the heavens and the earth.

The segment 'And fear the Fire, which has been prepared for the disbelievers' in this context is extremely clear proof that those who persist in usury after this warning are disbelievers and the fire of Hell is ready for them; we discussed this point in Surah Al-Baqarah. In reality, such people provide the fuel for their own fire 'doubled and multiplied.' For this reason, it is a reality that their fire is perfectly ready.

A Parable of the Vastness of Paradise: Meaning: if you strike many arrows through usury, you will make ten or twenty or a hundred or a thousand out of one, and its benefit will nonetheless remain limited to this life. In the Hereafter, this entire hoard will become fuel for your burning. In contrast, if you spend your wealth in God's path, you will become worthy of God's forgiveness in exchange and, as a result, the inheritors of such a vast Paradise that the vastness of all the heavens and the entire earth will become like dust before its vastness. Then why not compete to obtain this infinite kingdom of eternal life instead of running for a limited narrow alley of a dead-end street? This same subject appeared in Surah Al-Hadid: 'Know that the life of this world is but amusement and diversion and adornment and boasting to one another and competition in increase of wealth and children - like the example of a rain whose [resulting] plant growth pleases the tillers; then it dries and you see it turned yellow; then it becomes [scattered] debris. And in the Hereafter is severe punishment and forgiveness from Allah and approval. And the worldly life is not but enjoyment of delusion. Race toward forgiveness from your Lord and a Garden whose width is like the width of the heavens and earth, prepared for those who believed in Allah and His messengers. That is the bounty of Allah which He gives to whom He wills, and Allah is the possessor of great bounty' (Al-Hadid: 20-21).

This parable of the vastness of Paradise is nonetheless only a parable, from which a human can only obtain a hazy concept of its vastness. What its original reality of vastness is—this is known only to Allah; but despite this vastness, a human can buy it if they wish by spending in the path of God.

In 'alladhina yunfiquna fis-sarra'i...' (those who spend in ease...), some of those characteristics of spending are described without whose observance neither the right of spending is fulfilled nor does this spending attain the rank of 'Ihsan' (excellence). We discussed these characteristics in full detail in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah. In this series, the emphasis on suppressing anger and pardoning people has a specific aspect. Its justification can be seen under verses 262-265 of Al-Baqarah.

Exegesis of 135-136: A Resistance in the Path of Spending: This is the description of an extremely important resistance in the path of spending: just as the disease of usury produces such a thirst for money that it becomes a mountain for a person to spend on a good work, similarly the addiction to immorality and luxury also closes the path to spending on any virtuous work. Those who set out on this path become so helpless in the hands of their desires that they find no leisure to look in any other direction. For this reason, in the teaching of spending, where the Quran has stopped from usury, it has also stopped from immorality, indecency, and its mandatory result: extravagance and wastefulness. We discussed this under verse 268 of Al-Baqarah. Further discussion on this will come under verses 26-27 of Al-Isra.

It stated that only those will be able to advance in this path of spending who can protect themselves from the addiction to immorality and luxury. Those who knowingly persist in their sins will close the doors of this felicity upon themselves. The path of felicity is that if a person commits some large or small sin due to the dominance of emotions, the remembrance of God should alert them and they should immediately ask for His forgiveness. Besides God, there is no one who can grant forgiveness. Those who make sins their 'covering and bedding' in the hope of others' intercession will only face their own wretched deeds.

Meaning of 'Sunan': 'Sunan' refers to those codes and rules of Allah Almighty under which He deals with nations. If a nation obeys Allah Almighty's commands and instructions and follows His messengers, Allah Almighty makes it prosperous and successful. Conversely, if a nation disobeys God's commands and laws and belies His messengers, Allah Almighty destroys it. Manifestations of this Sunnah are countless in God's earth. In the land of Arabia too—whose inhabitants are addressed in this verse—manifestations of this Sunnah of Allah Almighty existed in the form of the ruins of 'Ad, Thamud, the people of Madyan, the people of Lot, etc. These manifestations of Divine justice are expressed here with the word "Sunan." This word is used repeatedly in this sense in the Quran: '...[This is] the Sunnah of Allah with those who passed on before' (Al-Ahzab: 38). 'Then do they wait for anything except the Sunnah of the former peoples?' (Fatir: 43).

After the prohibition of usury and the invitation to spend in the path of Allah, these two verses are of the nature of warning and threat. We hinted above that although the address is general, specifically in view are those people who were either not yet entirely single-minded or were in explicit hypocrisy.
