It stated: to estimate the end of those who belie Allah's messengers, there is no need to go very far. Sufficient material for taking heed exists in your own country.
We have revealed this clear warning; in it exists the entire material for guidance and advice for those who have the fear of God.

Meaning of 'Wahn': 'Wahn' means weakness. Regardless of whether this weakness is of action or resolve, of the body or of character and morals. In a Hadith, it is mentioned that the Prophet (PBUH) once said to the Companions (RA) that a time will come when you will be like the foam and debris of a flood. The Companions (RA) asked: O Messenger of Allah! What will be the cause of this? You (PBUH) said: 'Wahn' will be produced within you. People asked: O Messenger of Allah! What is 'Wahn'? You (PBUH) said: 'hubbu-d-dunya wa karahiyatul-mawt' (love of the world and dislike of death). From this Hadith, it was known that the baseness of resolve and courage and of action and intention produced from the love of the world and worldly life and the fear of death—which stops a human from Jihad in the path of truth—is 'Wahn'. This Hadith is the best explanation of this word. Here too, 'la tahinu' means: do not lose heart or become terrified by the fall you faced at Uhud. Effectively, the full point is: 'la tahinu mimma ashabakum wa la tahzanu 'ala ma fatakum'—neither lose courage due to the defeat that occurred to you, nor grieve over the loss you suffered.

Meaning of 'Al-Qawm': When the word 'Al-Qawm' appears in this context, it refers to the rival opponent and enemy. Here the hint is toward the disbelievers of the Quraysh. 

Meaning of 'Al-Ayyam': When 'Al-Ayyam' (The Days) appears in this plural form, it refers to those days of history in which major events and incidents occurred. 'Ayyam al-'Arab' refers to the wars of the Arabs. In the Quran: 'wa dhakkirhum bi-ayyamillah'—meaning remind people through those major events of Allah's mercy and His punishment upon nations in the world. In the verse under discussion too, attention was drawn toward the reality that such events of victory and defeat occur to every nation, and they occur under Allah Almighty's wisdom and His law of trial.

In 'wa liya'lamallahu-l-ladhina...', the conjoined part (ma'tuf 'alayh) is not mentioned here. We have mentioned in more than one place in this book that when a conjunction appears like this, its 'ma'tuf 'alayh' is omitted and is determined by context. In our view, 'linabtaliyakum' (to test you) is omitted before it. We have unfolded it in the translation.

Meaning of 'Shuhada'': In 'wa yattakhidha minkum shuhada'', 'shuhada'' refers to those who attain the rank of martyrdom in the path of Allah. The reason for calling these people 'Shahid' (witness/martyr) is that they fulfill the responsibility of 'Shahadat 'alan-nas' (bearing witness over mankind) placed upon this Ummah by Allah Almighty by giving their lives. For this reason, they became deserving of being titled with the title of 'Shahid'. This is effectively the use of the word for its original referent. From this segment, the point also emerges that the rank of martyrdom with Allah Almighty is such an exalted rank, and the quest for it within Muslims in the early period of Islam was so intense, that Allah Almighty provided opportunities for the satisfaction of the zeal and passion of those racing in this field. Consequently, in the fall that occurred at Uhud, while there were other wisdoms and benefits, one wisdom was that those for whom this high position was destined might be exalted with it at this occasion.

Meaning of 'Tamhis': In 'wa liyumah-hisallahu-l-ladhina amanu...', 'Tamhis' means to completely purify something from dirt, dross, and alloy. 'Mahash-adh-dhahaba bin-nari' means he purified the gold from mixtures and dross through fire.

After the research of the words of verses 139 to the aforementioned verse, we now present the contents of these verses in order. 

The dejection that overcame the weaker types of people due to the defeat at Uhud also produced the thought in many minds that the glad tidings of victory and dominance for Islam were all just 'foot-in-the-air' (baseless) talk. The purpose of them was merely to build one's own reputation and use people for one's own purpose. Now the entire reality has opened. The hypocrites and opponents took the opportunity to fan this thought intensely so as to make the Muslims entirely despair regarding the future of Islam. The Quran refuted this propaganda here and assured the Muslims: do not be heartbroken or low-spirited by the incident of Uhud. In this struggle of truth and falsehood, the dominant and exalted ones—as Allah has stated—will be you, provided you become true and firm believers. 

Following this, it stated that if some harm reaches you as it did in the Battle of Uhud, this is not a matter for despair. Your enemies too have already received sufficient blows in this very war and before it at Badr. These alternations of victory and defeat that occur—they occur under God's wisdom and by His command; it is not permissible to derive the conclusion from them that some change has occurred in God's Sunnah and that He has now started loving the bad instead of the good; rather, the purpose is to test and sift people and to highlight their capabilities. It is from this that the true and the raw, the sincere and the hypocrite are distinguished, and the martyrs who gamble their lives for the truth get the opportunity to show their essence.

The Purpose of Tawheed is to Erase Kufr: Then it stated: do not think that if the Quraysh achieved victory at Uhud, Allah Almighty has now started loving these wrongdoers instead of the believers; rather, in reality, this too is a strategy for erasing the people of Kufr. Allah Almighty, by putting the Muslims through the furnace of trial, desires to remove every kind of dross from within them to make them entirely pure gold and to sift them out completely from Kufr and the people of Kufr. After this separation, the believers will become entirely free from the chain that obstructs their progress, and simultaneously the erasing of the people of Kufr is also certain, because in this world, falsehood can remain only as long as it possesses some support from the truth. If the support of truth is entirely snatched from it, its annihilation becomes certain. Allah Almighty created this world 'in truth' (bil-Haqq); therefore, the nurturing of a pure falsehood is entirely against its temperament. After the mention of 'Tamhis' (purification), 'yamhaqal-kafirin' (He destroys the disbelievers) aims to point toward this very philosophy. The punishment that comes upon the people of Kufr after the migration of the Prophet and his companions also contains this secret. Detail of this will come in Surah At-Tawbah.

We discussed the various meanings of 'alima ya'lamu' in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah. Here, the context proves it is used in the meaning of distinguishing and sifting out. After 'jahadu minkum' (those who strove among you), its contrasting phrase 'those who did not strive' was omitted according to the well-known style of Arabic. In 'wa ya'lamas-sabirin', people have presented various justifications for the 'fathah' (vowel) on 'ya'lama', but in our view, it is conjoined to 'wa liya'lamallahu-l-ladhina amanu' above. Since the discourse there had turned toward describing other wisdoms of the law of trial, the mention of patience was linked to the subject of Jihad; but from its 'fathah', the point automatically emerged that among the people Allah Almighty wants to sift out through testing are the patient ones (Sabirin).

Trials are Inevitable on the Path of Truth: Addressing those who were discouraged by the defeat at Uhud, it was stated: if your presumption was that the path of truth is free from dangers and trials and you would enter Paradise directly on a cool road just by claiming Islam, then this thought of yours is entirely wrong. No one can enter God's Paradise until it is determined through testing whether they possess the zeal for Jihad in the path of Allah and whether they can endure trials for the truth. Consequently, for the testing of this very thing, this trial of Uhud came before you. Until now, expressions of great zeal and fervor for Jihad have been made from your side. But their nature was merely verbal expenditure. It is necessary that for the sake of distinction between the true and the false, the lover of truth and the seeker of lust, some such occasion arises when you have to fight face-to-face with death. Thus, Allah showed you this occasion and a touchstone for distinguishing your genuine from your fake came forward. It is worth remembering here that those who were weak of heart generally made more verbal expressions of the desire for Jihad so that a veil remained over their weakness. Surah An-Nisa points toward these very people: 'Have you not seen those who were told, "Restrain your hands and establish prayer and give zakah"? But then when fighting was ordained for them, at once a faction of them feared men as they fear Allah or with even greater fear. They said, "Our Lord, why have You decreed upon us fighting? If only You had postponed [it for] us for a short time." Say, "The enjoyment of this world is little, and the Hereafter is better for he who fears Allah. And you will not be wronged, [even by] as much as a thread upon a date seed."' (An-Nisa: 77).

Subject of Verses 144 to 148: In the following verses, first this misconception was removed: Muhammad (PBUH) is not some superhuman being; he is one Messenger among Allah's Messengers; just as many Messengers have passed away, he too must eventually pass away one day, and it is also possible that he be martyred; but Allah's religion must remain forever; therefore, a person's attachment to this religion should not be based on the assumption that you (PBUH) have come to remain in this world forever. The reform of this misconception was necessary because if some such delusion remained hidden in hearts, everyone's heart would sink upon your passing, and the hypocrites and opponents could take great advantage of this in opposing Islam. Consequently, as soon as some signs of this misconception's presence manifested, the Quran reformed it. Traditions state that when the Muslims were defeated at Uhud, the news also became famous that the Master of the Worlds (PBUH) himself had been martyred. This distressing news lowered the morale of many Muslims. They thought that since the Prophet himself was martyred, what could happen now? Although sensible people managed the situation by saying that since the Prophet was martyred, what is the use of our remaining alive—we too should be martyred for the same purpose of truth for which the Prophet was martyred—nonetheless, this was a weakness that had manifested within the Muslims whose timely reform from the tongue of the Quran itself was necessary to block Fitnas for the future.

Following this, previous prophets and their life-risking companions were mentioned as an example: they too had to perform Jihad in the path of Allah, and they too faced pains and calamities in this path, but they did not become low-spirited; so why do you become low-spirited if you faced a defeat or your Prophet suffered some pain? You too should adopt their way since you have risen for the same task for which they rose. In this light, recite the following verses. It is stated:

'Aqib' means heel. 'anqalaba 'ala 'aqibayhi' is an expression for turning back on one's heels. Here it refers to leaving Islam and turning back toward ignorance (Jahiliyyah).

The meaning is: just as many Messengers have passed in the world, similarly Muhammad (PBUH) is also a Messenger of Allah. Just as trials and calamities faced other Messengers, similar trials and calamities can face him too. Just as all Messengers had to pass through the stage of death, he too must pass away one day. His being a Messenger does not mean he will not pass away or cannot be killed or that the trial of some calamity or defeat cannot face him. If someone had accepted Islam with this misconception and now, after the incident of Uhud, has fallen into some hesitation and wants to return anew to the life of ignorance, then let them return; they will not harm Allah at all but will ruin only their own world and Hereafter. Regarding those who, even after seeing Islam, could not understand the difference between ignorance and Islam and did not become appreciators of Islam—Allah has no concern for them. Allah Almighty considers those worthy of His rewards who are grateful to their Lord for receiving the bounty of Islam; the thought of returning to ignorance does not even cross their minds.

'kitabam-mu'ajjala' is a construction similar to 'wa'd-allah' or 'sun'-allah-alladhi-atqana', etc. In this verse, two weaknesses of the weak and hypocritical types of people are pointed out. 

Two Specific Weaknesses of the Hypocrites: One is that they do not hold the creed that for every person's death there is a Divine decree; until the appointed duration of this decree is complete, no one's death can come; similarly, when the decree is complete, no one's death can be delayed for a single minute. For this reason, instead of fleeing from God's appointed duties, the correct attitude for a person is to perform their duty with resolve and determination and remain at peace regarding death—that its time is written with God and its form is also determined. The second weakness is that they consider their worldly interests entirely dependent on their own effort and strategy and fear that if they pursue the Hereafter too much, they will be entirely deprived of the world. However, this is not the reality. God gives the seekers of the world only as much of the world as is destined for them, and they remain entirely deprived of the reward of the Hereafter. In contrast, those who are seekers of the Hereafter—Allah Almighty blesses them with the rewards of the Hereafter and also gives them from the world as much as is destined for them. For this reason, the correct attitude is not for a person to abandon the Hereafter and remain only a servant of the world, but to become a seeker of the Hereafter and be content with what Allah Almighty bestows from the world. Clarification of this subject is coming in the next verse.

In 'wa sanajzi-sh-shakirin', the context proves that the verb is used in its perfect sense. And "the grateful" (shakirin) refers to those who are heart-felt appreciators of the great blessing of Allah Almighty which they received in the form of the final and perfect guidance. It stated: We will give them the full reward for this appreciation. As for those who, even after seeing this light, seek only darkness—they will be left to wander in the gloom.

Meaning of 'Wahn', 'Dhu'f', and 'Istikanah': The words 'Ribbi' and 'Wahn' have been discussed previously. Although the words 'Wahn', 'Dhu'f', and 'Istikanah' are somewhat shared in the meaning of expressing weakness, there is a subtle difference among these three. The cowardice produced in the heart from the fear of death and the love of life is 'Wahn.' The suspension in resolve and action produced from this 'Wahn' is 'Dhu'f' (weakness). The result of bowing the knees before the rival that manifests from this 'Dhu'f' is 'Istikanah' (submission/humiliation).

A Sunnah of the Prophets and their Companions: The meaning of the verse is: examples have already passed in history before this where God's prophets performed Jihad and many Godly people supported them in this Jihad, and they faced calamities and defeats in this path, but the effect of this on them was not that they lost heart, showed small-heartedness, or bowed before the enemy; rather, they showed steadfastness in the path of truth, and Allah loves such people.

This refers to the wars that Moses, David, Solomon (PBUT), and some other prophets had to fight. The mention of a war in the era of Prophet Samuel—which was similar to the Battle of Badr—has already passed in Surah Al-Baqarah. The purpose of this reference is to draw the attention of those who were discouraged by the defeat at Uhud to the reality that neither the occurrence of war for a prophet and his companions is a strange thing, nor is their passing through calamities and hardships a new incident. This is a Sunnah of the prophets and a mandatory demand of God's law of trial. Let no one remain in the misconception that one who is a prophet and his companions reach the destination without passing through the test. Those beloved to Allah are only those who show steadfastness in His path, not every claimant of religiosity; after all, how else will the distinction between the liar and the truthful be made without this test?

Exegesis of Verses 148-149: In 'wa ma kana qawlahum illa an qalu...', there is a hint toward the fact that when they faced calamities and trials, they did not make the kind of statements that weak Muslims and hypocrites are making today to create various types of doubts against the Prophet; rather, the fall they faced—instead of attributing it to God and the Messenger—they attributed it to their own weakness and their own transgression, and asked Allah for forgiveness for their faults. The reward they received for this was that God blessed them with power and government in the world too, and in the Hereafter too, an extremely high reward and prize exists for them. Finally, it stated: these are the ones who are at the station of 'Ihsan' (excellence), and Allah loves such doers of good.

...

Subject of Verses 149 to 155: Further on is also the commentary on those weaknesses that emerged from the Battle of Uhud and its defeat. The Quran took each one of them and highlighted its interior, stated the strategies for their reform, and hinted at the benefits of training and purification that were fulfilled through this trial for the Muslims.

Exegesis of Verses 149-150: After the defeat at Uhud, the disbelievers and Jews desired to completely erase the effects of the victory at Badr. Consequently, through their organized propaganda, they tried to make the Muslims believe: your thought is entirely wrong that Muhammad is some sent-one of God and has the help of God and the angels. If this were the case, why did you suffer defeat at Uhud? In Badr you achieved victory, in Uhud we were victorious. This is a game of strategy and resources. Attaching it to God and His angels and considering yourselves the monopolists of God's help is merely childish raw imagination. 

Response to the Propaganda: This propaganda of the disbelievers affected those Muslims who were of the weak type. The hypocrites also provided strength to it through their whisperings. For this reason, Allah Almighty warned the Muslims: if you accept the influence of these disbelievers' talk, they will take you back into that same darkness of ignorance from which God brought you out into the light of Islam, and your success will turn into failure again. Your 'Wali' (Ally) and place of return are not these disbelievers that you turn to them in your difficulties and anxieties and seek guidance from them; rather, your place of return and Master is Allah; turn to Him and seek help from Him; He is the best of helpers. This same subject passed above in the verse 'la tattakhidhu bitanah...'

Meaning: their morale which has increased a bit at this time is merely a temporary and provisional intoxication. It has no lasting foundation. Allah Almighty will very soon lower their morale and cast the awe of Islam and Muslims into their hearts. The reason is that they have associated such things in God's divinity for which no testimony exists in reason and nature, nor in the system of the universe, nor in the scriptures revealed by God. Such illusory things, first of all, cannot provide any support against realities; second, their attachment to so many different deities has scattered and dispersed their hearts such that they can never attain that concentration and single-mindedness which is the foundation of all resolve and morale. In the verse, 'zhalimin' (wrongdoers) refers to the polytheists. We have discussed the reasons for describing Shirk as 'Zhulm' (wrongdoing/oppression) in every other place. Among others is the point that Shirk is in reality a very great oppression by a human against their own soul. Allah Almighty made the human the most noble of all creations. The feeling of this honor is what contains the secret of all their power and greatness. When a human begins to worship a creation lower than themselves by accepting it as their provider and ruler, they effectively become an eagle that has accepted the slavery of a lowly sparrow, and thus not only loses their 'eagle-ness' but becomes even more wretched and lowly than the sparrow. Detailed discussion on this will come in the Makkan Surahs.

Meaning of 'hassa, yahussu': 'Hassa yahussu' means to kill the enemy in such a way that they are completely trampled and annihilated. The meaning of 'bi-idhnihi' (by His permission) is that this magnificent result was not merely a miracle of your strategy and your power but a miracle of Allah Almighty's power and will. 

Meaning of 'Fashal': 'Fashal' means to become sluggish, to become lax, and to show weakness.

Meaning of 'ma tuhibbun': In 'ma tuhibbun' (what you love), there is a hint toward the desire for victory. The Quran has opened this ambiguity in some places. For example, in Surah As-Saff: '...and another [favor] which you love - victory from Allah and an imminent conquest.'

Now the refutation of the propaganda that the disbelievers and hypocrites had started spreading is taking place—that the Muslims are unnecessarily afflicted with the obsession that God and His angels help them. If they help, where did this help go at Uhud? We hinted under verse 149 that weak types of Muslims were poisoned by this propaganda. To save the Muslims from this Fitna, the Quran informed: as far as the fulfillment of Allah Almighty's promise of victory is concerned, it was fulfilled at Uhud too, because in the beginning you thoroughly put the disbelievers to the sword, until they retreated and victory was right before you; but before you could thoroughly crush the enemy and force them to surrender, you became lax. Those whom the Messenger (PBUH) had stationed at the protection of the rear pass with the absolute instruction that they should not leave it in any case—they differed in determining the intent of the Messenger's command, and the majority of them, thinking that victory is now in sight, became engaged in obtaining spoils contrary to the Messenger's (PBUH) command; some among you were seekers of the world and some were seekers of the Hereafter. The presence of such people in the ranks of Islam—who would ignore the Messenger's command for the sake of the world—is not liked by Allah Almighty; therefore, the demand of His wisdom was that He put you through a test so that those who are seekers of the world are sifted out and separated from you. Consequently, He turned your face away from them and your victory changed into defeat. 

God's Promise of Victory is Conditional: The meaning is: Allah's promise of victory for the Muslims is not unconditional—that they may adopt whatever attitude they wish yet God's help will remain their companion in every state—but it is conditional upon the condition that Muslims do not become lax in performing their duty, do not differ in obeying orders, do not disobey God and the Messenger, and do not become seekers of the world while abandoning the Hereafter. 

If some such thing is found within them, Allah Almighty still does not immediately send down His wrath upon them but puts them through such trials by which these weaknesses of theirs are removed and they become more worthy of God's support and help. This too became a form of Allah Almighty's pardon, overlooking, favor, and kindness. Consequently, at the end of the verse, He pointed toward this pardon and favor too. 

Regarding the Battle of Uhud, it must be remembered that all biographers agree that the Muslims' initial attack in this war was very successful. They had gained dominance over the enemy, but a contingent stationed at the protection of an important pass—who had the instruction from the Prophet not to leave their place in any case—left their place prematurely and became engaged in gathering spoils. Only a few men of that contingent remained firm at their post. The enemy's cavalry took advantage of this and, performing a flanking maneuver, attacked the Muslims from the rear, and this attack was so sudden and successful that the Muslims lost their senses. The verse points toward this event.

Meaning of 'Is'ad': The original meaning of 'Is'ad' is to go in the direction of an ascent. From this, the idiom 'as'ada fil-'aduw' emerged, meaning to run away in some direction with the head held high.

Meaning of 'ghamman bi-ghammin': In 'ghamman bi-ghammin' (grief with grief), the 'Ba' is for the meaning of 'attachment'—meaning one grief was of the defeat itself, and attached to it another grief also came forward. In our view, this grief refers to the grief that reached the Muslims during this time from the rumor spread by the disbelievers that the Prophet (PBUH) had also been martyred. The mention of this rumor is in the books of history and Seerah, and the hint of it also emerges from this verse of the Quran because it stated: you were running away headlong such that you had no awareness of your right or left to turn and see who it was and what they were saying, to the extent that you did not even pay attention to that Messenger who was constantly calling you from behind: "O servants of Allah, come to me!" After this, with the 'Fa'—which comes in Arabic for the statement of a result—He mentioned this grief. From this, the point emerges clearly that this grief must be related to the person of the Prophet so that they are warned for the lack of appreciation for the Prophet that was issued from them.

To well-understand the system of this verse, it is necessary to cast another glance at the previous verse. Above, He stated: 'Then He turned you back from them that He might test you'—meaning God made you retreat due to your such-and-such errors and disobediences so as to put you into a trial (Ibtila). After this, He clarified that this putting into trial happened because God preferred to forgive you and bestow His favor upon you instead of punishing you for your error. Following this, from 'idh tush'iduna' to 'fa-athabakum ghamman bi-ghammin' is the detail of this trial. Then in 'likayla tahzanu 'ala ma fatakum wa la ma ashabakum', the benefit of this trial is mentioned which can be obtained by the believers if they fulfill its right. 

The Purpose of Trial (Ibtila): It requires no explanation that trial is not God's punishment but His mercy. Punishment comes upon disbelievers, and believers are put into trials. The purpose of punishment is to erase disbelievers. And the purpose of trial is to purify believers from rational and moral weaknesses. One is death, the other is life. The Divine law is: as long as Allah wants to keep a nation remaining, He does not give them the kind of punishment given to criminals and rebels for their crimes, but He continues to remove the diseases arising within them through various trials and tests. He delivers a nation to destruction only when it becomes entirely empty of the original attributes of life. 

Aspects of Removing Grief in the Trial of Uhud: As for the question: what was the point in this trial of Uhud that saved from grief? To understand this, first remember that 'grief' (huzn) here does not refer to that general sorrow and grief which naturally occurs upon the loss of an opportunity or the reaching of a loss; rather, it refers to that despair and discouragement which destroys a human's resolve and courage. Above in verse 139, 'la tahinu wa la tahzanu', this very grief was forbidden. There were multiple causes producing this despair and discouragement present at that time which were in need of reform and treatment. For example: a group among the Muslims was afflicted with the misconception that whoever is a prophet and his companions must necessarily achieve success in every expedition; for them, defeat was equivalent to making their entire claim doubtful. Similarly, a group was afflicted with the delusion that since we are Muslims and have supported the Prophet, we should be superior to the consequences of our errors. Some people also relied entirely on their own opinion and strategy; the reality was not clear to them that in this world, not only strategy is operating but the primary operator is destiny. A group was also afflicted with such bad suspicions about God which were from the remnants of the era of ignorance. Hints toward all these groups are coming in the following verses. Obviously, when so many misconceptions and raw thoughts were present in the Muslims, it was impossible for them to face the circumstances and difficulties they encountered at every step. For this reason, Allah Almighty's mercy purified the Muslims from many raw thoughts through this trial of the Battle of Uhud, which could have caused a faltering in their resolve and courage in delicate times. The verses that open these subjects within this Surah itself—some have passed above and some are coming ahead; however, we quote a verse from Surah Al-Hadid here, as it sheds light on an extremely important corner: 'No disaster strikes upon the earth or among yourselves except that it is in a register before We bring it into being - indeed that, for Allah, is easy - In order that you not despair over what has eluded you and not exult [in pride] over what He has given you. And Allah does not like everyone self-deluded and boastful' (Al-Hadid: 22-23).

Meaning of 'Amanah': 'Amanah' means comfort, peace, and tranquility. 'Nu'as' refers to drowsiness and sleep; here, it is effectively an explanation of 'Amanah' through the method of substitution (badliyat). Sleep is both a means of tranquility and comfort and a testimony to the heart's peace and the mind's single-mindedness. One whose mind is anxious and brain is dispersed finds their sleep flown away, and for such a person, it is not possible to do any task with resolve, courage, stability, and determination. For this reason, during war, it is extremely necessary for an army to get the opportunity to sleep and to be able to benefit from that opportunity. Consequently, due to this importance of sleep, among the strategies for lowering an enemy army's morale is the strategy of not allowing them the opportunity to sleep. It is a very great favor of Allah Almighty if an army gets this opportunity and can also benefit from it, because being able to benefit from it depends not only on getting the opportunity but also significantly on the sleeper's mental and spiritual capability. Regarding the Battle of Badr, among the favors Allah Almighty counted upon the Muslims in Surah Al-Anfal is that they slept well in the night before the war and thus became very alert and active for fighting in the morning. Here in the verse under discussion, it stated that one group slept peacefully, but another group remained concerned only for their own souls. Although the enemy had departed, they continued to think due to their fear and cowardice that he was still standing over their heads.

'yaghsha ta'ifatan' (overcoming a group): the present tense (hal) form is for painting the picture of the situation. 'yazhunnuna billahi ghayral-haqqi zhannal-jahiliyyah' is the clarification of 'ghayral-haqq' (other than the truth), and the purpose of this clarification is to show the repulsiveness of their thought: despite these people going around as Muslims, until now their thoughts and concepts regarding God's attributes and His relationship with human life are the same as they were in the darkness of the era of ignorance. 'yaquluna law kana lana...' (they say, "If there was anything for us...") is an example of their 'thought of ignorance' and also an expression and statement of what they were hiding in their hearts. 

A Case of Omission: The conjoined part (ma'tuf 'alayh) for 'wa liyabtaliyallahu' is omitted, and in such instances, not only is the 'ma'tuf 'alayh' omitted but sometimes the thing whose cause is stated through these forms is also omitted. An example of this is also in that verse of Surah Al-Hadid to which we referenced above. If this omission is unfolded in the light of the context, the full point would be: even if you had acted upon your own opinion, nonetheless, if your death had been destined, you would not have been able to save yourselves from death but would have reached those very spots and been killed where Allah had written your killing. But Allah did this through the hands of the Prophet so that this might become a thorn of regret in your hearts and so that the weaknesses in your hearts might come forward. 

Commentary on the events of Uhud: This entire verse is a commentary on the events and circumstances manifest from the defeat at Uhud. The promise is true, but the treatment of the weaknesses hidden within the community was also necessary. It stated: after the defeat at Uhud, there was indeed a group among you who did not become complaining or suspicious of God and the Messenger. It maintained its courage. It considered this fall that occurred as the result of some flaws of the community itself and remained pleased with God's decision. Consequently, instead of being discouraged and terrified, it slept with tranquility in the coming night while relying on God, which is a testimony to its peace of mind and strength of faith. But simultaneously, there was also another group that remained concerned only for their own souls; they remained afflicted with the type of bad suspicions about God that are not befitting of faith and Islam but are suitable for the era of ignorance. Their thought was that the Prophet acts with despotism and self-will in deciding matters, not valuing their advice. If their opinion had been accepted and the war fought while besieged inside Madinah, this regrettable form would not have occurred and we would not have been killed here with this disgrace. In their refutation, it was stated: this thought of yours is entirely wrong. Even if you were locked in your houses, whoever was to die where would have died there. These matters are not subordinate to your strategy but to the destiny destined by God. Since these weaknesses were present within you, Allah desired that such circumstances arise that your weaknesses manifest, your hearts be tested, and your dross come out. Allah is well-aware of the diseases of the hearts and their treatment.

It passed under verse 122 above that some weak types of Muslims were also affected by the mischief of the hypocrites, especially the morale-breaking conduct of Ibn Ubayy; some of them recovered immediately, but from some, weakness was issued. Allah Almighty did forgive these people because each of them later felt their own error, but He simultaneously clarified that they had some previous errors due to which Satan tripped them. Sin gives birth to sin, and Satan's tricks are more easily effective on those within whom some root of sin exists. For this reason, it is necessary that when a sin is issued from a person, they do not allow it to take root in the heart but uproot it through seeking forgiveness and sincere repentance. Otherwise, it is these types of people who become a cause of trial for large communities. In the light of the Quranic verses above, it has become clear that the trial the Muslims faced in the battle of Uhud occurred as a result of the same type of weaknesses of certain groups.

Subject of Verses 156 to 189: The commentary on the circumstances and thoughts produced from the Battle of Uhud that has been continuing from above—some further points in this series are being stated.

Exegesis of Verses 156 to 158: Meaning of 'Li': In 'wa qalu li-ikhwanihim', the 'Li' is like the one in 'wa qalal-ladhina kafaru lilladhina amanu law kana khayram-ma sabaquna ilayh' (And those who disbelieve say of those who believe, "If it had [any] good, they would not have preceded us to it"). Meaning: in the sense of 'about' and 'regarding.' 'Ikhwan' refers to brothers, kin, and people of connection and relationship. 'Ghuzza' is the plural of 'Ghazi' (warrior). The related part (muta'alliq) for 'li-yaj'ala' is omitted before it. Meaning: this point that is embedded in their minds—it is embedded because, due to their hypocrisy, Allah desires that this bad creed remains a thorn of grief pricking in their hearts. Examples of this type of omission have passed.

Death and Life are in God's Authority: This is an advice to the Muslims to keep themselves protected from imitating the way of the disbelievers and hypocrites. The original cause of their cowardice is this bad creed of theirs: that they consider death and life to be under their own strategies. Consequently, if someone among their kin is killed in some journey or war, they say with great regret: if he had been with us or acted upon our advice, this fall would not have faced him. Accordingly, they said this very thing regarding the slain of the Battle of Uhud. However, the matter of death and life is in Allah's hand. He gives life to whom He wills and gives death to whom He wills. Whoever's death He has written at what time, at what place, and in what form—it will come, even if they were to lock themselves inside iron fortresses. Those who think they can delay death through their strategies can obtain nothing from this delusion except a permanent prick of grief and cowardice. The believers should keep themselves protected from this Fitna. Life and death are in God's authority alone, and He is seeing our every deed at every step.

For further encouragement, it was stated: if you obtain martyrdom in God's path or death comes in some other way, this is not a thing for grief, because the forgiveness and mercy you will obtain in its reward are far better than all those transient hoards that the worshippers of this life are gathering for themselves.

Note that this verse is not inviting to recklessness (tahawwur) but informing of the reality that fleeing from duties is no strategy for saving life. The correct method for a person is to perform the duty when it becomes mandatory with full resolve and determination and hold the certainty that death will come when its time is appointed, and simultaneously hold the certainty that dying in the path of performing a duty is far more valuable than the life of this world and all its hoards. Further clarification of this subject is coming under verses 167 to 171.

Finally, it stated: whoever dies or is killed returns nonetheless to God; so why should a believer be frightened of proximity to God? This is the reality of sacrifice and its original objective!

159 to 160: In 'fabi-ma rahmatin', the same style of language is used as in 'fabi-ma naqdhihim mithaqahum'. Grammarians generally take 'ma' in such instances in the sense of emphasis. In our view, in some instances, it is used merely to keep the rhythm of the phrase correct.

Meaning of 'Fazh': 'Fazh' means harsh-natured, and 'ghalizh al-qalb' means hard-hearted.

Confirmation of the Prophet's (PBUH) Attitude toward the Hypocrites: This verse appeared by way of digression (Iltifat). The effect of the criticism made against the hypocrites in harsh words above could have been that the Prophet's (PBUH) and naturally the high-spirited Muslims' attitude toward them would become harsh. Allah Almighty did not prefer this change to occur at this stage. Although the hypocrites' way was highly objectionable and they were taking very wrong advantage of the Prophet's kindness and turning a blind eye, they were patients; for this reason, Allah Almighty preferred that they still be given further respite for reform so that those within whom even a minor room for reform remains can reform themselves. Consequently, in view of this wisdom, that very noble conduct of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) which had been with these people until now was supported and confirmed by Allah Almighty, and its benefit was also clarified: these hypocrites are not such appreciators of their own health and reform that they would endure any bitter draught for it; it was only Allah's favor that He made you extremely soft-natured and forbearing and you tried for their reform with this same soft-naturedness and this same kindness and compassion. If you had adopted the path of harshness with them even slightly, they are so wild and unappreciative that they would have run away from you, even if all the diseases of the world remained attached to them; so maintain this same path of your kindness and mercy with them for now, overlook their lack of appreciation, and continue seeking forgiveness for them from Allah. 

Note that later, when a group of hypocrites completely disappointed with their attitude and it became clear that they were not going to accept reform through the method of turning a blind eye, then finally both the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and the Muslims were instructed to change their attitude toward these people and, if they were taking wrong advantage of leniency, to bring them to the correct path through harshness. Detailed discussion on this will come under At-Tawbah verses 73, 123 and At-Tahrim verse 9. 

Here, along with the instruction for pardon and seeking forgiveness, the Holy Prophet (PBUH) was also instructed: 'wa shawirhum fil-amr'—meaning continue taking advice from them in matters. There is a specific occasion for the mention of this point which should be briefly understood. 

The Status of Consultation (Shura) in the Islamic System: The Holy Prophet (PBUH) was not in need of anyone's advice in matters of religion because he did everything under the guidance of Divine revelation; but in political and administrative matters, he used to consult his Companions regularly, and thus effectively the Prophet himself laid the foundation of that 'Shura-ism' which has been a fundamental characteristic of Islam's political system. In view of this same 'Shura-ism', at the occasion of the Battle of Uhud—the effects and results of which are under discussion here—he consulted the Companions: should the enemy be faced from within the city or by going out? The purpose of this consultation, as we hinted above, was that the weak people within the community come forward openly; thus it happened. The weak and hypocritical types of people emphasized that the encounter be made while secured inside the city, and their purpose in this was that in this way they would succeed in hiding their weakness and hypocrisy. But the sincere and life-risking ones' opinion was against this, and this opinion was correct and in accord with the Prophet's opinion; for this reason, the Prophet (PBUH) acted upon this opinion. When the hypocrites failed in having their advice accepted, they vented their anger in various ways. One group separated from the army at the exact moment on the pretext that its advice was not valued. The second group, which remained along with a forced heart, started spreading discouragement among the Muslims after the defeat: that this result of the war occurred because its advice was not valued—if their opinion had been accepted, this fall would not have occurred. Obviously, the objective of all these points was mischief and spreading discouragement among the Muslims, but in this stage the benefit, as we hinted, was only this: that the method of overlooking be adopted with these hypocrites and the social and communal rights they possess remain even despite these errors of theirs. Consequently, just as the Prophet was instructed in pardon and seeking forgiveness for them, he was also instructed that in matters that fall under consultation, you continue taking advice from them, even though their weakness and ill-will have become clear. 

1. The point reported in books of history and Seerah—that the Holy Prophet's (PBUH) own opinion was also to face the enemy while besieged inside Madinah but the passionate Companions forced him to go out—is an entirely unproven point. At the time the Holy Prophet (PBUH) placed this issue before the people, he did not reveal his own opinion so that everyone could reveal their opinion with freedom, and the objective was to take an estimate of the people's morale so that the correct state of the army would be known before the war. Abdullah bin Ubayy and his companions insisted on facing from within the city, and the life-risking ones on going out. When he estimated the weak and the courageous through this strategy, he entered the house and came out wearing weapons; this was the expression of the matter that the encounter was to be by going out. The life-risking ones themselves presumed that perhaps the Prophet had adopted this opinion due to their insistence; for this reason, they wanted to withdraw their opinion with an apology. Upon this, the Prophet stated that a prophet does not take off weapons after wearing them. Meaning: now that the resolve has been made, it cannot change. When the hypocrites saw that their point did not prevail, Abdullah bin Ubayy separated with his three hundred companions. It should be clear that the Holy Prophet (PBUH) certainly adopted some wise strategy to estimate the army's morale before setting out for any important war; at the occasion of Badr too, he had adopted this strategy, and at that same occasion, the leader of the Ansar had made that speech which will always be remembered in the history of Islam. 

The Correct Perspective regarding Majority and Minority: From 'wa shawirhum fil-amr' followed by 'fa-idha 'azamta fa-tawakkal 'alallah' (then when you have decided, then rely upon Allah), the hint also emerges that this instruction for consultation in administrative and political matters is for the strengthening and satisfaction of the one in authority; consultation is nonetheless necessary, but after consultation, the matter upon which his heart settles, he should do that task while relying on Allah. After the satisfaction of the one in authority, it does not hold much importance whether the opinion he adopted is of the majority or the minority. Neither is majority in itself proof of correctness and rectitude, nor is minority proof of error. However, it is certain that in the opinion of the majority, there is a dominant presumption of correctness. For this reason, if it is accepted as decisive in settling disputes, this path is safe from the aspect of benefit. Especially in this era when the force of following desires is strong and those who use choice and authority within limits are fewer. 

A Few Points: From the aforementioned two verses, the following points come forward. One, that for people in authority and politics, like general individuals, the preferred way is that of softness and turning a blind eye. It is from this that good-will and trust are produced in individuals, from which the blessings of unity, power, and stability manifest in the collective system. Harshness and strictness are not in its nature but among its accidents. Just as for health the original thing is food but sometimes for the treatment of some disease the need for medicine also arises, similarly in the collective system, the original thing is softness. Harshness must be adopted sometimes under necessity. Second, that consultation (Shura) in the collective system is the manifestation of that good-will and trust which should be between the shepherd and the flock, and the leader and the led. It is from this that the root of despotism and hard-heartedness is cut. And from both the leader and the led, that cooperation manifests which is the foundation of stability. Third, that trust (Tawakkul) is not some excuse for inaction and suspension, nor a pillow for the corner of obscurity, but is the foundation of resolve and action in all activities of individual and collective life. Fourth, that the original power is trust in Allah. The status of means and resources is secondary. Fifth, that trust is a mandatory demand of faith; the person who claims faith in God but does not have reliance upon God—their faith is meaningless.

161 to 163: Correct Meaning of the word 'Ghul': "Ghul yaghullu ghululan" means to commit betrayal, breach of trust, and disloyalty. This word is actually the opposite of the word 'Nush' (sincere advice/well-wishing). Among the lexicographers, Zajjaj has explained 'ma kana li-nabiyyin ay-yaghulla', as the author of Lisan al-Arab clarified, with the words 'ma kana li-nabiyyin ay-yakhuna ummatahu' (it is not for a prophet to betray his nation). The word 'Ghul' which has been used in several places in the Quran is used in the meanings of 'Ghish' (deception), enmity, 'Dhighn' (malice), 'Hiqd' (grudge), and envy ('do not place in our hearts any ghul for those who believe'). No proof has come to my knowledge for specifying this word only with financial betrayal. 

The Hypocrites' Accusation against the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and its Answer: This is the refutation of the accusation the hypocrites placed upon the Holy Prophet (PBUH) after the defeat at Uhud and which they spread well with the intention of producing discouragement among the Muslims. The accusation was: we trusted this person, pledged allegiance at his hand, and made him the master of our good and bad, but he is taking entirely wrong advantage of this trust and is destroying our lives and wealth for his personal ambitions and longings. We had clearly advised that the enemy be faced while staying inside the city, but he did not understand any value or worth of our advice and our brothers' lives and took them to an entirely unsuitable place to be put to the sword; this is explicitly ill-will toward the nation and treachery and disloyalty toward it. 

Hints toward this accusation exist in the previous verses too, and its clarification will follow. The Quran refuted this accusation of theirs: your accusation is a complete lie. No prophet ever commits disloyalty and breach of trust with his nation. Whatever step a prophet takes, he takes in the quest for God's pleasure and under His commands. He is not unaware of the reality that every breach of trust and disloyalty will be presented before God, and the breaker of trust will suffer the full punishment for what they did. The seekers of God's pleasure and those deserving of His wrath and anger will not be equal. Their ranks and resting places will be separate according to their deeds. Allah is watching everyone's deeds. 

This interpretation of the verse is according to the words of the Quran and the structure of the discourse. Some among the interpreters have also adopted this interpretation, as is clear from the Tafseer of Ibn Jarir. For this reason, there is no need to give much importance to the tradition reported in Tafseer books that a cloak had gone missing from the spoils, which the hypocrites accused the Holy Prophet (PBUH) of taking, and this is its refutation. First, this tradition is described regarding the spoils of Badr (because in Uhud there was no question of spoils, as the Muslims were defeated there), and here commentary is occurring on the circumstances of Uhud; in the middle of this, without any clue and without any reference, what was the occasion for mentioning an event of Badr upon which a long time had passed? Then the most important thing to consider is that the hypocrites were not so foolish as to place an accusation upon the Holy Prophet (PBUH) that no one would believe—rather, whoever heard it would laugh at it. Let alone the hypocrites, the state of your most staunch enemies among the Quraysh was such that they never had the audacity to place any accusation of financial betrayal, whether large or small, upon you. In both Islam and the era of ignorance, you remained famous by the title of 'Al-Amin' (the Trustworthy), and the awe of this reputation of yours was upon friends and enemies alike. In financial matters, if some ignorant people ever said something against the Prophet, its nature was not an accusation of betrayal but at most of giving someone more than another. Even on those occasions, after the reality became clear, the accusing party expressed severe remorse. For example, at the occasion of the Conquest of Makkah and the Battle of Hunayn. For this reason, this point seems entirely far-fetched that the hypocrites would accuse you of the betrayal of a humble cloak. However, by saying this, they could plant whispers in the hearts of weak people: that (God forbid) Muhammad (PBUH) is not loyal and well-wishing toward his nation, he is sacrificing his nation for his own ambitions. After the defeat at Uhud, an opportunity had come into their hand for this type of propaganda. Which they took advantage of. Especially because they were against going out of the city for war, but the life-risking Companions and the Prophet (PBUH) did not accept their opinion.

The Blessings of the Prophetic Advent: This verse has already passed in Surah Al-Baqarah with a slight difference. All its important components were explained there. From the aspect of structure, this verse presents the same reality from a positive aspect that was expressed from a negative aspect in the previous verse. In the previous verse, as described, the Prophet (PBUH) was declared innocent of the accusation of ill-will and disloyalty toward the nation; in this verse, the great favor is expressed which Allah Almighty performed upon the entire world and specifically upon the Arab nation in the form of the advent of Muhammad (PBUH). This favor is expressed here from three different aspects. One, from the aspect that Allah Almighty revealed a Messenger among the Arabs from among themselves so that the strangeness of language, the foreignness of lineage, the alienation of inclinations and tendencies, and the ignorance of past and present do not become a cause of any prejudice or suspicion, and people can trust him as their own brother and recognize and hear his voice like the voice of their own conscience. This reality is expressed through the words 'min anfusihim' (from themselves). Second, from the aspect of this Messenger's purpose and mission: that this Messenger recites Allah's verses, purifies you from all rational, moral, and practical misguidances, and is teaching you the Book and Wisdom. After all, can there be any greater well-wisher for you than the one through whose favors and blessings every corner of your individual and collective and manifest and hidden life is thus illuminated? Third, from the aspect of the addressee's need. The Arabs were unlettered people, uninformed of religion and Sharia and unacquainted with Prophethood and Messengership. They had been wandering in the darkness of Kufr and ignorance for a long time. Allah Almighty provided them aid through Muhammad (PBUH) and brought them out of the valleys of misguidance to stand them on the straight path of guidance. This reality is expressed through the words 'wa in kanu min qablu lafi dhalalim-mubin'. Here 'in' is the 'mukhaffafah' (lightened) which comes in the meaning of 'inna' (indeed), and the 'Lam' following it is its clue.

"A" is a particle of interrogation and "wa" is a particle of connection; in the Arabic language, the original place of the particle of interrogation is at the very beginning of the sentence, for example: 'a-fa-bi-hadhal-hadithi antum mud-hinun' (Al-Waqi'ah: 81). This particle of interrogation is for expressing astonishment, and the particle of connection is proof that this point too is one objection among those objections whose answers were given above. 

Removal of a Misconception: It passed above that some people were afflicted with the misconception that whoever is a Messenger of God must be protected from all types of hardships and calamities; when they set out on an expedition, the help of God's angels must be with them; when they face any war, it is mandatory that they be victorious in it. People of this thought naturally received a great shock from the defeat at Uhud; they began thinking that if Islam is a true religion and Muhammad (PBUH) is truly a Messenger of God, then where did this defeat come to them from? The hypocrites tried to take advantage of this psychological state of the weak and began presenting this defeat as an argument against the Holy Prophet (PBUH) before the people. The Quran, in this verse and the following verses, removed this misconception in detail and clarified the wisdom of the trials that face the people of truth.

In this verse, the first point stated was that the fall you faced—this did not face only you such that you make it a reason for suspicion and despair; rather, a double loss has already reached your enemies at your hands. In Badr, you killed 70 of the enemy and took 70 prisoners. In Uhud too, your side was heavier initially and some men of the enemy were killed and wounded at your hands. But later, you suffered defeat due to your own error; Allah is powerful over both victory and defeat, and His power always manifests under His wisdom. The second point stated was that you yourselves were the cause of this fall. Its clarification passed above in verse 152 with the words: 'And Allah had certainly fulfilled His promise to you when you were killing them by His permission until [the time] when you lost courage and fell to disputing about the order [of the Prophet] and disobeyed after He had shown you that which you love. Among you are some who desire this world, and among you are some who desire the Hereafter. Then He turned you back from them that He might test you. And He has already forgiven you, and Allah is the possessor of bounty for the believers.'

The Wisdom of Trials on the Path of Truth: Now the wisdom of this trial (Ibtila) is being clarified: that what has occurred has occurred by God's command, and the purpose of it is that Allah might clearly distinguish the true Muslims and the hypocrites so that every person sees with their own eyes who are the reliable ones within the community and who are not. The objective is that this trial was necessary for communal purification. If the sincere and the hypocrites had remained mixed like this, it is not known at what time the corrupt elements would have ruined the entire community.

'wa qila lahum ta'alaw...' (And it was said to them, "Come..."): this points to a group of hypocrites who were invited to Jihad at this occasion: rise, if war occurs then the reward of Jihad will be obtained, and if the enemy is overawed by our numbers, the objective of defense will be fulfilled. But these people did not rise for Jihad, and to place a veil over their hypocrisy and cowardice, they made the statement: we know that war is not going to happen at this occasion; if we knew of war, we would certainly have accompanied you. Regarding them, the Quran stated that while saying this, they were closer to Kufr than to faith, and they said with their tongues what was not in their hearts. What they were hiding in their hearts is well-known to God.

This revealed the secret of their hearts: that they themselves sat in their houses through word-making, but regarding their kin who participated in Jihad and were martyred, they said: if they had listened to us, they would not have been killed. The objective is: the original thing that prevented them is indeed the fear of death, but they made the statement that they are not rising because there is no apprehension of war.

Finally, it stated: if they are so aware of the occasions of death and life, then let them save themselves from death.

169 to 171: This is a warning to those hypocrites: do not consider those killed in the path of God as dead; the dead are not them but you; they are alive and are enjoying His blessings in the vicinity of their Lord's mercy. You are grieving out of your ignorance and lack of insight that they were killed, and thinking that if they had followed your opinion and sat in their houses like you, they would not have been killed. And their state is that they are rejoicing and happy over the favor and blessing with which Allah has blessed them. You have regret and sorrow over their death, and their state is that moment by moment they are receiving the glad tiding regarding their successors and children who are walking in their footsteps and are desirous of meeting them—even though they haven't met yet—that they too will soon meet them and they too will obtain that station like them where there will be neither any future apprehension nor any past regret. 

Concession for the Believing Successors of Martyrs: It should be noted that this reality is clarified in the Quran through various styles: that alongside high-ranking believers in Paradise, those among their descendants and successors whose end is upon faith will also be gathered, even if they are not of their rank in terms of their deeds. This will be a special favor of Allah Almighty upon the martyrs and the truthful: that for the completion of their joy, their believing progeny will be gathered with them, and for this purpose, the rank of Islam will not be lowered but the rank of the successors will be elevated. We will discuss this issue, God willing, in the exegesis of Surah At-Tur. 

Reflecting on these verses reveals that the Quran has, with extreme eloquence, erased all those bad effects the hypocrites wanted to cast upon the hearts of the Muslims generally and upon the descendants and successors of the martyrs specifically.

'Alladhina' (Those who) is the attribute of those believers mentioned above. The addition of this attribute made the speech perfectly suited to the situation, and a specific context came forward for the principled point made in the previous verse: meaning, those worthy of this great reward will be those whose resolve and faith are such that even after suffering the wound of the defeat at Uhud, no bend was produced in them; rather, as soon as the heralding of a fresh expedition occurred from Allah and the Messenger, they stood up. It is described in books of history and Seerah that after the Muslims' defeat at Uhud, the Quraysh army initially departed; but after reaching the place of Rauha', Abu Sufyan and his companions realized they had committed a severe error in departing so soon—they should have finished the matter of Madinah while they were at it. Thinking this, they began the reorganization of their army anew, and meanwhile, to terrify the Muslims, the rumor was spread through the hypocrites that the Quraysh were making preparations to attack Madinah with new equipment. When the news reached the Prophet, he too ordered the people to prepare for the pursuit of the Quraysh. In this army, permission to join was given only to those who had participated in the previous day's war; this precaution was likely taken so that this army remained pure from the taint of the hypocrites. Consequently, the Prophet (PBUH) set out in pursuit of Abu Sufyan with a group of life-risking companions and went as far as Hamra al-Asad, which is 8 miles from Madinah. When Abu Sufyan saw that no difference had yet occurred in the Muslims' morale, he changed his intention and the Muslims returned successful and victorious.

'lilladhina ahsanu minhum wat-taqaw': here 'Ihsan' means to perform the right of loyalty to Allah and the Messenger in the best possible form, and 'Taqwa' means avoiding all pollutions of hypocrisy. This degree is an extremely high one, and the ranks and degrees of those struggling for it vary according to their internal sincerity and their outward deeds and actions.

Exegesis of Verses 173-174: 'alladhina qala lahumun-nas...' (Those to whom the people said...) describes the aforementioned 'men of excellence' (ashab al-ihsan): when the hypocrites spread the rumor to terrify them that the Quraysh were again preparing for attack with new equipment, this news—instead of producing fear and terror within them—became the cause of increasing their resolve and faith. It is the rule that for a well whose springs are powerful, the more water is taken out from it, the more its springs erupt with even more passion. Similarly, if fire is powerful, even if you throw wet wood into it, it makes that its food too and becomes even more powerful. Such is the state of the people of resolve and faith. Obstacles, instead of weakening them, make them even more resolute and courageous. Every trial serves as a spur for their hidden capabilities, and every test opens a new field of victory for them. (Rakhti hai to meri tab' hoti hai rawan aur). 'wa qalu hasbunallahu wa ni'mal-wakil' (and they said, "Sufficient for us is Allah, and [He is] the best Disposer of affairs"). This is the manifestation of that increase in faith mentioned with the words 'fazadahum imana'. The treasure of the believers' power and strength is in reality this creed of 'Hasbunallah'. The believer's faith is on the point that all power and strength is in the hand of Allah, the One without partner, alone; so when the servant is rising by the command of God Himself to perform a duty appointed by God, how can any power of the world terrify them? The best Being to whom a servant can entrust their matter is the Being of God; so for one who made God their Trustee and Confidant, where does any room for fear and terror remain!

(What grief if the entire creation is an opponent?)
(Sufficient is it if one God is for me).

'innama dhalikumush-shaytanu...' (That is only Satan...): meaning, these threats were all from Satan, and in this way he wanted to establish his and his companions' and friends' awe over you; so do not fear Satan and his companions, but fear only Me, if you are true believers. Obviously, Satan and his allies here refer to the Quraysh and their companions, and the fear that has been prohibited is that fear whose ghost the hypocrites were showing—that out of their fear, the commands and demands of God's religion be put behind the back.

Verses 176 to 179: These verses hold the nature of a digression (Iltifat) toward the Prophet (PBUH) and the Muslims. After the defeat at Uhud—as is clear from the verses above and as we hinted in the introduction to the Surah—those who had infiltrated the ranks of Islam hypocritically either returned to open Kufr or began establishing preambles for returning. These people fell into the vain greed that by supporting the Quraysh, this religion could be defeated. Consequently, all their activities became dedicated to opposing Islam and the Muslims and supporting Kufr and the people of Kufr. Upon this situation, it was stated: there is no need to be distressed by this at all. These activities of theirs in the path of Kufr will not be able to harm Allah and His religion in the slightest. For those who are preferring Kufr over faith, Allah too desires for them that their portion in the Hereafter be only a painful punishment. Despite their hostile activities, the respite Allah has given them—they consider this respite their success, even though it is not their success; rather, Allah Almighty desires that the proof be completed over them and the measure of their sins be thoroughly filled so that when their boat sinks, it is not destined to rise again. After this respite, for them is only a humiliating punishment. Such a punishment after which no one will have any sympathy for them, but there will be only disgrace in the world and the Hereafter.

The Wisdom of the Event of Uhud: 'ma kanallahu liyadharal-mu'minina...' (Allah would not leave the believers...): this points toward the wisdom that was hidden for the believers in this trial of Uhud. It stated: until now, the community of Muslims has consisted of all types of individuals—raw and mature, impure and pure, and sincere and hypocrite. It was against Allah Almighty's wisdom that the community which was going to become the means for the reform and welfare of the entire world remain such a mixture of the righteous and the corrupt. Consequently, He desired that its corrupt elements be separated from it so that the sincere believers emerge and flourish according to their capabilities. For this, one form was that all Muslims be given the knowledge of the unseen (Ghayb) so they could know for themselves who among them is sincere and who is a hypocrite. But it is against Allah's Sunnah that He acquaints everyone with the secrets of the unseen. For news of the unseen, He chooses whom He wills from among His Messengers and informs them of whatever He wills from the matters of the unseen. Its second form was that your community face some such test that sifts and separates your genuine and fake and sincere and hypocrite itself. This form is according to Allah's Sunnah; consequently, in the form of the incident of Uhud, this test came before you. And this test distinguished your genuine and your fake.

'fa-aminu billahi wa rusulihi...' (So believe in Allah and His messengers...): this is an emphasis to benefit fully from the blessings of this test and to continue fulfilling all demands of faith and Taqwa so that after this purification, the disease of hypocrisy does not find a way to enter the community again. Here this verb is in its perfect sense, and at the end is the promise of a great reward.

The Weakness of the Hypocrites in the matter of Spending: Just as the people of hypocrisy are 'thieves' regarding their lives, they are also 'thieves' regarding their wealth; for this reason, their weakness in this was also warned against so that Muslims remain alert toward this disease too. It stated: those who are shrinking from spending their wealth in God's path—whereas it is granted by God alone and God granted it to them not on the basis of any entitlement but purely by His favor—should not think that they are doing some very useful work for the benefit of their future. The wealth being gathered by stealing from God's rights and duties will become a heavy collar around their necks on the Day of Resurrection, and the collars of gold that have today become a means of adornment and pride will be changed into the form of snakes and pythons. 

Furthermore, it stated that the entire inheritance of the heavens and the earth is eventually going to return to God. Whatever anyone received, they received from God, and then all of it is going to return to Him. All these things God has granted us as a trust, and the purpose is our trial. He is well-aware of how we have disposed of the blessings He granted, and according to this knowledge of His, He will give reward or punishment.

181-182: The Hypocrites' Sarcasm regarding the Majesty of Allah, Jalla Shânuhu: 'laqad sami'allahu qawlal-ladhina qalu...' (Allah has certainly heard the statement of those who said...): this is a threat and warning against the mockery the hypocrites used to make in their assemblies regarding the Quran's invitation to spend. When the Quran would say: "Who is it that will lend to Allah a goodly loan?", these hypocrites would quip: "These days Allah Miyan has become very poor and we are wealthier than Him, that He has come down to asking us for a loan." These hypocrites were mostly from the Jews; for this reason, in making sarcasm against God, they also perfectly harmonized with the Jews. The statement of the Jews quoted in Surah Al-Ma'idah—'yadu-llahi maghlulah' (the hand of Allah is chained)—its nature was also, as we will explain, exactly this. They too, to mock the aforementioned invitation to spend in the Quran, would say: "Nowadays the hand of the Muslims' Allah Miyan is very tight that He goes around asking servants for a loan." Upon this, Allah Almighty stated: 'sanaktubu ma qalu' (We will record what they said). Those who recognize the art of eloquence can estimate the wrath and anger hidden within these two words, the expression of which is not possible even in pages from the pens of us helpless ones. Then, even more eloquent is that He conjoined to this 'wa qatlahumul-anbiya'a bi-ghayri haqqin' (and their killing of the prophets unjustly). This crime of killing prophets is obviously the Jews'. Counting a statement of the hypocrites and an action of the Jews in the same category like this and using the same pronoun for both is proof of two things here. One, that by saying this grave thing, these hypocrites have again joined that very brotherhood of the Jews from which they had emerged to stage the drama of entering Islam. Second, that the hypocrites' mockery and the Jews' action are both such grave crimes that God is not one to forget them; rather, He too will one day say to them: 'dhuqu 'adhabal-hariq' (taste the punishment of the Burning Fire). Whatever this punishment of God is, it will be the fruit and result of their own deeds because Allah Almighty is not one to commit any kind of injustice against servants.

When the point reached the mention of the Jews from the mention of the hypocrites, He referenced another mischief of theirs and refuted it while He was at it, and simultaneously consoled the Prophet (PBUH) and the Muslims: you will still have to see and hear many such heart-hurting things from the People of the Book and the polytheists. This is the test of your patience and resolve.

A Mischief of the Jews: The mischief of the Jews referenced is that to shut the Muslims up, they would say: "We have an instruction from Allah Almighty that we should not confirm anyone's claim of Messengership until a miracle is issued from him such that he presents a sacrifice for which fire descends from heaven to consume it as a sign of acceptance." The Jews said this point purely out of mischief. The issuance of this miracle from some prophets is mentioned in the Torah—for example, regarding Prophet Elijah in 1 Kings 18:37-38 and regarding Prophet Solomon in 1 Chronicles 21:26—but it is nowhere mentioned that this miracle is among the requirements and conditions of Prophethood, such that until a prophet shows this miracle, his claim of Prophethood is not even worthy of consideration. Especially regarding the final Prophet, the prophecies they have are entirely free from these types of affectations. This excuse was fabricated by the Jews purely out of mischief, as we hinted; therefore, the Quran answered by keeping their mindset in view: tell them that Messengers have come before me who brought extremely clear signs and also showed that miracle you mentioned, so why did you kill them? This action of yours is bearing witness that you are liars in this statement of yours too. If this miracle is shown to you too, you will still persist in your same stubbornness and find some other excuse for not believing.

Digression to the Prophet (PBUH): Consolation to the Prophet (PBUH): if they are belying you, do not grieve over it; this is neither due to any shortcoming of yours nor because you are not showing them a miracle according to their wish; rather, its sole cause is that these people do not want to believe in you. This matter has not faced you specifically; many Messengers before you were belied even though they brought clear proofs, scriptures, and an illuminating Book; they did not come empty-handed. 

Three words were used here: 'Bayyinat', 'Zubur', and 'Kitab Munir'. 'Bayyinat' means clear and bright. This word is used as an attribute of 'Ayat'. Wherever this word is used alone in the Quran without a noun, it is used in two meanings: in the meaning of clear and silencing arguments, or in the meaning of physical miracles. 'Zubur' is the plural of 'Zabur'. Its meaning is pieces, segments, and scriptures. Its usage for the Psalms of David is well-known. Here it refers to those scriptures of the prophets included in the collection of the Torah. 'Kitab Munir' (Illuminating Book) refers to the Torah; among the things revealed before the Quran, only the Torah can be the original referent of this term.

In 'kullu nafsin dha'iqatul-mawt...' (Every soul shall taste death...), both aspects of threat and tranquility exist. For the hypocrites and opponents, this is a threat; and for the believers, a message of tranquility. The meaning is: you and your opponents all have to pass through the stage of death and eventually come to God; there on the Day of Resurrection, everyone will receive their full reward. There, the game is in reality won by the one who was protected from the Fire and entered Paradise. This world and its luster are merely a mirage-like enjoyment, and included in it are those who lost the Hereafter by pursuing it.

Instruction of Patience and Taqwa to Muslims: 'latublawunna fi amwalikum...' (You will surely be tested in your wealth...): this is an instruction to Muslims to remain firm on patience and Taqwa despite all the activities of the opponents. It stated: you will face trials of life and wealth at the hands of the People of the Book and the polytheists, and you will still have to hear many heart-hurting things from them. This is in reality the test of your patience and Taqwa. If despite these things you remain firm on your stance and observe the respect of Allah's limits, then this is that station of resolve ('Azimat) which is the specific portion of the 'Prophets of Firm Resolve' (Ulul-'Azm) and their life-risking companions, and which is ultimately the key to success on this path.

Final Warning to the People of the Book: This is the final warning to the People of the Book in this Surah. It stated: by referencing self-invented covenants, they are very skillful in opposing the truth, but that original 'Mithaq' (Covenant) which Allah took from them to reveal His Book to everyone and the instruction He gave not to hide anything of it—they put that covenant behind their backs and sacrificed it for the insignificant benefit of the world. Reference to this covenant occurred in both the Torah and Gospel through various styles and forms. For the sake of brevity, we quote only two references. In Deuteronomy: "Therefore shall ye lay up these my words in your heart and in your soul, and bind them for a sign upon your hand, that they may be as frontlets between your eyes. And ye shall teach them your children, speaking of them when thou sittest in thine house, and when thou walkest by the way, when thou liest down, and when thou risest up. And thou shalt write them upon the door posts of thine house, and upon thy gates" (11:18-20). Reflect on these words. The Book whose clarification was covenanted from the Jews in these words—they not only made it a 'bouquet for the shelf of forgetfulness' but also distorted its realities through alteration. 

Similarly, in the Gospels too, this instruction exists in very effective styles, and specifically this phrase is worthy of being written in gold: "What I tell you in darkness, that speak ye in light: and what ye hear in the ear, that preach ye upon the housetops" (Matthew 10:27).

It stated: those whose feats are these—who have so audaciously sold the Sharia for the sake of insignificant worldly interests and are then happy with this feat of theirs, who want to obtain credit for the work they have not done—do not consider them safe from Allah's punishment; they are within the strike of punishment in the world too, and in the Hereafter for them is a painful punishment.

'Mafazah' means a place of salvation and refuge.
