The meaning of 'wa yuhibbuna ay-yuhmadu bi-ma lam yaf'alu' (and love to be praised for what they have not done) is: the responsibility of the Book Allah Almighty placed upon the People of the Book and the covenant He took from them regarding its expression, announcement, teaching, clarification, and implementation—they fulfilled no part of it; rather, on the contrary, they plotted for its concealment and alteration and sold it for a small price for their worldly interests; but despite this, their desire is that they be considered bearers of the Book, be declared the chosen Ummah of God, and be deemed worthy of all God's favor, reward, kindness, and grace in both the world and the Hereafter. The People of the Book have such delicious dreams which the Quran described in Surah Al-Baqarah as 'amaniyy' (false hopes).

'wa lillah mulkus-samawati...' (And to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens...): this is the confirmation and validation of the aforementioned threat. The meaning is: the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth is in God's authority alone; no one else's force or influence has any role in it. Those who are committing rebellion against God are in God's fist every moment, and God is powerful over everything.

Section 39: Subject of Verses 190 to 200:

Conclusion of the Surah: This group of verses holds the status of the conclusion of the Surah, and if you compare this conclusion, you will find it very similar to the conclusion of Surah Al-Baqarah. Especially the prayer in it is the exact mirror of the prayer upon which Surah Al-Baqarah ended.

In this conclusion, first attention was drawn toward that universal reality: as far as the signs of God's power and wisdom are concerned, every corner of the heavens and earth is filled with them. The original thing necessary for one believing in the Prophet's call is not that he presents a burnt offering for which fire descends from heaven to consume it, but that people's ears open to hear his words, people open their eyes to see the wonders of the interventions of power in the heavens and earth, and people use their brains and their reason to reflect on the wisdom and purpose of this machinery of the universe. 

Then it stated: those whose hearts are awake, who remember God while standing, sitting, and lying down, and continue to reflect on the creation of the heavens and earth—they reach the conclusion themselves that this world is not a place of chaos which its Creator made purposelessly and left like an unbridled camel; rather, there is certainly a goal, purpose, and reward and punishment behind it. Consequently, they continue to pray to God to keep them protected from the punishment of the end. 

Further on it stated: such people of awake reason and awake hearts do not remain in search of miracles and wonders. When the call to truth falls upon their ears, the touchstone for testing its truthfulness is within their own reason and their own heart; they test it on that and recognize its value and worth themselves. For them, the Prophet's call and his face are the greatest miracle. After that, an indication was made toward those who were the true referents of these attributes in that era, and their life-risking devotions and sacrifices in the path of this call to truth were mentioned, along with the glad tiding of the great reward with Allah for them. 

Further on, in a few verses, the clarification was made: no misconception should arise from the force the opponents of truth are applying today; the respite they have received was received from Allah alone and it too has benefits. This respite is temporary and will end very soon. The success of the final end is only for God's pious and loyal servants. 

Finally, the group among the People of the Book who remained firm on the truth contrary to the opponents and was blessed with believing in the Prophet (PBUH) was praised; it stated: these people will receive the reward for their steadfastness and truth-worship with Allah. 

At the very end, the Muslims were instructed in extremely brief yet comprehensive words on those points whose observance is necessary for performing the responsibility Allah Almighty placed upon them as the final Ummah. 

Now, in this light, recite the following verses.

190-191: The Gaze of the People of Insight: After mentioning those blinded by the love of the world who were creating the type of hair-splitting arguments for belying the Prophet (PBUH) detailed above, the description of those people of insight is now occurring who remember Allah in every place and every state and continue to reflect on the creation of the earth and heaven. It stated: this remembrance and reflection automatically leads them to the conclusion that this great machinery cannot be without goal and purpose, and when it cannot be without goal and purpose, it is mandatory that it does not end merely at what is manifest; rather, it is necessary that a Day comes in which both the sinner and the doer of good receive the reward for their respective deeds and the great wisdom hidden in the creation of this world manifests.

Philosophy of the Universe: There is only a summary hint here toward the signs in the creation of the heavens and earth and the coming and going of night and day. Their detail is spread throughout the Quran. The Quran has highlighted the signs of the horizons from diverse angles with great clarity, which testify that behind this universe is not only a great power but also great wisdom; not only irresistible power but also infinite kindness and mercy. Not only immeasurable quantity but within this quantity is extremely amazing harmony and balance. This thing shows that the birth of this world is neither an accidental incident nor a player's game, but it is a world made by One who is Competent, Wise, Mighty, Forgiving, All-Hearing, and All-Knowing. For this reason, it is against its nature that it continues like this or ends like this without a distinction between good and evil and right and wrong. If it were so, it would mean that either it has no Creator and Master—it just appeared from somewhere and will continue like this—or that (God forbid) its Creator is of a playful temperament who is watching the show after making someone a beggar, someone wealthy, someone an oppressor, and someone oppressed. Obviously, both these points are entirely contrary to that power and that wisdom whose testimony is being obtained from every corner of this universe. It is entirely contrary to the glory of knowledge and wisdom of such an All-Knowing and Wise Being that He would do any unwise work.

In this way, the person reflecting on the power and wisdom of this universe reaches not only God but also the affirmation of the Hereafter themselves; and one whose mind reaches this reality—obviously their heart will tremble at the concept of reward and punishment and an intense motive will be produced within them to seek refuge from that punishment and that disgrace destined for those who considered this world merely a player's game and thus spent their entire life in complete vanity.

A Few Points: This was the straightforward meaning of these verses. Reflecting further on them reveals a few other points, which are also extremely valuable. One, that according to the Quran, 'Ulul-Albab' (People of Understanding) are only those who, by reflecting on the system of this universe, obtain guidance to the remembrance of God and the thought of the Hereafter. Those who have not achieved this—even if they measure all the distances of the heavens and earth and travel to the moon and Mars—are not 'Ulul-Albab'. They have skulls on their heads but no 'marrow' (brains) inside. If they had marrow, how was it possible that everything was visible to them yet they could not see the mountain hidden behind the molehill? Second, as far as God is concerned, He is not in need of thought and sight. He is the self-evident reality of this universe—in fact, the most self-evident of self-evident truths. Every atom of this universe is heralding Him. Our nature is bearing witness to Him. If a human possesses sound reason, it sees God just as a sound eye sees the sun. To find God, it is enough for a human to remember Him. However, the matter of the Hereafter is in need of reflection and contemplation. Third, as far as the remembrance (Zikr) of Allah is concerned, it is required in every state. For it, there is no restriction of standing or sitting, softness or heat, or morning or evening. Just as the coming and going of breath is necessary for the survival of a human's material life, the remembrance of Allah is necessary for the survival of their spiritual life; and from the verses of the Quran under discussion, the point emerges clearly that the specific attribute of the 'Ulul-Albab' is that they are never heedless of Allah's remembrance. Fourth, in religion, just as remembrance is required, reflection (Fikr) is also required. If there is remembrance but no reflection, then sometimes this remembrance remains merely an occupation of the tongue. It does not open the doors of gnosis. The characteristic of the 'Ulul-Albab's' remembrance is that it is accompanied by reflection, due to which their steps become firm in wisdom and gnosis stage by stage, because it is this reflection that leads toward the certainty of the Hereafter. Fifth, just as through reflection in this universe the 'Ulul-Albab' reach the reality that this universe is not a player's game and therefore a Day of Justice is necessary, similarly the reality also becomes clear to them that on that Day, true disgrace will be faced by those who are relying on false intercessions, because on that Day such wretched ones will have no helper at all.

193 to 194: The Attitude of the 'Ulul-Albab' regarding the Islamic Call: Now the attitude of the aforementioned 'Ulul-Albab' is described: how they deal with Allah's Messenger and his call. It stated: they neither perform hair-splitting arguments nor demand wonders and miracles. The Prophet's call is the voice of their own heart. The God and Hereafter for which he is calling to faith—they hear its testimony from their own interior; for this reason, the Prophet's voice and his face serve as the miracle for them. When they hear his call, instead of opposing it to suppress it, they say 'Labbaik' (here I am) to it and pray to their Lord to forgive their sins, overlook their slips, and make their end with His loyal servants.

'wa tawaffana ma'al-abrar' (and take our souls with the righteous): this is the expression of the desire to be included in the group of God's loyal servants. Meaning: when our end comes, may we be blessed with the companionship of those who remained firm in Your covenant and pledge until the final breath. We discussed the word 'Barr' elsewhere: the original spirit of this word is loyalty, adherence to covenants and treaties, and the performance of rights and duties. Reflecting from this angle, it contains a subtle critique of those People of the Book from whom the covenant of supporting and aiding the final Messenger was taken, but who spent their entire force in his opposition contrary to that covenant. It should be clear that the People of the Book are in view here in the discourse.

The Eloquence of the Prayer: It should also be noted here that this support of the truth from the 'Ulul-Albab' has come forward in the form of a prayer rather than a claim. This is proof that they are well-aware of the responsibilities and difficulties of this 'Amanna' (we believe); therefore, instead of expressing it with pride, they have placed themselves before their Lord with extreme humility and self-abasement: just as He gave the success to say this 'Amanna', similarly may He overlook all previous and future shortcomings and give the success to fulfill the responsibilities and difficulties of this path.

'fastajaba lahum rabbuhum' (So their Lord responded to them): this is the expression of the prayer's acceptance in an extremely eloquent style. As if on one side the words of the prayer emerged from their tongues, and on the other side the certificate of their acceptance was received from the Divine Presence. Regarding the prayers that emerge with the correct passion in the correct place and at the exact time—Allah Almighty's dealing with them is this. We have hinted at this reality in another place too.

Encouragement for the Oppressed and Weak: 'la udhi'u 'amala 'amilim-minkum min dhakarin aw untha' (I will not allow to be lost the work of any worker among you, whether male or female) contains encouragement for all the believers who stood up for the support of the Islamic call being indifferent to dangers in this delicate stage. This stage—as clear from the words 'those who emigrated and were evicted from their homes and were harmed in My path and fought and were killed'—was the stage of migration, Jihad, enduring calamities in God's path, and killing and being killed. In this difficult time, those who entered this field of love by playing the gamble of life included the free and the slave, men and women. And all were presenting sacrifices, each greater than the last, and were targets of hair-raising oppressions at the hands of the opponents of Islam for the crime of faith and Islam. Especially the tortures being inflicted upon the weak—meaning women and slaves—even today make the hair stand on end when heard; but it is the miracle of Islam that all these cruelties of the disbelievers could not succeed in turning even a single person away from Islam; in fact, it is not an exaggeration to say that whoever was weaker proved even more steadfastness and manliness. This situation required that Allah Almighty specifically arrange for the consolation and encouragement of these weak ones in His promise. Consequently, it was stated: 'I will not allow to be lost the work of any worker among you'—whoever among you is doing any service for My religion today, I will not let it go to waste but will give a full reward. After this, the words 'min dhakarin aw untha' (whether male or female) were also added—meaning regardless of whether it is a man or a woman, whoever is enduring any pain for Me today, this promise of Mine includes them all. This segment made the speech perfectly suited to the situation. And who can estimate how much support these two words must have provided to those oppressed women who were targets of various types of oppressions solely for the sake of Islam!

Women and Men are Equal in the Recompense of Deeds: 'ba'dhukum-mim-ba'dh' (you are of one another): in the middle of the speech, entirely incidentally, the proof for this point was stated: why does the action of both man and woman hold exactly the same weight in Allah Almighty's balance? It stated: because woman and man are of the same species. Both are the children of the same Adam and Eve, both are made of the same type of flesh and skin. Through these two words, the Quran refuted all those ignorant theories and wrong religious concepts that designated the woman as a lower creation compared to the man. We are going to discuss this issue in the next Surah; therefore, we suffice with this brief hint here.

Action and Reaction: 'thawabam-min 'indillah' (a reward from Allah): 'thaba, yathubu, thawban' originally means to return and come back. From this comes 'Thawab', which means that fruit and result which a doer obtains as a reaction to their action. Although the word has room for both good and evil, its dominant usage is for the good reaction to a good action. By describing the eternal and never-fading rewards Allah Almighty will bestow upon the humble deeds of servants with the word 'Thawab', the Kind Lord has increased the value and worth of the servants' deeds. Otherwise, what comparison is there between a grain and a mountain? Through the words 'min 'indillahi wallahu 'indahu husnu-th-thawab' (from Allah, and with Allah is the best reward), this distance was removed. Meaning: it is indeed the reward for your own action, but it is from Allah, with whom are the treasures of the best reward. That Giver may give as much as He wills to whom He wills. What lack is there with Him?

In 'la yaghurrannaka' (Let not deceive you), the address is general: the address is to the general Muslims. Address in the singular form like this, as we clarified elsewhere, expresses that every individual of the addressed group is addressed personally. 'Taqallub' means coming and going, movement, and arrival and departure. Depending on the occasion and place, the meanings of pride, stiff-neckedness, and 'strutting around' are also produced within it. In this verse, the context of the speech is proof that it refers to the free and autonomous disposal the disbelievers possessed in the country's circumstances and dealings at that time compared to the Muslims. Until that time, the Muslims were still weak and oppressed. And the disbelievers, in their pride of power, were strutting around everywhere and inflicting oppression upon the weak Muslims.

'mata'un qalil' (a little enjoyment) is the predicate (khabar) of an omitted subject (mubtada'), and we clarified elsewhere that when the subject is omitted like this, the purpose is to focus the entire attention only on the predicate.

'Nuzul' refers to that banquet and hospitality which is presented first upon a guest's arrival. 

This is further support for the subject of the encouragement given to the weak and oppressed Muslims in the previous verses. Addressing the Muslims—specifically the weak and oppressed ones—assurance is being given: no misconception should arise from the dominance and force the disbelievers currently possess in the country. This luster is merely for a few days. After that, their resting place is Hell, and it is a wretched resting place. True and eternal success is only for those who adopt Taqwa and remain firm upon Taqwa. For them, the first offering will be of Paradise, and whatever more exists with their Lord for such loyal servants is each greater than the last.

Praise for the Good People of the Book: Since the preceding verses and this entire Surah as a whole have severely condemned the attitude of the People of the Book, this praise was given at the end for those People of the Book who remained firm on their previous books too and were also honored with the wealth of Islam. This is an extremely subtle hint toward the fact that whatever butter was in this milk has emerged. What remains now is only whey. Regarding them, it stated: these people will find their reward with God which is specific for them. Then He consoled: do not think that there is much delay in receiving this reward. When this reward is received, it will be known that it was received before the sweat dried. 'innallaha sari'ul-hisab' (Indeed, Allah is swift in account) holds this very meaning when it appears in an occasion of consolation.

Fundamental Instructions for Fulfilling the Rights of the Sharia: This is the final verse of this Surah, in which all those fundamental instructions have been gathered as a conclusion which were necessary for fulfilling the rights of the Sharia and for dealing with those circumstances and difficulties in which the Muslims were surrounded. These instructions are for adopting four things and remaining firmly established upon them.

The Reality of Patience (Sabr): The first thing is 'Sabr'. We discussed this word in great detail in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah. The original spirit of this word is to keep oneself anchored upon some truth against resistances. Regardless of whether these resistances arise from within one's own self or attack from the outside, no person can fulfill the right of even the smallest right without solidifying this trait.

The Reality of 'Musabarah': The second thing is 'Musabarah'. 'Musabarah' means demonstrating steadfastness against one's rival and trying to outdo them in this attribute. The emphasis on this thing at this occasion was made specifically because in this stage, a practical armed struggle between the Muslims and their enemies had begun, and in this struggle, the final success was destined for the group that could outdo its rival in the field of stability and manliness. The original reliance of victory and defeat in the battlefield is not on numbers and weapons but on morals and character.

The Reality of 'Murabitah': The third thing is 'Murabitah'. 'Murabitah' is from 'rabt al-khayl' (the tying of horses). Its original initial meaning is to keep war-horses ready for facing the enemy and protecting one's borders. Now tanks and airplanes have taken the place of horses; therefore, with the change of circumstances, the meaning of this word will also change. After the instruction of 'Musabarah', this instruction of 'Murabitah' is the instruction for material preparation alongside moral preparation for facing the enemy.

The Reality of 'Taqwa': The fourth thing is 'Taqwa'. We discussed this word in detail at the start of the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah. Monitoring all the limits and restrictions appointed by God with sincerity and awe is 'Taqwa'. This very thing is the summary and objective of the entire religion.

It stated: O Muslims! Adopt these things so that you may achieve success in both the world and the Hereafter. 

These are the final lines which this worthless one was blessed with the success to write in the exegesis of Al Imran. May Allah Almighty forgive the slips and create room in hearts for the correct points. And our final call is that all praise is due to Allah, the Lord of the worlds.
